comments.recipe_name: stir fried rice noodles with beets and beet greens
comments.comment: You really need to slice the beets thinly--I would recommend using a mandoline. And am I missing something? Where and when are the 2 teaspoons of walnut oil added?

comments.recipe_name: fried eggplant with chickpeas and mint chutney
comments.comment: A little bit of cayenne goes a long way--there are enough other flavors in this dish that I would leave it out if I made it again--I tend to find cayenne too overpowering, but I'm not afraid of heat.

comments.recipe_name: fried eggplant with chickpeas and mint chutney
comments.comment: I found the frying process tedious, even though I'm someone who cooks a lot and enjoys cooking--I make a lot of stir-fries.

I know I'm a wimp, so I knew a full teaspoon of chili flakes would be too overpowering, so I simply left them out. The salad is flavorful enough as it is and can stand on its own without the chili flakes, if they're too much for you. I ate part of it on crackers and the rest of it over brown rice. I roasted two medium-sized globe eggplants in a 400-degree oven for an hour until they collapsed. Charring them provides a rich smoky flavor, but even just roasting is good and even simpler.

comments.recipe_name: swiss chard stalk and tahini dip
comments.comment: The chard stems give this hummus-like dip a wonderfully rich, earthy flavor. I used rainbow chard stems, which gave it a wonderfully rich color as well. Wonderful use for chard stems, although you can always chop up the chard stems and include them in virtually any dish you're cooking--they should never go to waste. A wonderful variant on hummus and wonderful with crackers or crudites.

Since there's no way I can find culantro in the suburbs of Chicago, I substituted cilantro. Also, being a Midwestern wimp, I substituted an entire jalapeno for the habanero, and the result was surprisingly spicy, but not overwhelmingly so. I also used Penzey Spices new Now curry blend. I covered the pot during both cookings of the chickpeas; otherwise, all of the liquid would have evaporated. I've never cooked beans uncovered. I own a Vitamix and the puree was more of a mince.

comments.recipe_name: chickpea tagine with chicken and apricots
comments.comment: Mr. Bittman mentions in the video that you needn't be restricted to dried apricots--dried raisins, any dried fruit, or even almonds will also do.

Unfortunately, I am just a wimp when it comes to spicy food. I've wanted to venture into Korean food, however, after a positive experience at a Korean restaurant. This was my first venture. Bought the gochujang on Amazon. I probably could have done with half the amount. The flavor itself was very interesting, but unfortunately, it's just too spicy for me--not that I don't appreciate flavor, but the heat was just too overwhelming for me.

Don't skip the step of making the stock from the corncobs--this makes all the difference as far as flavor and depth are concerned. I've made vegetable stock for years every summer from the fresh corncobs from the farmstand, and I'll never go back to sodium-laden store-bought stock again--homemade stock is a thousand times more flavorful and healthy and easy to make. This is a great late-summer/early-autumn soup to make with the last of the farmstand produce.

comments.recipe_name: roasted cauliflower salad with watercress walnuts and gruyere
comments.comment: I anticipate this becoming my go-to winter salad and look forward to trying it with broccoli, beets, and winter squash. Substituted arugula for watercress and threw in some dried cranberries. Was fortunate enough to be able to pick up some huge end-of-the-season cauliflower at the farmstand which they were practically giving away, which made the salad even more flavorful.

comments.recipe_name: afghan style pumpkin with yogurt sauce
comments.comment: Mark mentions in his Kitchen Matrix cookbook that you can simply skip the beef to make this a vegetarian recipe. He says that a 4-pound pumpkin will yield about 8 cups of cubes. He recommends cutting the pumpkin just as if you were carving a jack-o'-lantern: cut a circle around the stem, then pull up on the stem and discard it. Using the cavity as a handle, peel the pumpkin with a sturdy vegetable peeler. Then cut the pumpkin in half and scrape out the seeds.

Mark mentions in his Kitchen Matrix cookbook that you can make this recipe vegetarian by substituting more pumpkin for the lamb. He says that a 4-pound pumpkin will yield about 8 cups of cubes. He recommends cutting the pumpkin just as if you were carving a jack-o'-lantern: cut a circle around the stem, then pull up on the stem and discard it. Using the cavity as a handle, peel the pumpkin with a sturdy vegetable peeler. Then cut the pumpkin in half and scrape out the seeds.

Mark says in his Kitchen Matrix cookbook that a 4-pound pumpkin will yield about 8 cups of cubes. He recommends cutting the pumpkin just as if you were carving a jack-o'-lantern: cut a circle around the stem, then pull up on the stem and discard it. Using the cavity as a handle, peel the pumpkin with a sturdy vegetable peeler. Then cut the pumpkin in half and scrape out the seeds.

comments.recipe_name: baked ziti or penne rigate with cauliflower
comments.comment: I'm not a fan of gloppy, overly tomatoey and cheesey pasta, but nevertheless, the 14 ounces of tomatoes called for in this recipe is WAY too skimpy, and I ended up with a bowl full of pasta and cauliflower with a few tomatoes thrown in here and there and a light dusting of Parmesan. The cauliflower would have been more flavorful, I believe, if it had been roasted or parboiled only briefly, as David Tanis calls for in his Cauliflower Al Forno recipe.

comments.recipe_name: baked ziti or penne rigate with cauliflower
comments.comment: I measured all of my ingredients, using precisely the amounts called for in this recipe, but I found the amount way too overwhelming and overflowing for a 2-quart pan--I had to move everything to a 3-quart pan. Sloppy proofing and testing as usual by the NYT. I generally find Martha Rose Shulman's recipes to be very reliable, but something was amiss in the pan size called for here.

comments.recipe_name: stir fried broccoli stalks and flowers red peppers peanuts and tofu
comments.comment: I found this dish to be surprisingly flavorful: I suspect it is the high heat that sears in the flavors. Blotting the tofu thoroughly seems to help as well to make the tofu more flavorful--the drier it is, the better it sears, and the more flavorful it is. Don't skimp on the garlic and ginger, and 1/4 teaspoon of red pepper flakes was just perfect for me--gave it some kick without being overpowering. Skipped the bell pepper as it is out of season.

comments.recipe_name: beet and chickpea salad with anchovy dressing
comments.comment: I don't understand why the recipe calls for diluting the flavor of the anchovies--if you're going to dilute their flavor, why bother? Even though I used farm-fresh beets, cooked the chickpeas from scratch, and used sherry vinegar, I found the flavor wanting--not the flavor boost I was expecting from the anchovies, which normally add a piquant flavor to almost any dish.

comments.recipe_name: beet and chickpea salad with anchovy dressing
comments.comment: Why are the anchovies soaked in milk?

comments.recipe_name: beet and chickpea salad with anchovy dressing
comments.comment: I follow Martha's guidelines for roasting beets in her "The Simple Art of Vegetarian Cooking": I place them in a baking dish or lidded ovenproof casserole, add 1/4- to 1/2-inch of water, cover tightly, and roast at 425 degrees until easily penetrated with the tip of a knife. She recommends 30 to 40 minutes for small beets (3 ounces or less), 40 to 45 minutes for medium beets (4 to 6 ounces), and 50 to 60 minutes for large beets (8 ounces).

The leeks contribute a surprisingly wonderful subtle flavor to the fried rice, different than the standard green spring onions; I sliced mine on a mandoline, and the fact that they were farm leeks I'm sure made them only better. The drizzling of sesame oil and soy sauce at the end were another subtly wonderful flavorful addition--I used Loriva sesame oil and Bragg's aminos, which seem more smoky to me than overly sodiumized soy sauce. I can see this is going to be a go-to recipe.

I consider myself a French onion soup connoisseur, but I also prefer to eat vegetarian: that being said, this soup is AMAZING. The miso is a BRILLIANT idea. Don't be stupid, however, as I was: whisk in the miso BEFORE adding the onions (duh). The thyme is a lovely touch: don't leave it out.

comments.recipe_name: sweet potato and kale salad with roquefort
comments.comment: As David Tanis says regarding the current craze for kale chips, "Do enlighten me." :-) That being said, however, I do love kale and sweet potatoes, not to mention Roquefort cheese, as well as Martha Rose Shulman's recipes in general, so I thought I'd give this a try. I would have used lacinato kale if I had had any on hand, so I used arugula instead. Overcooked my sweet potatoes, however, which regrettably made them mushy. A good dressing and combination of flavors, however.
The texture of the beans and farro is terrific and "meaty."

comments.recipe_name: provencal garlic soup with poached egg
comments.comment: As with all of David Tanis' recipes, this was absolutely exquisite in its simplicity and hit the spot on a 10-degree day as I was getting over a cold or the flu. Gentle, soothing, but flavorful, and far more sophisticated than traditional chicken noodle soup--not to mention that it's more appealing being vegetarian. I'm a huge proponent of and believer in the restorative powers of garlic.

comments.recipe_name: provencal garlic soup with poached egg
comments.comment: What would be the equivalent amount of regular garlic to use? "New-crop garlic" isn't necessarily available year-round. Sixteen cloves just seems a lot.

I recommend pimenton over regular paprika--that would have made this even better, though it was very aromatic & flavorful as it was. I used a rustic whole-grain bread & threw in the crusts with positive results. Used a light chickpea broth but would use richer homemade vegetable stock next time. Wonderful on a cold, rainy, February evening.
Does sweet Spanish paprika mean pimenton?

This recipe makes no sense without the template: "Cut spuds into chunks. Heat 3 tablespoons butter in a deep skillet or broad pot over medium-high heat; add potatoes, 1 diced onion, 1 teaspoon of garlic and a sprig of thyme or rosemary; cook, stirring, until potatoes begin to turn golden, 10 minutes. Add good stock to barely cover the potatoes (about 2 cups). Bring to a boil, reduce heat and simmer, stirring occasionally, until potatoes are tender and liquid is reduced, about 30 mins."

comments.recipe_name: spicy roasted shrimp and broccoli rabe
comments.comment: Well, gee, here's an important detail that is left out of the recipe but is mentioned in the accompanying article: "The one thing to be careful about is trimming the broccoli rabe. Although the thick, fibrous stems can soften when they are cooked in liquid (blanching or braising, for example), they will get stringy if they are roasted. So lop them off entirely. Nice looking leaves, florets and any thin, tender stems are what you want to use here."

comments.recipe_name: spicy roasted shrimp and broccoli rabe
comments.comment: Ten minutes in a 425-degree oven? Are you sure this isn't going to turn the shrimp to rubber?

comments.recipe_name: smoked fish and potato soup with chorizo
comments.comment: We made this for Valentine's Day on a zero-degree day in Illinois, our first cooking date together. Could not have been lovelier or turned out more perfect. We used cod but chorizo supplied enough smoky flavor. Also no piquillo peppers so used regular and were just fine. Lime & cilantro finished it off beautifully & in a very interesting way. Highly recommended. David Tanis never lets me down.

comments.recipe_name: shredded tofu and shiitake stir fry
comments.comment: I was really looking forward to making this, but I was disappointed; I feel as though I did something wrong or I missed some ingredient, because it came out dry and fairly bland-tasting. Maybe I'm spoiled because I just made David Tanis' vegetarian mapo tofu, which was intensely flavored. It's not that I prefer gloppy Chinese food, because I don't, but I just felt that this recipe was lacking and disappointing and that I must have done something wrong.

Absolutely superb. I used a mixture of kohlrabi and scarlet turnips. Since I had kohlrabi greens, I sauteed them with some garlic and chile in olive oil while the soup was simmering and topped/stirred them into each serving of soup. A dusting of ground chipotle chile gave it a wonderfully aromatic and flavorful finish as did the lemon, which brightened it up.

The miso, ginger, and cloves all introduced a very interesting, nuanced and complex flavor; I absolutely loved this, and it smelled so richly divine as it was simmering away, particularly from the wine. I used a rich homemade stock, which I'm sure helped, but if I hadn't had any on hand, I wouldn't have let those shiitake stems to go waste, and I would have made a quick stock out of those, which I'm confident would have contributed an earthy and musky flavor.

comments.recipe_name: sweet and sour eggplant tomatoes and chickpeas
comments.comment: This is a favorite recipe of mine, one that I have made repeatedly every summer ever since the NYT first published it. It introduced me to pomegranate molasses, which I was easily able to find at Whole Foods, and opened up a whole new world to me. I cannot adequately describe the savoriness of this recipe, if that is the right word. It is one of the rare recipes which I truly cannot praise enough. I make it with fresh tomatoes and eggplant from the farmstand, and it truly melts in your mouth.

comments.recipe_name: panzanella with winter squash and sage
comments.comment: I substituted shredded lacinato kale for romaine and tossed it at the same time with the bread cubes, squash, and dressing, since kale is sturdier than romaine. The dressing really makes this salad--interesting combination of sherry and balsamic vinegars. I would recommend using sherry vinegar over red wine vinegar for a far more interesting and nuanced flavor.

comments.recipe_name: radish and herb salad with meyer lemon dressing
comments.comment: How in the world does one slice a radish on a mandoline?

David Tanis never lets me down. The ginger, garlic, & chiles flavor the dish intensely but pleasantly, without overpowering it. I ate this over brown rice. As baby bok choy was not available, just coarsely chopped some regular bok choy. Was skeptical of leaving shiitake whole & did halve or quarter some large ones, but should have trusted that they would indeed shrink. Wonderfully satisfying vegetarian dinner, without leaving me feeling heavy, & went back for seconds!

comments.recipe_name: savory oatmeal with greens and yogurt
comments.comment: David Tanis never lets me down--this was terrific and is a keeper. I used his broccoli rabe recipe from his One Good Dish cookbook and threw in a tablespoon each of teff for added protein with the oatmeal and topping. I already love grains and greens, and I have made his broccoli rabe over and over again, so this is a great new way to eat it, or any green that tops the vegetables I buy--beet greens, kohlrabi greens, radish greens--and radishes themselves, I imagine, would make a great addition.

Used this as a side/topping to a savory steel-cut oatmeal bowl a la Davis Tanis & Mark Bittman. Wisely decided to quarter the bok choy as others recommended & as I have seen done in other recipes. Needed only a minute to finish at end or else sauce/glaze would have burned in the pan.

I was attracted to this recipe because it isn't sugar-laden, but it turned out rather dry. I've made better granolas that are still healthy. I used the minimum nutmeg and cinnamon but wished I had used more.

comments.recipe_name: noodle bowl with mushrooms spinach and salmon
comments.comment: This proved to be an incredibly easy and simple weeknight dinner yet deeply satisfying and flavorful without being overly filling.

comments.recipe_name: stir fried bok choy or sturdy greens
comments.comment: I did not blanch the greens first, as I find doing so necessary only with bitter greens such as broccoli rabe. Used this as part of a savory oatmeal bowl a la David Tanis or Mark Bittman. The greens could have used more ginger or some chile, as I used only water instead of broth or stock.

comments.recipe_name: broccoli panzanella with walnut sauce and basil
comments.comment: As much as I love a summer panzanella drenched in fresh tomato juices & good olive oil & red wine vinegar, it's challenging to make an "autumn" panzanella work. I love roasted broccoli & making good use out of leftover bread, but this salad was just too dry & bready, if you appreciate a true summertime panzanella.
Oh, & because I own a Vitamix, I didn't have to fuss with straining--the soup was just fine blended as it was.

Ridiculously easy to make, wonderfully flavorful & refreshing--don't skip the radish garnish, & it helps to use corn from the farmstand & basil from the garden. I won't add ice cubes next time, however, as they made the soup too watery, & I prefer a thicker soup.

David Tanis never steers me wrong; his recipes are always absolutely superb. This turned out *so* flavorful & aromatic. I used some cod I had picked up that was reduced for quick sale & threw it in with the squash. I always prefer using fresh turmeric, but the dried I used wasn't bad. Used fresh squash from the farmstand. Took a while to prep the ingredients, but then the recipe came together quickly.

Definitely better & more substantial, less thin the second time I made this without ice cubes: the ice cubes are unnecessary. Chilled buttermilk & corn make the soup refreshing enough. Definitely no beed to be so fussy as to strain. Basil infusion absolutely refreshing as well, as was the biting radish garnish.

comments.recipe_name: seared summer squash and egg tacos
comments.comment: Surprisingly flavorful, despite the mild squash, alhough it was from the garden--the searing, chile, & garlic all definitely help--and surprisingly filling. Used good tortillas but would have been even better, I suspect, if I had used better eggs. The longest prep time is dicing the squash, but then it all comes together rather quickly & easily.

comments.recipe_name: farro salad with beets greens and feta
comments.comment: Made this with new, fresh, small beets from the farmstand & brought to a church potluck--great way to introduce beets to those who might otherwise be skeptical. Balsamic-sherry vinaigrette sets off the beets wonderfully. Love farro's nutty flavor & chewy texture. This makes a great portable lunch, too. Always love Martha's healthy & fresh recipes.

comments.recipe_name: cold sesame noodles with crunchy vegetables
comments.comment: This is a favorite recipe I've made dozens of times as a base for fresh, seasonal vegetables. Tonight I added fresh, shredded beets from the farmstand. I've begun omitting the sugar, & I don't miss it; if I had honey, I might use that instead, but it's just fine without it. Even better with homemade tahini, which is simple, easy, & inexpensive to make. Of all the sesame/Chinese noodle recipes on this site, this is my favorite, to which I turn again & again.

Absolutely wonderful on a hot summer evening--tossed with noodles. Three-quarters of a teaspoon of cumin was wonderfully fragrant & flavorful. Used a nice, big, fat clove of garlic for some good garlicky flavor. Wonderfully easy & surprisingly flavorful.
Excellent as usual ... David Tanis never lets me down. Highly recommend making with your own chickpeas & chickpea cooking liquid. Served in pitas with tahini sauce & hard-cooked egg.

Ended up adding feta to make this dish more substantial & interesting as a side salad. Might make a good base for other complimentary vegetables (like what?), but rather blah, unsatisfying, & uninteresting on its own as a salad.

Roasting the eggplant & peppers was intensely flavorful, but grating the tomatoes made the sauce too watery; I kept hoping the sauce would thicken, but instead it just reduced without really thickening, & these were good farmstand tomatoes. I've achieved better results in the past by simply chopping the tomatoes.

I love baby bok choy, & I feel stupid saying this, but I just didn't feel that it worked in this recipe; if I were to make it again, which I probably won't, I would use spinach, inauthentic as that sounds. I quartered the baby bok choy, but it still takes a few minutes to wilt, & the noodles were starting to stick to the pan. Also, I found the sauce too salty from the soy sauce & just not very interesting or complex.

comments.recipe_name: shredded oxtail salad with mustard and shallot
comments.comment: Not worth the effort--now I know why oxtails are on clearance every week. The meat was extremely fatty & difficult to debone. The article will tell you although the recipe does not that this is besr served on crisp winter greens such as frisee or watercress, given the assertive, mustardy dressing, as well as served warm. I will not, however, be making this again.

comments.recipe_name: kale salad with butternut squash cranberries and pepitas
comments.comment: Be advised that a little bit of dressing goes a long way. I would also use half the amount of oil to toast the pepitas. I used delicata squash instead of butternut, which saved on having to peel the squash. Lovely salad and certainly one I would make again, although I would be more sparing about adding the dressing, even though I thought I was being sparing when I did. You can always add more, but once the greens are soaked, you can't blot out extra dressing.

comments.recipe_name: broccoli salad with cheddar and warm bacon vinaigrette
comments.comment: I roasted the broccoli for 15 minutes at 450 degrees, tossing once midway through cooking, because I far prefer roasted vegetables to boiled or steamed. Used some excellent peppered bacon from Whole Foods and served on a bed of arugula and spinach. Excellent winter salad that I will make again.

Very flavorful and easy to make; I made it in a 5-quart Dutch oven, however, rather than a medium saucepan, because I believe a medium saucepan would have been too crowded. Brought to a potluck and was highly praised.

comments.recipe_name: rustic cabbage beef and buckwheat soup
comments.comment: I didn't experience at all the problem that Debra did with her meat, and I found the recipe absolutely superb--really hit the spot on a cold winter night. The pimenton makes all the difference, as it always does--you can hardly go wrong with anything to which pimenton is added. I didn't even use beef stock, but rather, some leftover broth from cooking chickpeas, flavored with pepper and bay leaves. Also used red cabbage. Wonderful winter soup, and I plan on making it again.

comments.recipe_name: chickpea stew with orzo and mustard greens
comments.comment: Good, but I wouldn't say it was excellent, despite using flavorful broth from cooking chickpeas, spicy mustard greens, and fennel as opposed to celery--even garnished with scallions, olive oil, and real Parmesan, as encouraged. Would probably make again as I love chickpeas and greens, but would not dilute with water and would use all four cups of chickpea stock (as it was, I used three).

I love greens, and I found this light, simple soup to be wonderfully satisfying and relatively quick and easy to make, easy for a weeknight and reminiscent of Anna Thomas' green soups. I used leftover stock/broth from cooking chickpeas flavored with bay leaves and peppercorns. I could smell the sweetness of the greens as they cooked, and the egg adds a lovely depth of flavor. I think Americans have a hyper-engineered and hence false sense of what genuinely flavored food really is.

Made this soup again a year later, the following winter: tremendous, but then, I love a good "meaty" bean soup, and I love the texture, chewiness, and nuttiness of farro as well. The Parmesan rind adds depth and creaminess. Used leftover stock/broth from cooking cannellini beans with bay leaves and peppercorns instead of just water. Terrific winter soup.

comments.recipe_name: baked rice with chicken and mushrooms
comments.comment: I'm wondering whether he's serious about a 2-quart Dutch oven.

Lamb bones on clearance prompted my cooking this soup, and oh what a treat it turned out to be on a grey, dreary, overcast winter day. I always use leftover bean cooking stock/broth rather than just plain water to add depth, flavor, and heft. Lovely texture and nuanced flavor; highly recommended.

David Tanis, as usual, nails it. I haven't been inclined to eat pork chops in years. This is such a light, delightful, elegant, and classy way to prepare them, not to mention flavorful. Very easy to prepare. Picked up some thin-cut pork chops on clearance and made a very inexpensive, lovely dinner. David Tanis never lets me down.

David Tanis never lets me down; I love David Tanis, and this recipe is so typical of his comfortingly familiar repertoire, relaxed yet refined. Wonderfully simple and luscious to make on an overcast spring weeknight, and hit the spot without being bloatingly filling and complicated. I used to make a version of this years ago and fill omelettes with it. Highly recommended, and don't skip the sherry.

I similarly ditched the high-fructose "chile sauce" and used Worcestershire sauce and red wine vinegar instead. Took advantage of bison on sale--would not dream of making this with bland turkey, even dark. Deeply flavorful with coffee, dark beer, and cocoa, but should have used a serrano pepper as recommended instead of a jalapeno. I skipped the cayenne & used ancho chile instead, which was a mistake; even I sought more heat. Next time would use chipotle chile powder or pimenton.

comments.recipe_name: warm lentil and smoked pork belly salad
comments.comment: David Tanis never lets me down, and this is so classically David Tanis--absolutely lovely, sophisticated, and elegant, a far cry from bloated American fare. Do use good-quality pork belly or bacon; Hormel simply doesn't cut it and is WAY too salty.

A refreshing switch-up from Mark Bittman's sesame noodles, which I've made countless times. Very easy to make, and I tossed with roasted broccoli from the farmstand. Haven't made these noodles in a while and pleased to return to them. Being the wimp that I am, however, I'll reduce the chili paste to one teaspoon next time; wouldn't leave it out completely, however, as it adds a refreshing kick, even for a Midwestern wimp raised on German meat and potatoes.

David Tanis never lets me down: his recipes are always trustworrhy, and I find myself turning to them again and again. This is a summertime staple made with farmstand bounty. I used Barilla whole-wheat penne and roasted the eggplant. Do use ricotta salata if you can find it: I buy mine from Whole Foods, and it's not expensive.

This is certainly much better and fresher than the gloppy, calorie-laden dish you'll get in a restaurant. It turned out to be an excellent use for the large zucchini my farmstand was practically giving away, and a warm dish on an early fall cool evening. Easy to assemble on a weeknight. If I were to make this again, I would make sure to brown the zucchini so that it doesn't leach water while baking in the casserole, and I would similarly go easy on the tomatoes.
It would be helpful to know what size dish, but as the recipe calls for only a "large" butternut squash, I suppose it varies.

comments.recipe_name: balsamic roasted winter squash and wild rice salad
comments.comment: This was really lovely; I love Martha's recipes and generally trust them. I have half a kabocha squash left and am inclined to make more of this salad with some Forbidden rice I have in the pantry. Chopped up some celery leaves and threw those in, too. Tarragon was a lovely addition, as was the walnut oil, both of which subtly heightened the flavor.

This was truly lovely: David Tanis never lets me down. As usual, simple, elegant, and flavorful. Lovely late summer/early fall meal made with the bountiful abundance of fresh bell peppers from the farmstand, which made all the difference, along with good sausage. The splash of red wine vinegar was a lovely, classy, and flavorful finish and lifted this dish above standard American fare. Easy to make on a weeknight.

comments.recipe_name: hot and sour seared tofu with snap peas
comments.comment: This turned out to be an absolute delight. I made it with zucchini as one of the alternative suggested vegetables, and I plan to make it again with green beans. I highly recommend using Wildwood high-protein sprouted tofu if you can get it; I buy mine from Whole Foods (and it was on sale!), and as it is much firmer, denser, and drier than conventional water-soaked tofu, it sears better than conventional water-soaked tofu.

comments.recipe_name: balsamic roasted winter squash and wild rice salad
comments.comment: Made it this time with black Forbidden rice and fresh, fragrant, crisp kohlrabi from the farnstand instead of celery. Fragrant tarragon and flat-leaf Italian parsley as well as the walnut oil lovely, subtle additions. Absolutely divine. Great lunch to pack for work.

comments.recipe_name: spinach salad with roasted vegetables and spiced chickpeas
comments.comment: Since I had a bunch of Lacinato kale from Whole Foods' $1-a-bunch sale, I shredded a bunch and massaged it with 2 tablespoons of the dressing and let it and the shallots soften and macerate while the vegetables and chickpeas roasted. Purple carrots offered a colorful and healthy contrast. Sweet potatoes from the farmstand and chickpeas made myself from scratch--well worth the effort, and really not much effort. Lovely autumn dinner and flavors with leftovers for lunch during the week.
I have made this recipe several times, and I found 1 tablespoon of oil and 2 tablespoons of za'atar too much for 2 cups of chickpeas: I use only 1 teaspoon of oil and 1 teaspoon of za'atar.

comments.recipe_name: creamy pasta with roasted zucchini almonds and basil
comments.comment: Unfortunately, roasting turned my zucchini to mush rather than searing it. I actually prefer a high percentage of vegetables to pasta, and believe me, the zucchini shrinks considerably in a 500-degree oven. All right, but will not make again.

comments.recipe_name: brown rice and barley salad with sprouted red lentils and green beans
comments.comment: This was really lovely and made a great salad to bring to work: I love Martha's whole-grain salads. I made it with farro rather than brown rice, since that was what I had on hand, as well as the last vegetables from the farmstand: kohlrabi, cucumber, and roasted broccoli. A great template that's very flexible and adaptable with the seasons and what you have on hand, and very satisfying and flavorful.

I look forward to making this soup every year, and I have made it numerous times, as my supermarket always seems to have lamb and beef bones on clearance. I used pinto beans today, as that was what I had on hand, and I always use leftover broth from cooking usually dried chickpeas, flavored only with bay leaves, instead of water to add depth of flavor and nutrition. Do try to use porcini mushrooms if you can find them, as they add a lovely smokiness. Wonderful to smell them simmering.

comments.recipe_name: cumin lamb meatballs with tahini yogurt dipping sauce
comments.comment: This really was very quick, easy, and flavorful to make--a lovely weeknight dinner. Took advantage of some ground lamb that was on clearance at the store, and it really turned into a quick-to-pull-together yet sophisticated weeknight dinner.

Made the soup this year with all scarlet turnips. Definitely add the lemon to brighten it up and chipotle chile powder to give it some smoky depth. I don't totally puree the soup, as I prefer some texture, and if I had to do it over, I would have cut the turnip smaller and pureed only half the soup. I also used all vegetable stock rather than half stock and half water, as mine is homemade and not overly salty: it did not detract from the flavor of the roasted vegetables shining through.

comments.recipe_name: sheet pan spicy roasted broccoli pasta
comments.comment: This was good, but I wouldn't necessarily call it speedy, as it was quite time-consuming to cut 2-1/2 pounds of broccoli into florets.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with kale pesto and roasted butternut squash
comments.comment: I used sweet potatoes instead of butternut squash since Whole Foods had such a beautiful display of them and walnuts instead of pine nuts because they are less expensive. I didn't blanch the kale because I generally like raw kale, but it definitely needed the lemon juice then to cut the bitterness.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with roasted cauliflower and blue cheese
comments.comment: I would hold off on adding the water until after you stir in the cheese: I stupidly added it at the same time as the cheese, so it turned out too watery. Also mind the salt: blue cheese is already salty. Even allowing for the fact that Melissa Clark's recipes tend to call for kosher salt, they still tend to be on the salty side, which obscures the flavor of the roasted vegetables rather than drawing it out.

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: This really was very quick and easy to make. I didn't find high heat to be too high, as I feared it might be, but necessary to sear the sprouts. I used bacon and Barilla whole-wheat penne. The rosemary created a wonderful fragrance. Lemon juice definitely added a bright, citrusy note to balance the sprouts. I ended up grating pecorino over all.

This was a wonderful change-up from regular oatmeal on a cold, raw, rainy morning. I made it with half-milk as suggested, which did indeed result in a creamy texture. I did not find the need to add any sweetener. Don't be stupid as I was, however, and leave the heat on medium-high while attempting to boil the milk, even if it is cold, because it will boil over all of a sudden even with close watching and create a mess.

I make these all the time and have become a sweet potato addict! Whole Foods has a gorgeous display of several varieties, and I keep replenishing my stock. Sweet potatoes are interchangeable with winter squash, and I use sweet potato puree in a number of dishes. This is really too easy to make--all you do is stick it in the oven for an hour, and you can roast them ahead of time. Highly recommended--sweet potatoes have so many varied uses and tasty combinations.

Superb comfort food on a cold, rainy Saturday in April. Used elbow macaroni, a blend of Westminster Royal white cheddar and Emmenthaler cave-aged Gruyere, and smoked paprika. Made topping with homemade bread crumbs, olive oil, and Parmesan. Did not bother using casserole and simply baked in Dutch oven used to make sauce. Superb and much appreciated on this cold April day.
The Gruyere is a lovely, subtle, sophisticated addition: I will never be able to eat the orange or boxed stuff ever again.

comments.recipe_name: sauteed brussels sprouts with sausage and pickled red onion
comments.comment: Bleah ... this just wasn't very exciting. I let the Brussels sprouts cook for only 5 minutes per side because I was impatient, and they weren't very big, but they were still raw. Will not make again. Feel duped.

This is such a simple, healthy, and satisfying lunch and dinner--came home and popped two sweet potatoes in the oven, one for dinner tonight with cottage cheese and one for lunch tomorrow with cottage cheese. Smelled wonderful while it was roasting. Look forward to trying this with goat cheese and feta cheese.
And no need to remove the skin! It's tasty as well as nutritious!

comments.recipe_name: roasted sweet potatoes with smoked paprika
comments.comment: I halved the recipe and topped it with an egg and some feta cheese I had on hand for a Saturday-morning breakfast. Didn't have any foil on hand so roasted the potatoea openly as I do all my vegetables, tossing every 10 minutes, and was done in 20 minutes.

comments.recipe_name: roasted sweet potatoes with smoked paprika
comments.comment: I've started throwing these into a chickpea, lentil, and freekah salad I regularly make from Deborah Madison's "In My Kitchen" cookbook--wonderful addition!
Wonderful--I always love Bittman's dishes--very easy. Threw in some leftovee rice and made fried rice.

comments.recipe_name: roasted sweet potatoes with smoked paprika
comments.comment: I am finding how enjoyable it is to make a batch of these for breakfast hash on the weekend and then to throw the leftovers into a salad for lunch or dinner as the weather grows warmer. If your market has a selection of different varieties of sweet potato, as my Whole Foods does, you can make a pretty rainbow melange, as I did this morning. Also, these are great budget eats, as my Whole Foods haa a reduced bin for sweet potatoes that are beginning to grow old and dry out.

I had not made this in a while, as I've been making Milk Street's version, but as I ran out of miso paste, I switched back to this. I used all sesame paste rather than sesame-peanut, and as I didn't have any ginger on hand, I used a teaspoon of dried. I was afraid 2 teaspoons of chili paste would be too much for me, but it turned out to be fine. Easy to make with leftovers for lunch, and a refreshing change-up from the Milk Street version.

I don't know where or how Sifton comes up with 20 minutes--maybe if you have a NYT staff to chop and prep vegetables for you, but not in real life. Mark Bittman's fried rice is much faster. The recipe says the eggs are supposed to be beaten but then says to break the eggs into the rice. Nevertheless, it waa very tasty and indeed a great way to use up veggies.

comments.recipe_name: summer pasta with zucchini ricotta and basil
comments.comment: This was divine: David Tanis never lets me down. Made this with the first zucchini of the season. Look forward to making it again with more zucchini to come. The lemon adds a bright touch. Nice, mild, not-heavy pasta dish for a summer day--mild, but not at all bland, definitely not with the lemon and basil. Very fresh and very simple to make, so typical of Tanis--simple, yet elegant and sophisticated.

comments.recipe_name: summer squash tahini and yogurt dip
comments.comment: Well, it may sound romantic and traditional to pound the squash to a paste in a mortar and pestle, but I have no comprehension how she achieved this result, as mine kept sliding all over the place. I finally enlisted a whisk in an attempt to mash it--very frustrating, and the quantity waa too small to blitz in a food processor. I normally appreciate using a mortar and pestle and am not a big fan of my food processor. Would not make again--too frustrating and too much of a hassle.

Wonderfully refreshing, cool, fragrant, and flavorful on a hot summer day, but I question the amount of salt, and I should have known--Melissa Clark's recipes usuallu call for kosher salt, which is half the amount of sea salt, and her recipes are usually oversalted as it is.
And I meant to add that I use a Vitamix, so there's no need to strain.
I did not have any honey or almond extract, but it was just fine without.

I don't have a grill, so I used Martha Rose Shulman's method: heat the over to 450, line a baking sheet with foil, and oil with olive oil. Halve eggplant lengthwise and score down the middle of the cut side, cutting down to but not through the skin. Lay the eggplant cut-side down and roast for 20 minutes. Didn't have fresh oregano so used 1/4 tsp dried, but could have used fresh mint just as well. Delicious! Had smoky flavor even though roasted. Easy to make!

I roasted my eggplant--much easier and healthier with no loss of flavor, especially when you use fresh eggplant and ripe tomatoes from the farmstand and basil picked from the garden. David Tanis never steers me wrong, and his recipes are always a delight to make, simple yet sophisticated and elegant. A summertime delight.

comments.recipe_name: orecchiette with cherry tomatoes and arugula
comments.comment: Really lovely and simple and very flavorful, especially with cherry tomatoes from the farmstand--I wouldn't want to make this in the winter with strip-mined tomatoes.
This was eeally a lovely and flavorful summertime pasta. I didn't gave oriecchete, so I used medium shells to cup the loose corn kernels. Really lovely and light.

Made this again with some whole-grain rotini I had,on hand--lovely--added a real earthiness. But be warned: Melissa Clark oversalts everything--I don't know what her obsession with salt is. The 1/4 teaspoon at the beginning is plenty.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with seared zucchini and ricotta salata
comments.comment: A really lovely, light, inexpensive, simple, yet flavorful summer pasta, perfect for the end of a hot summer day as it's beginning to cool down and shadows are lengthening. A squeeze of lemon over the finished dish added a bright, citrusy touch. Used whole-grain penne, but there was no way I was going to add 1/2 teaspoon of salt: what is it with Melissa Clark and salt? She drowns everything in salt--1/4 teaspoon was plenty.

comments.recipe_name: orecchiette with cherry tomatoes and arugula
comments.comment: I didn't have orecchiette, so I used penne, and it was just fine. Minced the garlic and cooked it along with the tomatoes because I was impatient, and it worked out just fine.

comments.recipe_name: seared summer squash and egg tacos
comments.comment: I hadn't made this in a while, so it was good to revisit with a huge 2-pound zucchini that I had. Light but flavorful and filling meal without being too heavy, and easy to make.

This was really wonderful--cool, refreshing, easy, satisfying, and flavorful on a hot summer day. I can see why the author loves the dressing so much, and I'll remember it in tne future--hope to use it more. I haven't made green goddess dressing in a long time, but whatever recipe I made before wasn't as good as this one.

The author adds this note in her cookbook: "I am not sure I want to live in a world where ranch dressing is more beloved than Green Goddess, which is superior in both name and taste, so I'm doing everything in my power to bring this vibrant, herb-packed green dressing into the limelight. Green Goddess is a kitchen MVP. It can be used as a salad dressing, yes, but it's also a dip for crudites, a marinade for grilled chicken, and it's great on pizza or with fries, too."

I love this recipe and look forward to it every summer when the farmstand is overflowing with green beans. And I love David Tanis' recipes--always reliable and simple yet sophisticated and elegant. This is my favorite way to make salmon, especially wild salmon--as he says, and unlike so many other recipes, it doesn't mask or smother the salmon, and if you get good salmon, you want it to shine, as it does here.

comments.recipe_name: creamy pasta with smoked bacon and peas
comments.comment: This was a simple, inexpensive, satisfying dinner, and I can see it as an easily adaptable template to use with other vegetables such as corn, even zucchini when in season.

Even though Martha understandably describes this as a winter salad, I look foeward to making it every summer when beets appear at the farmstand. I didn't even recook it in a skillet--just tossed it all together. Didn't have nuts, but I have made it in the past with walnuts.
Very simple, inexpensive, yet flavorful summertime pasta.

Because I am Midwestern German wimp, I did not add the full 4 teaspoons of chile flakes, but I added enough to make it flavorful and interesting. This was a simple yet decadent late-night dinner, a kind of sophisticated version of noodles and butter, and oh so comforting--how could it not be with all that oil?

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: This is so easy to make, especially if you have a food processor to shred the sprouts, and I far prefer shredded sprouts to big chunks. I look forward to making this recipe every time sprouts become available.

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: I didn't have penne on hand, but spaghetti worked just fine.

comments.recipe_name: spaghetti with romano beans black pepper and goat cheese
comments.comment: This is SO SIMPLE AND EASY to make, and surprisingly flavorful, too. It's even easier if you skip the ice-water step, trim the beans ahead of time, start the pasta, and then add the beans. I used to be able to get some really interesting and flavorful fresh, local goat cheeses at Whole Foods, but no more with Amazon and since it's become Whole Liquor; even still, with Whole Foods-brand goat cheese, it was still pleasurable.

comments.recipe_name: roasted sweet potatoes with smoked paprika
comments.comment: Topped with a fried egg for breakfast on a rainy Saturday morning--delicious. I'm not normally a fan of Sam Sifton's recipes, but this one is the bomb. Smoked paprika makes everything taste better--learned that from Mark Bittman years ago. Didn't have an onion, but it was just fine without it.

Lovely and simple, nourishing lunch on a rainy afternoon--I absentmindedly forgot the herbs, but it was fine without it. You don't even need the skillet, though I can imagine that would be warming in the winter or if you made the beets and farro ahead of time. As an afterthought, I added some arugula to add some green. Wonderfully simple, flavorful salad--I always love farro.

comments.recipe_name: marinated zucchini with farro chickpeas and parmesan
comments.comment: I found this salad very satisfying. Roasted the zucchini a la Martha Rose Shulman's Zucchini Parmesan recipe--roasting, rather than frying the zucchini, allows you to cut down on olive oil and time (I do the same with eggplant, also a la Martha), with no sacrifice of flavor. Farro cooking with chickpeas smelled divine and would have been even better if I had made my own chickpeas from scratch, as I usually do. I don't know what people were expecting for flavor--sriracha?
I found that the dried fruit sweetened the oatmeal enough for my taste. This was very satisfying on a still-warm September morning, and very easy to prepare.

As expected, an always-reliable recipe from David Tanis--delicious and simple, despite the fact that I screwed up and grabbed mirin rather than rice-wine vinegar from my pantry. Even despite being mirin-heavy, which was rather interesting, nevertheless, it was still delicious--a wonderful dressing, one I'll have to use elsewhere, and I look forward to tasting what it's *supposed* to taste like. A very forgiving recipe.

comments.recipe_name: blond puttanesca linguine with tuna arugula and capers
comments.comment: This was a very simple, flavorful, and aromatic weeknight dinner. One challenge is always the arugula clumping. I did not need the final 1/2 or 1/4 cuo of water. I always chop capers coarsely to break them up, a technique I learned from David Tanis, which I have grown to appreciare. An interesting dish.

A comforting, warming dish on a chill, overcast autumn day. As I do not find kale bitter, I did not bother blanching it first, but the unblanched kale may have leached more water into my dish, but it was fine nevertheless. Did not have herbes de Provence on hand, but was fine without it.

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: Lovely, warming dinner on an early fall weeknight when it was cool, dark, and overcast--always a welcome pleasure to make when Brussels sprouts are available and in season.

comments.recipe_name: spicy spaghetti with caramelized onions and herbs
comments.comment: This was an easy and inexpensive, deeply flavorful, warming weeknight dinner on a cool and dark autumn evening. Great suggestions in the notes--I used arugula.

comments.recipe_name: farro salad with tomatoes and romano beans
comments.comment: A really lovely, simple summer salad, alhough I was thrilled to discover Romano beans still at the farmstand in early October. I love farro, and adding the dressing to the warm farro was wonderfully fragrant, although I should have known not to use it all. Used sherry vinegar, which I recommend, and also topped with feta, as others did. Martha's recipes are always reliable, simple, and dependable.

I love sweet potatoes, and you can hardly go wrong with sweet potatoes--but this recipe does. I hardly ever cook with cornstarch anymore. I far prefer Sifton's roasted sweet potatoes w/smoked paprika or Bittman's stir-fried sweet potatoes, and both are easy to make. You need to have a well-seasoned or really good nonstick pan to make this. I appreciate the flavor of vegetables shining through, but cornstarch added a yuck flavor. It wasn't inedible, but I won't make it again.

I baked my loaf for the entire time, and I thought that my toothpick came out clean, but nevertheless, the loaf was a little moist. Nevertheless, the flavor was SUPERB, not to mention the aroma of it baking on a cool and dark autumn evening. The lemon zest finishes it off nicely, and the cinnamon and nutmeg are wonderful. The yogurt and olive oil make this cake luciously moist and flavorful. I'm usually not a fan of zucchini and banana bread, but this recipe is superb.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with anchovies garlic chiles and kale
comments.comment: I hadn't made this in a while; it was an easy and flavorful weeknight autumn dish. I love fresh greens, and this is an easy way to incorporate them into an easy, satisfying meal.

comments.recipe_name: pasta aglio olio with butternut squash
comments.comment: I didn't have any nuts, unfortunately, but I did add greens, as another reader suggested--always a good idea. I used sweet potatoes rather than squash. A comforting, flavorful, satisfying weeknight dinner on a cool, dark autumn evening.

Emily Weinstein added this helpful note in her newsletter: "This is a great recipe as written if you have extra time, but you could use 4 to 8 skin-on, bone-in thighs instead, arranging them skin side up on a sheet pan. Scatter the herbs around, or skip them altogether; I also like to brush the skin with olive oil. Keep the same oven temp, and they should be done in 35 minutes."

comments.recipe_name: midnight pasta with garlic anchovy capers and red pepper
comments.comment: I was tired, and this was a simple, flavorful, satisfying, yet sophisticated dinner on a weeknight evening. David Tanis never lets me down: his recipes are always reliable, unfussy, and yet mature and sophisticated

It was good to revisit this recipe now that the cold weather has set in again, on a cold, rainy weekend morning. I found it immensely more satisfying, not to mention healthy and economical, than going out to eat, and the aroma while it simmered was consoling. I found no need to add sweetener even though I used unsweetened cocoa.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with mushrooms fried eggs and herbs
comments.comment: What a cheering, comforting, simple, enjoyable meal this was on a cold, wet, dark November evening! I have really come to like Colu Henry and her unfussy recipes and to look forward to new recipes from her, and I look forward to trying recipes from her cookbook. Highly recommended, along with anything else by her! This was a pleasure to make.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with mushrooms fried eggs and herbs
comments.comment: Anyone with a smiling dog on the back page of her cookbook is okay by me. :)
What in the world is 60-percent chocolate? I've never seen such a thing.

comments.recipe_name: olive oil braised chickpeas and broccoli rabe
comments.comment: Two of my favorite ingredients! This was wonderful, and so easy and warming on a cold, wet November evening, and filled my home with its wonderful aroma. I used my own chickpeas. I kick myself for forgetting to pick up some bread, so I served it over some leftover polenta, but the stems do require a little slicing, so next time, I'll serve it on its own with bread or over pasta. When is Ali Slagle going to come out with a cookbook? I have really come to like her recipes.

comments.recipe_name: olive oil braised chickpeas and broccoli rabe
comments.comment: This time I cracked/crushed the fennel in a mortar and pestle, as David Tanis suggests for his broccoli rabe in "One Good Dish." It may seem like a small, inconsequential step, but I think it's like smashing the garlic as opposed simply to throw in whole, peeled cloves: crushing/cracking the fennel released its aroma and flavor, and I found it more pleasant to eat than whole seeds, even though they're small. I add only 1/4 teaspoon of chile so as not to overwhelm the dish.

This was such an easy, warming dinner on a cold, November Friday evening when we had our first snow; I'm so grateful that Emily Weinstein reminded me of it in her newsletter. I used my own chickpeas, and oh what a difference that made--none of that tinny, metallic taste.

comments.recipe_name: pumpkin bread with brown butter and bourbon
comments.comment: I used apple cider because it was what I had on hand and was less expenaive, but I'm sure bourbon would have been very good and flavorful. The browned butter smelled wonderful, but then, I used European butter. Used my own pumpkin because it was more straightforward and trustworthy--who needs all those extra ingredients? Buttered the pan, and it worked fine. Used the entire 60 minutes. Smelled wonderful while baking. Comforting on a cold November day. Nice to have an extra loaf to share.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker lentil soup with sausage and greens
comments.comment: I had hoped to use black lentils, as I've cooked with them before, but since Whole Foods has been taken over by Amazon, they're no longer available in bulk at my store as they used to be, and I did not think to look in the packaged-food aisle, so I used green, which were fine. Found a wonderful Kentucky bourbon sausage. Used my own stock from cooking beans and added high-calcium turnip greens; the soup was hearty enough to stand up to them. Welcome dinner on a cold November day.

comments.recipe_name: pumpkin bread with chocolate chip streusel
comments.comment: It turned out okay, but I have my doubts about the 1/2 tbsp of butter for the streusel after someone suggested that it should be 1/2 cup--that's a heck of a difference as well as editorial oversight. I used my own pumpkin, as I rarely if ever use canned.

Wow--who would ever think that "cauliflower stew" could be so flavorful, and even more so, *naturally* flavorful, without a ton of spices and artificial flavors poured in? That said, my firat mouthful left me amazed by the depth of the flavor of the mustard and cardamom without being overwhelming or artificial. Used carrots instead of parsnips as someone suggested, but I bet parsnips would have been even more intensely flavorful. Used beautiful purple cauliflower and potatoes.

comments.recipe_name: olive oil braised chickpeas and broccoli rabe
comments.comment: Since I forgot to pick up bread, and my Dutch oven was being used to braise the broccoli rabe, so I couldn't make pasta, I tried her suggestion of spooning the juices over mozzarella--an interesting and flavorful idea. Wonderfully warming and consoling dish on a cold and dark November evening. Once again used my own chickpeas, which are superior to anything canned and tinny-tasting, even if it is braised.

OH - MY - GOODNESS--this was comfort food at its best, as the wind howled outside, the geese clamoured, sleet pelted my windowpane, and a blizzard blew in from the Plains at the end of the Thanksgiving weekend. I steamed my pudding using the slow-cooker method--the aroma was wonderful as it cooled. Rich in coffee/espresso flavor and surprisingly, not disgustingly, overly sweet. Highly recommended. Delightful to anticipate all afternoon while it steamed and poured cold rain outside.
This was a hit--so flavorful, and so pretty.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker curried sweet potato soup with coconut and kale
comments.comment: Wonderfully warming stew on a cold, rainy December day. Every winter I intend to use my crockpot more but never do, my Dutch oven being the heavy worker in my kitchen, so I sure appreciate these recipes. I pureed only a couple of cups to thicken the stew as I prefer a chunky stew with some texture. Was afraid the curry paste would make the stew too overwhelmingly hot, but the coconut milk tempered and mellowed it. Threw in a couple of cups of chickpeas and used tahini rather than peanut butter.
Yummy but messy--I must be challenged--because my slabs fell apart, and the coating slid off of them--disappointing.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with chickpeas chorizo and bread crumbs
comments.comment: I used my own chickpeas, which helped immensely, and shells to cup the chickpeas. I should have added some greens, as I had intended to do. All right, but nothing stellar--total bachelor food.

comments.recipe_name: orecchiette with brussels sprouts and bratwurst
comments.comment: I loved this! It really connected with my inner German! Surprisingly not overly heavy. I used some lovely small purple Brussels sprouts. Sour cream definitely adds flavor--reminds me of my Austrian grandfather's use of sour cream.

I found this wonderful cake to be so deeply flavorful enough on its own that it did not require superfluous or distracting turbinado or powdered sugar or ice cream. Wonderfully warming on an overcast December day, both with its flavor as well as its aroma.

Turned out perfect--David Tanis never lets me down. "Winter's balm" indeed on a cold, overcast day at the end of the year. Made a perfect bed for Ali Slagle's Olive Oil-Braised Chickpeas and Broccoli Rabe.

comments.recipe_name: creamy pasta with bacon and red cabbage
comments.comment: Another dish that connected with my German roots. I love red cabbage. Very warming and cheering with its bright purple color at the end of a cold, gray, December day at the end of the year. Very economical, simple, and unfussy. I have come to love Colu Henry's recipes, and I always look forward to a new one from her--always unfussy and reliable but nevertheless interesting and never boring or dull.

comments.recipe_name: roasted squash with turmeric ginger chickpeas
comments.comment: The recipe should have called for stirring the squash halfway through, because mine turned black on the bottom. I'm not a fan of dried turmeric, as I find that it has a tinny, metallic taste. Uaed my own chickpeas, but not even that helped--I would not make this again. I had high expectations but was disappointed.

comments.recipe_name: lentils with chorizo greens and yellow rice
comments.comment: David Tanis never lets me down--this was absolutely superb. Made it for a quiet New Year's Eve--humble, yes, but at the same time rich in flavor and a pleasure to eat on a cold, rainy night. Two of my favorite ingredients--chorizo and pimenton. And the rice is absolutely to die for--I will definitely make the rice again. Highly recommended and really very easy to make.

comments.recipe_name: lentils with chorizo greens and yellow rice
comments.comment: Since I can't edit my comments, I would just like to reiterate that the rice is really out of this world, not to mention the wonderfully Spanish-flavored stew, both of which take mere, humble rice and lentils to another plane. I wish I could have eaten more! True comfort food without the fat and calories of greasy pizza and the like. I don't know that I can ever make plain basmati rice ever again, although sometimes it's more appropriate. This recipe just takes white rice to a new level.

Turned out well enough with a relatively rich, dark color, but frankly, my vegetable stock from Deborah Madison's Vegetarian Cooking for Everyone turns out richer, darker, and more flavorful due to caramelizing vegetables over high heat for 5-10 minutes. But a good use of a turkey carcass without letting it go to waste.

comments.recipe_name: orecchiette with brussels sprouts and bratwurst
comments.comment: Made again with Kentucky Bourbon sausage--out of this world!

Deceptively flavorful! I used olive oil rather than canola oil and usually use honey rather than agave, but had none, and only 1/4 cup maple syrup which I made up with water before I realized I could have just used more molasses. Still turned out moist and flavorful--no need for any spread--and smelled wonderful while baking--a treat on a cold, gray, January Saturday morning. Martha's recipes are always reliable and a pleasure.

I hadn't used my food processor much except for shredding; I tried making cauliflower rice once in my beloved Vitamix, but it is too conical. This is a great recipe, and I've been wanting to make cauliflower rice for a long time. Beware of overprocessing, or you will have millet! And you definitely can't fill the cannister more than halfway. Easy, and much appreciated! I love Ali Slagle's recipes!

I was thrilled to see this recipe appear because I was inspired by Emily Weinstein's suggestion to use cauliflower as a substitute for ground meat, and so I am using cauliflower in lieu of ground meat in another recipe, tossing the cauliflower with olive oil and garlic as if I were going to roast it to provide it with some "fat" and flavor.

I used my own black beans, bean broth instead of water, store-bought chorizo, and a yellow onion. Very warming and satisfying on a January day which did not get out of the teens, and very easy to make: most of the time involved simmering on the stove.

comments.recipe_name: cheese grits with saucy black beans avocado and radish
comments.comment: Very easy and very warming as the temperature plunged into the single-digits. Used my own beans and stock and skipped the cayenne, although in hindsight, cumin or smoked paprika might have been good; greens would also have been a good addition in hindsight (greens are always a good addition). Butter and cheese make this very luscious: I used a Scottish cheddar.

I love Ali Slagle's recipes! I made the vegetarian version with smoked paprika, which has been my friend ever since Mark Bittman introduced me to it years ago on these pages. Its aroma filled my home as the soup was simmering! Wonderful on a cold January day when the temperature did not break out of the teens.
I meant to add that adding the split peas in two stages does indeed give the soup a better texture, I believe, rather than just mush, which is why I haven'f made split-pea soup in a long time.

comments.recipe_name: cheese grits with saucy black beans avocado and radish
comments.comment: Swapped smoked paprika for the cayenne: much more subtle and flavorful. You don't need to smother this dish in hot sauce and cayenne to make it edible--allow the natural flavors to come through.

Made this with green lentils, homemade turkey stock, thyme, two cloves of garlic, and sherry vinegar and topped with Greek yogurt and red cabbage on a day that began at 8 below and was up to 10 by lunch. Easy and budget-friendly indeed, and I love the list of suggested toppings! I can definitely see myself making this again.
Meant to add that I did 't bother pureeing it--even easier, and I prefer the texture.

comments.recipe_name: olive oil braised chickpeas and broccoli rabe
comments.comment: This has become one of my favorite dishes--I make it every chance I get, whenever I can snatch up broccoli rabe from the grocery store. Snowing tonight and 10 degrees, and this is pure comfort food.

Eating leftovers for lunch the next day, and it's even better! Of course, it probably helps that I used my own turkey stock, but nevertheless, this is a terrific, warming, and yet simple soup, and super-easy to make--and very versatile! Amazing how much you can do with simple, humble, inexpensive ingredients!
If you use your own chickpeas, how much liquid needs to be added?

This was very quick and easy to make. Used my own chickpeas and 2-1/2 cups of their cooking liquid; perhaps could have used 3. Used only 1 tbsp of harissa and wasn't overwhelming--perhaps could have used 2 and didn't need any honey--will perhaps stir another tablespoon into the rest. Meant to make a soft-boiled egg but forgot and was distracted. Warming and flavorful on an overcast wintry day.

comments.recipe_name: sausage and peppers pasta with broccoli
comments.comment: I used broccoli rabe, because I love broccoli rabe, and I would have used chorizo, but I discovered some Italian sausage on clearance. I added only 1/4 tsp of red pepper flakes to the broccoli rabe while I sauteed it, and I didn't end up adding any pasta water to the finished pasta, as I found the moisture from the broccoli rabe and peppers sufficient. This was very easy to put or pull together and was satisfying. Grateful for the author's suggestions for variation--I always appreciate those.

I served this over polenta, which isn't traditional, but it's what I happened to have. Mine turned out rather soupy, likely because my store, much to my disappointment, was out of coconut milk, so I had to use "lite" coconut milk, which is like skim milk, which is to say, water. I also didn't realize I was low on garam masala so was able to use only 2 teaspoons. Let mine cook for 5:hours 15 minutes, and the meat was indeed buttery. Still flavorful. Made chickpeas this am and will add.
I've been making this with five cups of water but made it today with only four cups, as I cook it with the lid on, and very pleased with the consistency.

I don't normally make eggplant in the winter, but my grocery had it on special for Chinese New Year. Roasted the eggplant rather than fry it a la Martha Rose Shulman, which I always do, and used my own chickpeas. Had only 1 tsp of sumac and fire-roasted tomatoes. Served over polenta. Very similar to a Martha Rose Shulman recipe I've loved for years. Very easy and tasty, especially if you roast the eggplant.

comments.recipe_name: butternut squash polenta with sausage and onion
comments.comment: I should have added that the caramelized onions add a wonderful sweetness to the dish.

comments.recipe_name: butternut squash polenta with sausage and onion
comments.comment: I used sweet potato and cracked the fennel seeds in a mortar and pestle a la David Tanis. This is a great suggestion for cooking polenta in general in the future and adding some veggies, nutrition, and flavor.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with chickpeas and a negroni
comments.comment: I didn't make the Negroni, but I did make the pasta. Used my own chickpeas, which I've made for years a la Mark Bittman. Threw in an entire box of pasta because I hate all those little leftovers, but I probably should have used only 12 oz. The quality of the tomatoes makes all the difference. All right in a pinch, but there are better recipes out there--I made one from Milk Street that was fabulous and not that much more involved.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with sausage squash and sage brown butter
comments.comment: Used penna and sweet potatoes, but I suspect orecchiette would have worked better. Came together relatively easily, but precooked sausage would have been even quicker. Wonderful combination of flavors--you really can't go wrong with ingredients such as this.

comments.recipe_name: quick braised greens and beans with bacon
comments.comment: I used lacinato kale, fresh sage, and aduki beans, because I prefer their size, and served this over polenta. I could also imagine this served over a hearty toast. Used my own turkey stock. I always love beans and greens--good wintertime meal. I wouldn't call this a 30-minute dish, however--it takes time to destem the kale, much as I love lacinato kale.

Chopped up some broccoli rabe and added that to the second half of the pasta cooking time. Used chicken sausage and orecchiette. Very easy to pull together on a Friday evening, and you can hardly go wrong with ingredients such as these.

Very easy to pull together, but I would not call this "insanely flavorful"--granted, I used beef, simply because it was on sale and more economical, but I question whether even lamb would be "insanely flavorful," which is, as usual, typically hyperbolic of Roman. Added wine and fennel and served over polenta. The quality of your tomatoes would need to be exceedingly high to make this dish "insanely flavorful"--two anchovies aren't much to make much of a difference--a rather half-hearted gesture.
By all means, do NOT water-down the sauce: I used only enough water to loosen up the tomato bits in the can.

I wish now that I had thrown in the entire TIN of anchovies: THAT would have made this "insanely flavorful." I do that with broccoli rabe, and as anyone who has cooked with anchovies knows, doing so doesn't in the least make the dish anchovyish, but it does lend a great deal of "insane flavor."

I will not make again: this is nothing more than a simple, basic meat sauce, the same kind of thing my mother used to make for dinner in the seventies: two fillets of anchovy do not make an "insanely flavorful" sauce.

From the accompanying article: "if you feel the need to sass it up by adding a pinch of fennel seeds to the onions, a splash of red wine to the tomatoes or a mess of freshly chopped herbs at the end, I will not be offended. (The beauty of a good recipe is often in its flexibility, no?) Other options for an upgrade include mixing up the meat (pork and lamb) or serving the finished pasta with a spoonful of seasoned ricotta dolloped on top." These notes would have been helpful in the recipe notes.

I don't normally buy eggplant in the middle of winter, but I thought I'd give this a try. Roasted my eggplant a la Martha Rose Shulman. Didn't use basil in the middle of winter and should have substitited another herb or green. Should have thickened my tomato sauce more. I don't think there's much flavor to be gained in using whole tomatoes unless you're using either fresh or high-quality tomatoes. Not as good as I had hoped--will not make again--mostly pasta and not very meaty or cheesy.

comments.recipe_name: cheesy baked pumpkin pasta with kale
comments.comment: This was absolutely wonderful, pure comfort food. My cheesemonger sold me on Emmi Roth rather than Gruyere, and I'm so very grateful that he did. Used shells and took entire 15 minutes to brown; did not run under broiler. Relatively easy to pull together. Used my own turkey stock and two large cloves of garlic but only 1/4 tsp of chile flakes. Didn't find dry at all but then I don't normally drown my pasta in sauce or glop. Don't normally buy canned pumpkun but bought on clearance.

Very simple and easy to pull together on a Friday evening; used Maille Old-Style Mustard and I'm embarrassed to say Smucker's sugar-laden preserves. Resonated with my German roots. Hit the spot, but nothing to write home about--very much bachelor food.

Overall, just too heavy and processed and greasy--I found the roasted peppers better than the kielbasa, and a mess to clean up--Smucker's preserves just too cheap, commercial, and sugary alongside a cheap, greasy, commercial kielbasa--this is total supermarket/Walmart food, akin to getting cheap fried chicken from the supermarket. Give me vegetables any day--this was enough to drive me back to vegetables.
Sat in my stomach like a rock--ugh.

comments.recipe_name: cheesy baked pumpkin pasta with kale
comments.comment: It was Emmi Roth Le Maréchal instead of Gruyere which sent this pasta over the top: I'm sold now on this cheese and a true devotee.
This has become my favorite go-to unrecipe. Didn't have any sage, but as I chopped up broccoli rabe again and added that to the second half of the pasta cooking water, I didn't need it anyway.

comments.recipe_name: cheesy baked pumpkin pasta with kale
comments.comment: Made again on a cold rainy weekend, this time with Gruyere instead of Le Marichel and chickpea broth instead of turkey stock--not as intensely flavorful, but still very, very good, pure comfort food on a cold, rainy day. I'm sure the quality of my canned pumpkin contributed as well--I would be rather skeptical or doubtful about using Libby's.
I discovered an even simpler version of recipe in Colu Henry's Back-Pocket Pasta: Campanelle with Broccoli Rabe and Chorizo.

I have come to love Ali Slagle's recipes! I was skeptical about the temperatures on this one, but they turned out to be just right. Made this on a Sat morning and was relatively easy to pull together--I could easily imagine making this on a weeknight. I really appreciate her use of smoked paprika rather than chorizo and have used smoked paprika myself for years fairly frequently. Really comforting and warming on an early spring weekend morning. Used a mix of red/gold/purple potatoes.

comments.recipe_name: umami garlic noodles with mustard greens
comments.comment: I used soy noodles, turnip greens, and nutritional yeast. I was concerned it might be too salty, but it turned out to be fabulous. Relatively easy to pull together: most time-consuming part was slicing the greens.

comments.recipe_name: creamy white bean and fennel casserole
comments.comment: I halved the recipe, which in hindsight turned out to be stupid, because I was concerned that two pounds of fennel was going to overwhelm my pan--I should have realized that it would shrink, so it turned out too flat and thin. I followed the suggestion of tucking in some sausage and used my own chickpeas. Probably would have been better if I had made the entire recipe. Sounded promising but turned out to be disappointing as made. Not all recipes halve well. Not a big fennel fan.

comments.recipe_name: pearl couscous with creamy feta and chickpeas
comments.comment: This was wonderful and very easy to pull together on a weeknight evening. I used my own chickpeas, chickpea cooking broth, rosemary, and parsley. The cumin imparted a lovely flavor. Thoroughly enjoyable on an early spring evening as the geese clamoured outside.

I love Ali Slagle's recipes, and I love caramelized onions, so what a great combination on a cool, rainy, early spring weekend afternoon. Deglazing the pan with sherry vinegar took this dish over the top, as did some leftover Emmi Roth Le Maréchal cheese. Genuine comfort food, and easy to make, too. I can easily imagine making this on a weeknight. Whole Foods' "bits and pieces" of leftover ends of cheese make it possible to purchase just a small bit of some really interesting cheese.

comments.recipe_name: hearty whole wheat pasta with brussels sprouts cheese and potato
comments.comment: This was really wonderful--it smelled wonderful while it was cooking, and it was relatively easy to pull together. Very warming and satisfying and not too heavy. Roasted Brussels sprouts were wonderful. Wish I had some small purple Brussels sprouts that I had had earlier in the season.

This was incredibly easy to make. I love Colu Henry's recipes, and I've been working my way through her Back-Pocket Pasta cookbook. I used sweet potatoes and didn't have a lime so used cider vinegar, and I love tahini and sweet potatoes. Didn't use chile flakes but in hindsight might have used some smoked paprika or chile powder. I used the full time to cook. Good bread helps immensely. Even without the lime juice, the dressing was very good. A simple but satisfying, warming dinner.

comments.recipe_name: one pot french onion soup with porcini mushrooms
comments.comment: I love French onion soup, and this was a pleasure to make on a cool, overcast, early spring Saturday afternoon. I made the vegetarian version, and the fennel, porcini, porcini soaking liquid, and wine all more than compensated for beef stock. Because I used only a 5-quart Dutch oven, I couldn't fit all the stock. There are no shortcuts to genuine French onion soup. This is not a fussy recipe, but it does require long, slow cooking, and it's well worth the wait--restaurant-quality.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with brown butter and parmesan
comments.comment: I love Ali Slagle's recipes. This was super-easy and comforting to make on a cool, overcast, early spring Friday evening as rain was approaching--definitely what Colu Henry calls a "back-pocket pasta." Easy to reduce for one, too.

comments.recipe_name: baked polenta with crispy leeks and blue cheese
comments.comment: This was really easy to pull together and very warming on a cool late-March evening. The polenta method is a keeper, even if you don't make the leeks and use blue cheese. I used an end bit of Emmi Roth buttermilk blue cheese and chickpea cooking broth. I also bought an end piece of aged Irish Cheddar and plan on trying that. I think using a smaller amount of cheese as well as the buttermilk blue with its mellow, earthy flavor and soft, creamy finish kept the dish from becoming too rich.

comments.recipe_name: braised chicken wings in bean sauce
comments.comment: The sauce was good but messy: I used black bean-garlic sauce, no sugar, and liquid aminos rather than soy saice, preferring its smokier flavot. Will not make again.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with roasted broccoli almonds and anchovies
comments.comment: This was yummy and very easy to make on the first warm day of spring. I love Colu Henry's recipes and have been working my way through her Back-Pocket Pasta cookbook. Lemon added a lovely sunny, bright, citrusy touch, and no additional cheese was necessary.

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: I love this dish--I make it so often--I snatch up Brussels sprouts every time they're on sale! So good and so easy! I slice the sprouts in the food processor. Very warming meal on an early spring evening as thunderstorms were passing through. This has become a very easy go-to pasta dish for me, one I have made repeatedly.

comments.recipe_name: baked cheesy pasta with wild mushrooms
comments.comment: For starters, a 9 x 13 pan is 3 quarts, not two, and this dish would have burned if I had put it in a 9 x 13 pan, although I already got the pan dirty before I realized that. Other than that, this was easy to pull together on a Friday evening and warming on a cold, blustery, early spring day. Great for using up accumulated leftover pasta when a recipe calls for 12 oz but you have to buy 16. I love roasted vegetables. Baby bellas worked fine as did mozzarella. Very good.
So warming and consoling as it snowed in April! Even better with bananas! Although I trust avocado would be good as well!

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: This is a great way to use up accumulated leftover portions of pasta when a recipe calls for only 12 oz but you are compelled to buy an entire pound. I hadn't used the rosemary before becauae I never happened to have any, but it really does add a wonderful flavor.

comments.recipe_name: creamy cauliflower pasta with pecorino bread crumbs
comments.comment: You really need to make sure you core your cauliflower deep or else break up the center slices by hand, because the core just won't work in this recipe. That said, this was an easy recipe to pull together after a long Monday. Didn't have a shallot or even a small onion but was fine without it. Used penne because that was what I had.
