Lemon zest is a must, and this turned out to be a surprisingly flavorful and satisfying dish. I love caramelized vegetables.

comments.recipe_name: creamy cauliflower pasta with pecorino bread crumbs
comments.comment: Looking again at the photo in the accompanying article, I just found that if I didn't break up the center pieces, no matter how thinly I cut them, you end up with a cauliflower steak which is inedible--I don't mean that the core isn't edible, but that you just end up with this clumsily large piece. I encountered that problem with another Alison Roman cauliflower recipe. Slicing the cauliflower is certainly more quick and easy to prepare than cutting it up into florets.

This was a nice, flavorful spring dish--I used more flavorful and nutritious turnip greens and halved the recipe. Appreciated the greens. Interesting idea and not heavy at all like a traditional carbonara.

Another super-easy recipe by Colu Henry. I didn't even use "best"-quality vinegars--Eden and Whole Foods 365--and it was still absolutely delicious. Davis Tanis' basic polenta included in the link has been my go-to for ages. I love Colu Henry's recipes and have been working my way through her Back-Pocket Pasta, and I look forward to more recipes from her.

I love Colu Henry's recipes! I've been working my way through her Back-Pocket Pasta cookbook! This recipe was super-easy to make and oh-so-fragrant. Used two cinnamon sticks and served over egg noodles. Wonderful--a keeper and a winner.

comments.recipe_name: linguine with melted onions and cream
comments.comment: This is really very easy to make once you slice the onions--it cooks itself. You just need to be patient, and it's worth it! Warming and comforting on a cold, wet evening. I let the onions go a full hour for the second part even though I checked on them halfway through. Not caramelized onions, but definitely melted, and tasty and delicious! Inexpensive, humble food.

comments.recipe_name: olive oil braised chickpeas and broccoli rabe
comments.comment: I've started chopping the broccoli rabe before I add it to the pot, which makes it easier to eat, especially if I serve it over polenta--the stems can be rather fibrous, even if they are edible and tasty. You don't have to chop it finely, only roughly--the volume will reduce plenty as it cooks. One- to two-inch pieces or so are fine.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with sardines bread crumbs and capers
comments.comment: Super-easy, cheap, tasty, and satisfying--classic Minimalist.

comments.recipe_name: spaghetti with broccoli rabe toasted garlic and bread crumbs
comments.comment: I love broccoli rabe and snatch it up every chance I get. This recipe is classic Minimalist--easy, inexpensive, healthy, and satisfying

comments.recipe_name: spiced chickpea salad with tahini and pita chips
comments.comment: I love chickpeas--make 'em myself almost every weekend. Love tahini, too. Dressing very good--used all cumin and no cardamom. Used kale as a base. Did not use cucumber as not in season. Used full cup of oil, which wilted baby kale. Should have broken up pita chips. Very good and satisfying.

comments.recipe_name: spicy turkey stir fry with crisp garlic and ginger
comments.comment: The flavor enhancers definitely worked their magic on this otherwise normally bland meat. Did not use or need any sweetener. Served over black rice.

comments.recipe_name: black rice and red lentil salad
comments.comment: I used home-cooked chickpeas in place of edamame and walnuts because that was what I had on hand. Grear use for leftover black rice. I soaked the lentils for 3 hours, and they were just fine--red lentils are not the same as brown and green and break down more quickly. Used walnut oil. Good, though I think it would have been more flavorful with sesame oil.

comments.recipe_name: linguine with crisp chickpeas and rosemary
comments.comment: This was terrific and very easy to make! I love Ali Slagle's recipes! Used my own chickpeas which made it even better, and I love rosemary.

This dish was very easy to make and a wonderful balance of flavors between the sausage, chard, and rhubarb and a wonderful way to use some rhubarb I pucked up at the farmstand. A nice, light but nevertheless satisfying early summer dinner. I hadn't made rhubarb in a long time--its tartness balanced nicely with the earthiness of the chard. Wonderful first farmstand dinner of the year.

This really turned out well--very warming to bake on a cool, rainy day. The orange-rhubarb combination is lovely, just like orange and cranberry. I think the yogurt makes for a more moist and flavorful bread--it worked well in another Times recipe as well

comments.recipe_name: skillet chicken with orzo dill and feta
comments.comment: As another reader wrote, so good and so easy! Very Greek! Not everything has to be doused in sriracha or whatever it is. I've really come to like Sarah Copeland's recipes, and I have her book, Every Day Is Saturday from the library and look forward to trying more of her recipes--they seem very down-to-earth. I skipped the cucumber as it's not in season yet and used chickpea cooking broth, which I keep in the freezer, and an electric skillet.

comments.recipe_name: one pot pasta with ricotta and lemon
comments.comment: Super-easy and super-delicious--I always love Ali Slagle's recipes. Added fresh Michigan asparagus from the farmstand. A keeper.

comments.recipe_name: swiss chard rice bowl with chorizo
comments.comment: Easy to make. You really need to make sure you have dry and fluffy rice, however; next time, I would bake the rice, as I usually do. I'm sure it's great with chorizo but I topped with an egg. You can hardly go wrong with smoked paprika, which makes everything taste better. I love grain bowls and greens. Tossed in some home-cooked chickpeas. Hadn't made chard in a while, so was good to revisit. Appreciate recipes such as this.

comments.recipe_name: chicken milanese with tomato mozzarella and basil salad
comments.comment: Well, it would have helped if the recipe had specified to make the basil oil with only 2/3 cup oil rather than just "the oil." Made with turkey cutlets and regular breadcrumbs. Nevertheless, easy to make and a flavorful, light, but satisfying meal before the farmstand kicks in.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with marinated tomatoes and summer herbs
comments.comment: This was a beautiful, simple summer meal to make with fresh tomatoes from the farmstand and basil and parsley from the garden. Used mozzarella, and do include the lemon zest.

comments.recipe_name: creamy pasta with smoked bacon and peas
comments.comment: Used fresh corn as it is in season and mint, though I imagine basil would have worked equally as well. Lemon would have been nice but I was too impatient to juice one. Made during a summer downpour--quick, easy, tasty, and fresh--hit the spot with leftovers for lunch.

comments.recipe_name: farro and green bean salad with walnuts and dill
comments.comment: I love Colu Henry's recipes--this was wonderful, simple to make, and satisfying--how can you go wrong with farro? Used fresh beans from the farmstand. Gremolata wonderful--very flavorful. Very simple dish and yet very satisfying. Always a pleasure to make simple, satisfying dishes in the summer with fresh farmstand vegetables and herbs.

I appreciate Emily Weinstein's note on this recipe in her weekly newsletter: "serve with roasted potatoes and broccoli rabe or with noodles and broccoli. I’d make it with chicken thighs, which stay juicier and are thinner than breasts so I can skip halving or pounding."

comments.recipe_name: spiced seared eggplant with pearl couscous
comments.comment: I agree with Eric M--I felt this recipe was skimpy on the eggplant, and I should have trusted my judgment and used more eggplant. I used a Dutch oven, so I could have fit more, or perhaps have used a wide, deep skillet. Skipped the cayenne as I find it overpowering, and the dish didn't lack any flavor. I use fragrant, farm-fresh eggplant as oppposed to strip-mined, so I didn't want to losr that flavor. Easy to make and good but should have used more eggplant.

comments.recipe_name: coconut curry chickpeas with pumpkin and lime
comments.comment: I used my own home-cooked chickpeas and Craig Claiborne's garam masala blend. Threw in some lacinato kale just because I love it so much and snatch it up every chance I get along with a can of green beans I bought on clearance. Wonderfully warming on a cool, rainy evening, deeply flavorful, and easy to make. Served over brpwn rice. I love pumpkin and chickpeas. Seems like it would be a very adaptable and variable recipe. Mine did not turn out soupy--thick coconut milk helps.

I suspect my German ancestors who owned the Old Heidelberg restaurant in Chicago are turning over in their graves. I also suspect that the rich quality of my own homemade vegetable stock helped immensely. I forgot to blend in the extra olive oil at the end, which probably would have helped even more. I ate it with brown rice. The sauce was surprisingly good, more sweet than overwhelmingly hot. I had my doubts, but it wasn't bad. A relatively quick & easy Friday-night dinner.

This was surprisingly good--I was concerned it might be too salty with all that soy sauce (and I didn't use Kikkoman). I always smash my garlic cloves to release more flavor, and I used cilantro instead of basil since basil is out of season. Served over brown rice. The quality of your soy sauce and rice wine obviously make a huge difference in this recipe. I usually use Bragg's liquid aminos which I think has a smokier flavor. Used only 1/4 chile flakes which was plenty for me.

comments.recipe_name: lemony white beans with anchovy and parmesan
comments.comment: I like Emily Weinstein's suggestion in her weekly newsletter: "You could treat this Alison Roman recipe as a side and serve with simple chicken or fish, but I’d just straight up make it for dinner. Omit the anchovy to make it meatless, and try upping the amount of capers to make up for the lost salt and oomph. I’d serve it with thick pieces of toast, but it would be delicious with farro, and I would also be up for tossing it with a relatively short pasta shape, like gemelli."

comments.recipe_name: cinnamon apple quick bread with apple cider glaze
comments.comment: This turned out good, meaning flavorful, and not too sweet! I akipped the gooey glaze because I don't need the calories, sugar, and sticky-sweetness. Used olive oil and an apple from Michigan from the farmstand. Good and warming on the first morning of the season down in the thirties. Mine took only 50 minutes to bake, and although I let it cool, I was still able to enjoy it while it was still warm without it falling apart.

comments.recipe_name: parmesan white bean soup with hearty greens
comments.comment: I agree with Paul--although I normally love Ali Slagle's recipes and this soup sounded great in theory, I, too, had to add chickpea-cooking broth because I had way too much Lacinato kale, much as I love it. In hindsight, I should have used only one bunch rather than two, even though I prefer a chunkier to a brothier soup. Used my own chickpeas. Even then it was still too many greens--the proportions were way off. I didn't get a soup looking like the photo, either. Will not make again.

comments.recipe_name: sausages with potatoes and red cabbage
comments.comment: I picked up some wonderful Dunkelwurst from Whole Foods. Easy to make and smelled wonderful while it was cookng. Mine wasn't burnt at all but rather underdone. Appeals to my German roots and is a lovely-sounding fall dinner, but because it was underdone, it didn't turn out as satisfying as I had hoped or anticipated--not sure I would make again.

comments.recipe_name: white bean piccata pasta with broccoli
comments.comment: This was super-easy to make. I used regular rigatoni and home-cooked chickpeas, because that was what I had, though I can see why mezze rigatoni is called for, because regular rigatoni rather overflowed the pan. I always break up my capers, even though they're small, by chopping them coarsely first, a trick I learned from David Tanis I look forward to trying this with broccoli rabe. Very tasty! I used fresh broccoli from the farmstand.

This was tasty and very ineresting! I, too, had to whisk the soup to break up the bread and get it to dissolve. Just as flavorful after it cooled down as it was hot. Very warming and comforting on a cool autumn day. I agree that this recipe is a keeper--a refreshing change from oatmeal and good for both breakfast and lunch--flavorful but not sweet, but nor is it overly spicy. I used only half a cup of yogurt and felt that was sufficient and no need to garnish with additional to stir in.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker lentil soup with sausage and greens
comments.comment: Made this time with Italian sausage, black lentils, black bean broth, red wine, and broccoli rabe. Even though I had just bought my black lentils from a Whole Amazon that hasn't shrunk its bulk foods section to make way for a bar and Whole Alcohol, it still took seven hours to cook them--six was not sufficient, but they were fine at seven. Absolutely exquisite.

comments.recipe_name: roasted sausages with grapes and onions
comments.comment: This was super-easy to make. Made with turkey Italian sausage and lightly cracked/crushed fennel seeds in a mortar and pestle to release more flavor and make more easily edible and palatable, a trick I learned from David Tanis. Also used sherry vinegar. Very tasty.

WOW--this was SUPERB. This is right up there with Sam Sifton's 3-Cup Chicken. Even though I thoroughly enjoyed it and it wasn't difficult to make, this is no 30-minute recipe unless you have a NYT staff to prep the ingredients for you. But an absolute delight--WAY better than sodium-laden takeout, much less expensive, not that hard, and way more satisfying.
This was excellent--David Tanis never lets me down. Very easy and simple to make--a wonderful autumn dinner. I used a wonderful Porter bratwurst. Wonderful combination of flavors.

This was terrific and another great Ali Slagle recipe. I have been eager to try it since making Sam Sifton's 3-cup chicken. Used all four recommended root vegetables. Rich in flavor and very satisfying. Served over brown rice. Very easy to make and not greatly time-consuming. Had hoped to throw in some blanched broccoli rabe and wish I had--the green would have been a pleasant addition. Terrific autumn dinner.

I can see this is going to be another go-to recipe for me by Ali Slagle in addition to her broccoli rabe and chickpeas. Easy and unfussy comforting and warming dinner to make on a cold November Friday evening. Simple and satisfying, real comfort food. Made again; a real pleasure.

I used olive oil instead of vegetable oil, Fage Greek yogurt, a mix of light and dark brown sugar, and threw in 4 ounces of semi-sweet chocolate chips. Rose nicely. I had to bake for full 75 minutes. Smelled wonderful; very warming on a cold November day. Easy to pull together; not fussy. Turned out excellent--I actually like this pumpkin bread better than Melissa Clark's. Very moist but baked through--no need for any spread--very flavorful without being overly sweet.

comments.recipe_name: stir fried turkey and brussels sprouts
comments.comment: This was very tasty and came together rather quickly. I sliced my Brussels sprouts in the food processor because I prefer them that way. Served over brown rice. I made this spur-of-the-moment and didn't have any ginger on hand and didn't want to go out to get some, but even without the ginger, it was still good, and would have been even better and more flavorful with it.

Wow--this really IS "serious turkey stock," and it's easy to make and well worth it. I let it simmer all night, and I'm glad I did. This recipe's a keeper and is going to make some glorious soup this winter. Shredded the meat to make Martha Rose Shulman's Turkey and Brussels Sprouts Stir-Fry. Filled my home with a warming aroma on a cold, overcast November day. Wonderful weekend project--threw it in the pot Friday evening. Meat just falls off the bone after simmering all night.

comments.recipe_name: orecchiette with brussels sprouts and bratwurst
comments.comment: This was an easy and comforting Friday-night dinner. Didn't have orecchiette so used medium shells, and had only 12 oz. Had only 8 oz. Brussels sprouts. Sliced them in food processor as someone else recommended because I like them that way. It all came together.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker lentil soup with sausage and greens
comments.comment: I have made this soup several times, and it does freeze well. Made it this time with Sam Sifton's Serious Turkey Stock, and it was well worth it.

This was excellent and warming on a cold, gray November day. I didn't bother to puree the sauce and didn't use any serranos. I used Craig Claiborne's garam masala which gave the dish a sweet but nevertheless deep flavor. What a wonderful way to use leftover cooked chicken and turkey.

So simple, easy, tasty, and satisfying! When I was in college, sometimes we would eat just noodles and butter for a simple, cheap dinner. Perfect comfort food. Used as a base for Molly O'Neill's Goulash and might consider using as a base for Ali Slagle's Braised Broccoli Rabe and Chickpeas.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker beef stew with maple and stout
comments.comment: I wish I had seen the note at the end about searing the meat first before I had thrown everything into the slow cooker, as the meat dried out; I would definitely sear the meat first next time to lock in and seal the juices and flavor. But I doubt I'll make this again: very disappointing.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker lentil soup with sausage and greens
comments.comment: Used Lacinato kale this time, another favorite green of mine, and green lentils, Italian sausage, and homemade vegetable stock--yum! This has become a favorite soup of mine and so easy to make.

comments.recipe_name: spicy sheet pan sausage and squash
comments.comment: Simple and versatile. I made mine with Italian sausage and sweet potatoes. Chickpeas and feta are always welcome additions.
Polenta is winter's balm indeed; I look forward to making this recipe every winter as a base for many dishes. Comfort food indeed! Simple, warming, and flavorful.

I did not remember that I had made this before, so it must have been a while! Lovely brine and very flavorful--definitely sealed in the moisture, like brining a turkey. Served with Pierre Franey's Braised Red Cabbage rather than arugula salad. Very easy, satisfying, and warming.

comments.recipe_name: orecchiette with brussels sprouts and bratwurst
comments.comment: This is such a great recipe; I have made it several times. This time I made it with my own beer-braised brats. Delicious, warming, satisfying, and easy! Made with just s 12-oz. box of orecchiette, and it was fine. I definitely appreciate the shape of orecchiette for this recipe.

comments.recipe_name: vinegar chicken with crushed olive dressing
comments.comment: What does "crushed" mean? Minced?
This was easy to make but did not turn out as good as it sounded. Despite baking for only 25 minutes, it had a burnt flavor, and I found it overly gooey and gloppy. I will not make it again.

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: This recipe is so simple, and I've made it so many times--it's my go-to recipe when Brussels sprouts are in season. The rosemary really adds a lovely touch. Made tonight with gemelli because that is what I had on hand.

comments.recipe_name: roasted turkey drumsticks with star anise and soy sauce
comments.comment: This sounded good, but either my marinade didn't thicken or it was rather thin, so I felt as though a lot of ingredients went to waste and couldn't really adhere to the turkey or impart much flavor. I think I'll follow the suggestion to try Mark Bittman's recipe next time. Will not make again.

comments.recipe_name: slow roast pork shoulder with herb rub
comments.comment: Low and slow is the way to go indeed! I cooked mine for 12 hours in a foil-lined Dutch oven, and I had moist, juicy, pulled pork. I do wish I had remembered Mark Bittman's Pernil recipe, however, and will likely make that next time.
Watering down the sauce as suggested was a disaster.

Not even worth basting ribs with this diaster of a watered-down sauce--what a waste of ingredients and time. Should never have followed suggestion to water down sauce--will just end up pouring down drain. Should have just bought store-bought and will do next time.

I would have loved to have added bourbon but too expensive and not worth the cost. Added only one cup of water or would have been too thin. Did not add salt because already in the ketchup and I tend to find NYT recipes too salty even though yes, I realize this recipe uses k
Definitely easy and a great way to use up old bread and warming, too, on a cold winter day. Threw in a handful of chocolate chips.

comments.recipe_name: spiced irish oatmeal with cream and crunchy sugar
comments.comment: Well, first of all, I'm grateful I used a 2-quart baking dish, because it was all I could do to get it in the oven without overflowing and spilling. On what postmodern New York planet did you get everything to fit in a 1-1/2-quart baking dish? Do the math ... 7 cups of liquid is nearly 2 quarts. After 40 minutes it was still liquid, and it overflowed the too-small pan and spilled everywhere. Merry Christmas! USE A BIGGER PAN, and do your math beforehand.
This was very easy to make and turned out excellently--the moistness certainly does balance the whole wheat.

comments.recipe_name: vinegar chicken with crushed olive dressing
comments.comment: This was a truly easy recipe and very good--came together very quickly--most time-consuming part was pitting the olives. Smelled wonderful while cooking. I used cider vinegar and black olives, as that was what I had on hand. Would definitely make again. Made with chicken thighs.
These were good, and cleanup was easy with the parchment paper. A nice wintertime holiday or weekend breakfast.
What a mess--oil splattered everywhere. I far prefer Sifton's orange beef recipe. The flavor didn't compensate for the mess and the cleanup--I don't have a NYT staff to clean up for me.

This was so easy and very good! I actually have never made a ham before, as I haven't been a big ham eater, but now I have a lot of leftovers for other recipes! This recipe basically cooks itself, and the glaze was easy to make and flavorful. Cloves added a nice touch, too.
A 9 x 13-inch pan is 3 quarts.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker chicken with 20 cloves of garlic
comments.comment: This was good--the chicken was moist and succulent, and you definitely want to run it under the broiler to crisp and brown the skin. Cooked mine on high for 3 hours and used only 1/4 tsp pepper flakes. Do add some greens to the beans. Ate mine with pumpernickel. Did not find mine too garlicky: just right. Most time-consuming part was peeling the garlic, but after that, it cooks itself.

comments.recipe_name: baked oatmeal with berries and almonds
comments.comment: Mine didn't turn out as photogenically beautiful as the photo. Most of the oats sat on top of the liquid, making for a rather bifurcated oatmeal. Will not make again.

This bread is DELICIOUS and easy to make. Mine took the entire 75 minutes to cook. I oiled my pan, and the bread came out easily. Used sour cream instead of yogurt because that was what I had on hand and turned out great. Highly recommended and a great cold-weather Saturday morning treat. I used regular chocolate chips because that was what I had on hand and turned out just fine--I regularly add chocolate chips to my zucchini bread.

comments.recipe_name: one pan coconut curry rice with chicken and vegetables
comments.comment: I used green curry paste and tahini, because that is what I had on hand. Very easy to make and very warming on a cool, rainy evening. If I made it again, I wouldn't sprinkle salt over every layer and in the sauce. I generally trust Ali Slagle's recipes.
I wouldn't puree the greens--I'd add them after pureeing to add some texture and color.

comments.recipe_name: roasted potatoes with anchovies and tuna
comments.comment: This was good and very simple to make! Anchovies and capers very flavorful. I used fingerlings, and roasting potatoes smelled wonderful. Very substantial but with a light, not overly heavy touch. Very warming on a cool evening.

This was warming on a cool, damp evening and incredibly easy to make. I usually use my own beans but slummed it and used canned. Made with Cheddar and ate with tortilla chips, but next time I'd like to use Manchego. And how can you go wrong with smoked paprika?

comments.recipe_name: baked steel cut oats with nut butter
comments.comment: This was ridiculously easy to make and smelled wonderful while baking. I used almond butter but am eager to try it with tahini. Very good--the almond butter added a wonderfully nutty depth and flavor. I topped with raisins and walnuts and a little bit of milk--my new go-to recipe! Very warming and satisfying on a cool, rainy day.
This was delicious and easy to make on a cold, blustery day.
Manchego is the way to go--I far preferred it over the Cheddar.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker short ribs with chinese flavors
comments.comment: This was wonderful and ridiculously easy, the first time I ever made short ribs, and it filled my home with its wonderful warming aroma on a cold, overcast day. Served with brown rice.

This was an easy and tasty dinner to make. I had some soy noodles I had bought on clearance. Didn't have mirin so substituted sherry and didn't have rice wine vinegar so substituted plum vinegar--still turned out well, however. Used chickpea miso and Bragg's liquid aminos. Hit the spot without being overly heavy and filling.

In my haste I misread the recipe and added the full 1-1/2 cups of water, which was a disaster and made the sauce way too thin. It still turned out all right, however, and made for a simple, flavorful, and satisfying dinner. Made with buccatini and topped with nori. Definitely a great back-pocket pasta, but definitely do NOT add the full 1-1/2 cups of water. I would definitely make again for a simple dinner and would be great with roasted veggies.

Made with red lentils, fresh rosemary, diced tomatoes, and sherry vinegar, and left the bell pepper out since I didn't have one, and they're out of season. Ate on its own. Smelled wonderful while it was cooking and tasted just as good. I love Ali Slagle's recipes and always look forward to them.
This was lovely and easy to make. I used all four spices, yogurt, and lemon zest but no fruit as it is not in season.

I wish I had seen the note about the bay leaf and thyme or rosemary before cooking! Will definitely do next time! It would have been helpful to list them in the ingredients and recipe as optional. Mark Bittman's recipe uses bay leaves, which are wonderful, and I use thyme when I make vegetable stock, and rosemary is always a welcome addition.

I was skeptical about not soaking the beans but the quick-boil method worked like a charm and did not boil over, and these were aromatic and delightful. I've been cooking my own beans for years using Mark Bittman's method, but this might be my new go-to. I have long preferred making my own beans to canned and freezing and reusing the broth elsewhere. And Bittman introduced me to beans on toast as well, another simple yet luscious comfort food.

I must say, the simple aromatics in this recipe flavor the beans far more subtly and richly than Mark Bittman's add-ins. The quick boil and lengthy cooking time ensure genuinely creamy beans, and good bread is a must for this recipe: I used a flavorful whole-grain.

I didn't have a chile so used 1/4 tsp of crushed red pepper flakes. Used a 28 oz can of crushed tomatoes so didn't add salt and used leftover brown rice and mozzarella, because that was what I had on hand. Incredibly easy to make. Used one pan that went from stovetop to broiler. Cheap, simple, easy and satisfying on a cool, overcast day with rain approaching, but I don't know that I'd make it again: Cheddar definitely would have been better, but I was trying to use up some leftover mozzarella.

It just didn't work for me; it *sounded* good, and *looked* good. But I stood and I stood and I stood, beating it with a hand mixer, and nothing happened; how long is it supposed to take? I ran out of patience and just decided to do my usual French press over steamed milk, which is a lot less work and mess.
This was really good and easy to make--I always enjoy making long, slow-cooked dishes. My grocery store frequently has turkey necks on clearance, and like pork neck bones, they are really meaty.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with chorizo chickpeas and kale
comments.comment: Fusilli makes sense to capture the greens, but it loses the chickpeas,: if I made this again, I'd use mezze rigatoni to capture the chickpeas.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with chorizo chickpeas and kale
comments.comment: This was very easy to make, and you can hardly go wrong with chorizo, kale, and chickpeas. Manchego would have been nice but I had only Parmesan. Used Lacinato kale because I prefer it over curly kale. Water caused kale to clump, however, which was disappointing. Still turned out good, however, and a simple dinner without being overly heavy or fussy.

comments.recipe_name: brussels sprouts pasta with bacon and vinegar
comments.comment: Would have been helpful if the directions had stated to reserve 1 cup of pasta water: I should have known and thought I looked ahead but must have overlooked it, so I did without. Used cabbage, whole-grain mustard, and cider vinegar. I thought the vinegar and mustard married well with the brassicas and cabbage. Very simple to make and warming and satisfying on a cool evening without being heavy.

This was really very easy to make: the most time-consuming part is cutting up the cauliflower. I used a lovely Cheddar cauliflower and swapped smoked paprika for the cayenne. Should have let it cook the full 20 minutes to thicken the cream more. Roasting the cauliflower definitely adds a flavor punch. This was really very warming and satisfying on a cool, overcast evening. I have grown to look forward to Ali Slagle's recipes.
Upon further reflection, I have come to realize that the cream and cheese never came together to make a genuine sauce, despite stirring; it's just too watery. Will not make again.

It was all right, and the meat was moist, but the hot oven made for a very browned and crisp skin, which again, was all right, but I wouldn't rave about it. The milk definitely caramelized the skin. Very simple to make, however, and you can hardly go wrong. I don't know that I'd make it again, however.

comments.recipe_name: creamy one pot pasta with chicken and mushrooms
comments.comment: I found this dish easy to make and surprisingly rich in flavor. I think the key to intensifying the flavor is not to be impatient and rush it--cook the mushrooms for the full ten minutes and the dry pasta for the full two to three minutes, and don't add all the liquid all at once. Wine helps as opposed to water, and I used leftover bean cooking broth which added to the starchiness. Scraping up the browned bits adds flavor. Too. Used leftover shredded chicken breast added at the end.

comments.recipe_name: cannellini bean pasta with beurre blanc
comments.comment: This was really simple and seemed like a great way to use up those little handfuls of pasta left over when you buy a pound box but the recipe calls for only 12 ounces; I used up some leftover penne, and the recipe was reminscent of Mark Bittman's chicken-and-pasta recipe with mushrooms. Used homemade vegetable stock. I think if I made this again I'd use my electric skillet. I just didn't get the starchy, creamy consistency described; mine turned out rather soupy despite trying to reduce.

comments.recipe_name: marinated celery salad with chickpeas and parmesan
comments.comment: This salad waa good,,but I wouldn't rave about it. I used whole-grain mustard as I realized that I was out of Dijon. Very fragrant but smelled more aromatic than it actually tasted. I allowed it to marinate for only two hours. Allowing it to rest longer at room temperature may have melded the flavors better.

comments.recipe_name: marinated celery salad with chickpeas and parmesan
comments.comment: I should add that I did not use basil, as it is not in season, and this salad definitely needs basil.

comments.recipe_name: roast chicken with maple butter and rosemary
comments.comment: Ridiculously easy to make indeed, and the rosemary smelled heavenly while it was roasting. I love Colu Henry's recipes. It was wonderful indeed--this recipe's a keeper--oh so flavorful.
I used coffee because I didn't have cola, but I found thar the meat dried out, as is wont to happen in slow cookers; I should have made pernil. Will not make again.
Swapped tahini for sesame paste and peanut butter, didn't have any nuts, and tossed with leftover roast chicken--simple to make on a cool, rainy evening.

comments.recipe_name: chicken and rice with scallion ginger sauce
comments.comment: I actually made this with some leftover roast chicken which I tossed with the sauce or dressing and ate over some ginger rice which I had picked up on clearance--very simple and satisfying without being overly filling.

I had the same experience as another reader: it tasted all right, but the batter certainly wasn't "pourable," and "whisking" was unthinkable; "doorstop" was a pretty apt description. Rosemary and honey sounded so promising, but this recipe fell flat; fortunately, I don't have to share my mistakes with anyone else. Will try the other beer bread recipe instead next time.

comments.recipe_name: roasted tomato and white bean stew
comments.comment: I love Colu Henry's recipes. Made this on a cool, rainy day. Very easy to make, but beware splattering tomatoes! I normally make my own beans and use their cooking broth but used canned this time so used Melissa Clark's homemade chicken stock, which added a heavenly note. Took the suggestions of adding some sage leaves and kale and served with beer bread. Just right--not too thin or soupy or thick. Really lovely and warming without being overly heavy.

comments.recipe_name: chicken noodle salad with creamy sesame dressing
comments.comment: This was the perfect use for a leftover breast from a roast chicken and very easy to make; used my own homemade chicken stock, which boosted the flavor--I usually make either Sam Sifton's or Mark Bittman's Chinese noodles.
This was very easy to make with turkey cutlets and very tasty and satisfying without being overly heavy and filling, and I love arugula. I love Martha Rose Shulman's recipes.

comments.recipe_name: crispy sour cream and onion chicken
comments.comment: This was really easy to make, and the sour cream definitely did the trick of keeping the meat moist. I just used regular bread crumbs. I love Ali Slagle's recipes and always look forward to them.

You can hardly go wrong with this winning and satisfying combination--I used lacinato kale, farro, roasted chickpeas and sweet potato tossed with smoked paprika, and dressed the whole thing with tonnato.

comments.recipe_name: smoky roasted chickpeas with lemon garlic mayonnaise
comments.comment: This was wonderful--I love Ali Slagle's recipes, and you can hardly go wrong with smoked paprika. Used my own home-coojed chickpeas and tossed in a cubed sweet potato and used to top Julia Moskin's Big Grain Salad with farro and kale and a tonnato dressing--the Wall Street Journal had a similar recipe that called for frying the chickpeas, but I used this recipe instead.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with spicy sausage broccoli rabe and chickpeas
comments.comment: I love broccoli rabe and chickpeas, so you can hardly go wrong. Very easy to make; I used my own home-cooked chickpeas. Oriecchiette cups the chickpeas well so they're not sliding around. The lemon adds a wonderful sunny taste. Will definitely make again.
Make sure the chocolate is absolute liquid, not just soft, or you will have a mess on your hands. Since I used gingersnap crumbs, I did not add any sugar.

comments.recipe_name: seared tuna white bean and fennel salad
comments.comment: What a simple, satisfying, and flavorful lunch or dinner--this so classically and reliably David Tanis. Even though seared fresh tuna is naturally better, I could easily imagine making this with quality canned or jarred tuna or even salmon. I served it over a bed of arugula

comments.recipe_name: charred shallots with labneh and pita
comments.comment: This was wonderful on a hot summer day and relatively easy to make. What a wonderful discovery! I used lemon zest instead of preserved lemon, and it still tasted wonderful. The lemon adds a bright note. I made mine in the oven and roasted the garlic for 15 minutes, the shallots for an additional ten minutes, and the onions for an additional ten after that, and it still tasted flavorful, even though not grilled, although I'm sure grilled would have been wonderful.
And why am I not surprised tp discover that this is from Joshua McFadden? I have his Six Seasons cookbook and love it.

This was so easy and flavorful--definitely a keeper and to try with other greens--arugula sounds good. I tossed with mezze rigatoni and Ali Slagle's smoked chickpeas, but I can imagine using other roasted vegetables. Wonderful recipe.

comments.recipe_name: roasted salmon with miso rice and ginger scallion vinaigrette
comments.comment: Really simple to make and very satisfying.

I appreciated Sam Sifton's suggestions: "Craig Claiborne put red bell pepper in his tuna salad sandwiches, and called for homemade mayonnaise to bind the ingredients. I do neither, preferring scallions, hot sauce and jarred mayonnaise of the sort called Hellmann’s east of the Rockies, and Best Foods to the west. You might add curry powder, or white balsamic vinegar, or grated carrots. You might waffle the sandwich with cheese, for a pockmarked tuna melt. You might add dill."

I appreciated Sam Sifton's suggestion, not because I don't appreciate this recipe, but as alternatives for switch-ups or if you don't have bell pepper and red onion on hand: "Claiborne put red bell pepper in his tuna salad sandwiches, and called for homemade mayonnaise to bind the ingredients. I do neither, preferring scallions, hot sauce and jarred mayonnaise. You might add curry powder or white balsamic vinegar."

comments.recipe_name: sugar snap peas with yogurt feta and dill
comments.comment: Wonderfully cooling and refreshing lunch on a hot summer day with some bread and relatively easy to make.

This was really simple and really good, but in my stupidity, I did not mince the chicken small enough--would mince smaller next time. Tarragon would have been divine if I had had it. Poaching the breasts kept them moist.
Meant to add that I served it on pumpernickel toast, which was divine.

This was wonderful to make on a cool and rainy day with a large (and hence cheap!) zuke from the farm stand. I liked the hand-whisking of the wet ingredients as opposed to beating them with a hand mixer as well as browning the butter, both of which I believe contributed to and greatly improved the flavor and texture. Turned out very well, and I was very pleased with it. I've been burned by other recipes of Roman's, but this one was a winner.

1/4 garlic clove? Really? I decided to be bold and use an entire small garlic clove. Blew out my Vitamix motor trying to make it in there. Used my own home-cooked chickpeas and 1 cup chickpea cooking liquid instead of ice water. When I finally finished it up in the food processor, where I should have begun, turned out creamy, smooth, and flavorful.

I thought I'd try throwing in stems and all, as I add them successfully to my smoothies, but I ended up with a more fibrous sauce that required more water and diluted from the flavor contributed by the garlic oil. Next time I'll take the time to destem the kale as I had a smoother, more flavorful sauce last time when I took the time to do so.

I roasted a fresh sweet potato from the farm stand at 459 degrees rather than steam it. The tahini butter was delicious, but then, I love tahini, and it was a perfect complement to the sweet potato. Simple, satisfying dinner.

I hadn't made this in a while and enjoyed making it again with a large zuke from the farm stand. Used spaghetti instead of fettuccine, Parmesan, and slivered basil instead of parsley since I had it. Simple but satisfying , without nd delicious. I always enjoy Florence Fabricant's recipes. The basil added a fragrant gouch. Really delightful and simple, and surprisingly flavorful without a lot of intense heat, on a summer day, without being heavy.

What a lovely and verdant dip indeed with an abundancw of fresh summer herbs on a hot summer day. Came together very quickly and easily in my Vitamix, and I did add the mayo to make it creamier. Ate with crisp, fresh kohlrabi.

comments.recipe_name: grilled lamb chops with lettuce and ranch dressing
comments.comment: Very herby, verdant dressing, just like Melissa's green goddess dip which I just made. Used what I had on hand: mint instead of basil, yogurt instead of mayo, and milk-and-yogurt combo for buttermilk. Very crisp and refreshing on a warm summer evening and very easy to pull together.

comments.recipe_name: roasted sweet potatoes with yogurt and sesame seeds
comments.comment: This was really good and interesting and a simple dinner made with fresh farm stand sweet potatoes. I love sweet potatoes and am always looking for new ways to eat them.

comments.recipe_name: breton tuna and white bean gratin
comments.comment: This really hit the sot on a cool, rainy day and was easy to make as well as surprisingly flavorful: not the tuna-noodle casserole of my Roman Catholic childhood in the seventies!

comments.recipe_name: roasted cauliflower with feta almonds and olives
comments.comment: I love Colu Henry's recipes. Made this with a gorgeous purple cauliflower, substituted walnuts and black olives for green and almonds, because that was what I had on hand, and ate over a bad of green lentils tosses with olive oil and lemon while still war. Easy to make. I love cauliflower and especially roasted cauliflower. The most time-consuming part is cutting up the cauliflower. Threw in some rosemary-baked chickpeas I happened to have on hand.

comments.recipe_name: creamy white beans with herb oil
comments.comment: This was a simple, satisfying, and warming dish on a cool,rainy day. I love beans on toast. Used my own homemade chicken and halved the recipe as I was making it only for myself. Tried making the herb oil first in my Vitamix, which was a disaster; ended up having to transfer it to the food processor. The "herb oil" is really a pistou, which is traditional on this kind of dish. I used basil and parsley. Wonderfully simple and satisfying dish.
This was ridiculously easy to pull together and immensely satisfying in its simplicity, allowing the fresh and unadulterated ingredients go shine. I love Colu Henry's recipes.
This was a wonderfully earming and simple Friday-night dinner on the first cool night of autumn.

This was superr-simple: the hardest and most time-consuming part ischopping the onion and garlic. Surprisingly satisfying. I made with black beans and topped with Greek yogurt, although I imagine avocado would have divine, and cilantro would have added some depth as well.
When made as muffins, they definitely taste like donuts.

I made muffins, and they turned out all right, but I don't believe I'll likely it again. Make sure you read the recipe, because the ingredient list doesn't note that the butter and brown sugar amounts need to be divided: I ended up having to throw out my first go-around because in my haste, I added the full amounts. Mine took the full 20 minutes to bake.

comments.recipe_name: cheesy baked pumpkin pasta with kale
comments.comment: A 9 x 13-inch baking dish is 3 quarts.

comments.recipe_name: herbed white bean and sausage stew
comments.comment: I soaked the beans overnight, simmered the stew gently, and kept an eye on the liquid, but after two hours, the beans were still on the dry side, and it's not like my beans have been sitting around for years. When I cook beans, I normally do so with the lid on. It's a flavorful soup/stew, but the beans weren't as creamy as I would have liked them to be.

comments.recipe_name: carrot loaf cake with tangy lemon glaze
comments.comment: This was good and very easy to make--most time-consuming task is grating the carrots. Baked in only 50 minutes, and cleanup and removal was easy with the parchment paper. Not overly sweet, which I appreciated. Didn't make the glaze, and it was fine without it.
Turned out all right, but I prefer a banana and chia seed overnight oats that I make regularly. Used Fuji apples and tahini, walnuts, and all raisins, because that was what I had on hand.

comments.recipe_name: roasted butternut squash with lentils and feta
comments.comment: I should have known the squash would shrink and made the entire 2-pound squash; where does one find a one-pound squash? I imagine Delicata would work well. I started the squash first since duh, it takes longer, but I sliced mine too thin. And don't pour the broth down the drain! Deborah Madison has a recipe for cooking rice in black bean broth. I love squash and black lentils, and the cinnamon gave the dish an interesting flavor, but I should have made the whole squash and not sliced it so thin.

comments.recipe_name: roasted butternut squash with lentils and feta
comments.comment: Overall, it sounded and looked better than it turned out being; will not make again.
This turned out all right and was done in four hours, but many if most slow-cooker recipes tend to turn out soupy and bland; I will not make this recipe again.
Well, mine just didn't thicken, and I ended up with soup, not stew. Swapped smoked paprika for regular paprika which definitely enhanced the aroma and flavor, but will not make again.

comments.recipe_name: spicy butternut squash pasta with spinach
comments.comment: Indeed, the most time-consuming part is peeling, seeding, and cutting up the squash, but I love winter squash, ans this recipe really hit the spot on a cool, damo, blustery evening. Made in my Dutch oven. I think it could have used a higher squash-to-pasta ratio, however: 2-1/2 pounds of squash seemed to get lost in a pound of pasta . I think I would have added more spinach, too. But it was basically good and came together quickly once I got the squash seeded, peeled, and diced.

Well, they certainly taste good, and they were very easy to make, but maybe I should have let them go for 12 minutes, or maybe I flattened them too much, because they seemed to fall apart. Milk Street has a recipe that I'd like to try as well.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker red beans and rice
comments.comment: This recipe says to cook on high for seven hours, and if the beans are not done, to turn up to high for another 10 or 15 minutes.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker red beans and rice
comments.comment: Every time I make beans in the slow cooker, despite soaking the night before, they never turn out creamy.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker spicy black bean and sweet potato chili
comments.comment: I find that slow-cooker recipes always turn out too soupy; about the only slow-cooker recipe with which I've had any success is Sarah Digregorio's lentil soup recipe.
10 ounces of pasta? Really? Where did you come up with that number?

This was super-easy to make and VERY good. I used McIntosh apples and left the skins on because they contain nutritional value and flavor, and the dish was fine. Also used walnuts rather than pecans because walnuts are less expensive, and that was what I had on hand. Great recipe--mine was done in 50 minutes.

This was so good and so easy to make that I made it again with some apples I was able to get on sale for 99 cents per pound, but this time I peeled the apples to avoid the string of peel that hung from my mouth the last time I made this. Peeling the apples was worth the time and effort, and the peels didn't go to waste, as both they and the cores went into a vegetable stock. True comfort food on a cool autumn day.

comments.recipe_name: lentils with chorizo greens and yellow rice
comments.comment: This recipe is so typically David Tanis: I love David Tanis' recipes, I love lentils, and pimenton makes everything better. I used fresh turmeric rather than dried because I dislike the metallic taste of dried. I say this recipe is so typically David Tanis because he takes humble ingredients and makes them pop. I ended up taking Sarah DiGregorio's slow-cooker lentil soup from yesterday and pouring it down the drain because the lentils never cooked.

comments.recipe_name: lentils with chorizo greens and yellow rice
comments.comment: The rice alone in this recipe is a keeper and well worth making as an accompaniment to other recipes! As someone who has multiple sclerosis and who lives virtually constantly in phtsically pain, I deeply appreciate the turmeric and cinnamon.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker lentil soup with sausage and greens
comments.comment: Brown lentils did not cook after 8 hours of cooking; I just don't seem to have any success with dried beans and legumes in the slow cooker.

This is pure cold-weather comfort food! I have made this recipe multiple times; I snatch up apples on sale for 99 cents per pound. Wonderful to make on a cold Saturday morning; fills my home with wonderful smells. It's comforting to have something warm in the oven or on the stove bubbling away. Wonderful recipe and relatively easy to make. Not a fussy recipe. Wonderful way to take advantage of God's goodness and bounty in the fall.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with sausage squash and sage brown butter
comments.comment: I generally love and look forward to as well as trust Ali Slagle's recipes, but I felt that this recipe was skimpy on the squash and heavy on the pasta. I did not add any pasta water because I thought doing so would water down and dilute the flavor. Will not make again.

comments.recipe_name: butternut squash pasta with bacon and parmesan
comments.comment: Pretty good--how can you go wrong with winter squash and bacon? :-) Relatively easy to make.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with cauliflower bacon and sage
comments.comment: I love roasted cauliflower, and I made this recipe with a lovely purple cauliflower, but as usual, I thought the ratio of pasta to vegetables too high; I prefer more veggies and less pasta. Relatively easy to make, but I doubt I'll make again.

comments.recipe_name: sausage and peppers pasta with broccoli
comments.comment: Easy to make indeed as well as tasty. Used the last of my farm stand broccoli and peppers, which amounted to a pound and a half of broccoli, and had only 12 oz of rigatoni, but the dish didn't suffer, nor from the fact that my broccoli was rather stemmy, but since it was farm stand broccoli, the stems were tender, so I just sliced them 1/4-inch thick. Wonderful dish that I hadn't made in a while so was providential that I ran across it again.

comments.recipe_name: maple roasted squash with sage and lime for two
comments.comment: My mother loved this and took home all the leftovers! She wasn't bold enough to eat the skins, but I was surprised to realize that butternut squash skins are edible. She was also too old-fashioned and traditional to squeeze lime over it, but I loved it.

comments.recipe_name: sauteed greens with smoked paprika for two
comments.comment: I made with broccoli rabe, and everything tastes better with pimenton--introduced my mother to both. We made the turkey thighs as well, and this was a terrific accompaniment.

comments.recipe_name: smoky lentil stew with leeks and potatoes
comments.comment: I love David Tanis' recipes, and this recipe is so dependably David Tanis. I think chorizo would be a great addition. I used rosemary, which made it heavenly. Even though I used farm-fresh potatoes that smelled wonderful as I peeled them, they didn't break down as I thought they would: next time I would slice thinner and halve or else use Yukon Gold. For those who balked about three pans, the potato pan doesn't even get dirty, and what can I say? Go to McDonald's.

comments.recipe_name: cheesy baked pumpkin pasta with kale
comments.comment: A 9 x 13 pan is *3* quarts, NOT 2 quarts; this recipe does NOT fit in a 2-quart pan.

I wasn't exactly sure what I was aiming for: I THOUGHT it was set ar 45 minutes--it APPEARED set--but when I cut into it, it was soupy. Tastes terrific, however, but next time, I think I'll let it go the full hour. I'd definitely like to make this recipe again, however: I far prefer a simple, homespun, humble dessert such as this to any chocolate ganache or fancy dessert. Pure comfort food on a gray November day.

I can see why some chose to roast rather than sear, because I nearly set my kitchen on fire and filled my home with smoke, and I ended up doing the last batch on medium-high rather than high. But I think you definitely want a charred broccoli to intensify the flavor, which it was; this definitely wasn't boiled broccoli. I used the last of the farmstand broccoli, which was rather stemmy anyway, so it was a good use for them. Lemon added a bright flavor, which it always does.

comments.recipe_name: sheet pan crisp tofu and sweet potatoes
comments.comment: I love sweet potatoes, and I appreciated the idea, but I won't be making this recipe again. I usually toss anything I roast in a small amount of oil before roasting, and even drizzled with oil or dressing after roasting, I thought the potatoes turned out dry. The tofu was an interesting idea, and although I appreciated the texture, I thought it was lacking in flavor as well. My dish was easily done in 35 minutes.

I made this dish again this year when turkey necks went on clearance. I'd recommend serving it over egg noodles or polenta. Used my own homemade vegetable stock. Wonderfully rich, succulent sauce. The necks are surprisingly meaty, just like pork necks. Humble but richly rewarding food.

comments.recipe_name: sheet pan sausages and brussels sprouts with honey mustard
comments.comment: I generally look forward to Ali Slagle's recipes, and this dish was easy to make, though cleanup won't be so. I love walnuts but wasn't about to put them in a 450-degree oven for ten minutes when the sprouts were already charring at 15--seriously? But it was a good and simple meal to make, and sausages are always on clearance at my store.

comments.recipe_name: sheet pan sausages and brussels sprouts with honey mustard
comments.comment: And turning the vegetables over so that they are cut-side down on a hot pan is a bit unrealistic.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker lentil soup with sausage and apples
comments.comment: This soup was very good with an interesting complexity of complimentary flavors. But it required a LOT of prep time--get up early! I'm eating it for dinner. Used plain brown lentils, and it was fine. Chop only ONE apple as you prep your ingredients: otherwise, your other chopped apple will have to sit in the fridge all day.

comments.recipe_name: baked alfredo pasta with broccoli rabe and lemon
comments.comment: I love broccoli rabe, and I love baked pasta on a cold, gray day, especially when the pasta contains greens. Came together very quickly, using only 6 oz of mozz kept it relatively light as opposed to heavy and gloppy, and the lemon added brightness. Used regular bread crumbs as that was what I had. I love Ali Slagle's recipes and always look forward to them. This one's a keeper and will make again.

comments.recipe_name: honey soy braised pork with lime and ginger
comments.comment: This was absolutely delightful and a wonderful slow-cook on a wet, wintry Saturday; I love taking advantage of pork shoulders when they are on sale and I can get a small one, which makes for a very economical meal. As another reader noted, the lime and cilantro are essential. I usually use Bragg's Liquid Aminos and wish I had here due to its smoky flavor. Served over brown rice. Far lighter than anything glopped with barbecue sauce. I agree with others and lean more toward tamari than soy sauce.
This has become my go-to crumble recipe, and I'm embarrassed how many times I've made it this fall.

I subbed dried cranberries, walnuts, and coconut flakes because that was what I had on hand, but it still turned out good: not as fancy or as elegant as the Madison's, but more economical. Very easy to pull together.
And, I should add, terribly addictive.

comments.recipe_name: honey soy braised pork with lime and ginger
comments.comment: Have to add that I really enjoyed the dark and caramelized sauce. This whole dish was surprisingly good and relatively easy once garlic minced, ginger grated, and lime zested.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker lentil soup with sausage and apples
comments.comment: The cider in this soup is hugely aromatic and flavorful and lasted through the week as I ate leftovers for lunch. I used a local Chicago brand, Right Bee. This soup was so flavorful that since I had leftover cider I am making it again. I already love lentils and lentil soup, and this recipe took the flavors over the top in a positive way. Highly recommended: make this soup while apples are in season!

comments.recipe_name: midnight pasta with roasted garlic olive oil and chile
comments.comment: I love roasted garlic, and I love Ali Slagle's recipes. The aroma of roasting garlic filled my home on a cold, gray evening. This was very simple to pull together and satisfying without being heavy and gloppy. The suggestion to microwave the garlic made me cringe. As for it taking 40 minutes, what can I tell you? Either pour yourself a glass of wine, or go to McDonald's.

comments.recipe_name: olive oil braised chickpeas and broccoli rabe
comments.comment: I love this recipe and look forward to it every winter. I just learned that Melissa Clark has a similar recipe in her book, Dinner: Changing the Game and says you can use just about any green.

comments.recipe_name: lentil and orzo stew with roasted eggplant
comments.comment: This was very good, and I love roasted eggplant. Used plain brown lentils, ditalini, and feta. Would make again--a wonderful way to use end-of-the-season eggplant as the weather begins to cool.

comments.recipe_name: roasted chicken thighs with winter squash
comments.comment: This was wonderful and incredibly easy to pull together. The lemon infused a wonderful flavor into the chicken skin. I love winter squash and used Delicata. Would definitely make again, and I can see why it's one reader's go-to recipe.

This was really very good, humble but rich in flavor, and very easy to make: a great use for a ham bone and warming to make on a gray, overcast day. I made my own white beans and used the bean cooking broth to flavor this soup. I found this soup much more satisfying than gloppy split-pea soup. Used my last cabbage from the farm stand, and I'll have leftovers for all week for lunch. This is a soup meant to be shared with someone who appreciates home-cooked food.

I think adding one teaspoon of salt was a mistake, given how salty the ham already is. This turned out to be a very rich bread. I thought it would be a great way to use leftover ham. I grated all of my cheese, though little cubes of cheese would have been pleasant to bit into as I have done in other breads, but I didn't have the patience or energy to do so. My bread too only 40 minutes to bake. Definitely give it a good five minutes to cool in the pan, or it will fall apart. Will not make again.
A 9 x 13" pan is 3 quarts, not two.

comments.recipe_name: spicy white bean stew with broccoli rabe
comments.comment: I love broccoli rabe, and this soup/stew came together rather quickly and easily. Used my own home-cooked beans, the cooking broth, and tomato paste and red pepper flakes. The feta is a great addition. Would definitely make again--a warming and satisfying lunch on a snowy, housebound New Year's Day. I snatch up broccoli rabe every chance I get and could live on soup all winter long. Very healthy and satisfying.

comments.recipe_name: baked risotto with greens and peas
comments.comment: I used kale, Swiss chard, and my own homemade vegetable stock on a cold and gray Saturday in January when I wanted to hibernate and stay indoors. I love winter greens. This was very warming and satisfying and very easy to make.

comments.recipe_name: turkey thighs with pickled cranberries and onions for two
comments.comment: On second thought, they were not done at 40 minutes; if I were to make this again, I would let them go for 50 to 60 minutes, even though I had brought them to room temperature first.

comments.recipe_name: turkey thighs with pickled cranberries and onions for two
comments.comment: I think the secret to their being done in 40 minutes is allowing the skin to dry out: my mother skipped this step, and lo and behold, they weren't done. I did allow the skin to dry out, and they were done in 40 minutes. This was a wonderfully simple small-scale meal. The pickled onions and cranberries were a wonderful accompaniment along with the greens and squash. I appreciated this small-scale and simple, unfussy menu: not everyone makes or participates in Thanksgiving for 12 or a crowd.

comments.recipe_name: turkey thighs with pickled cranberries and onions for two
comments.comment: Ugh--these DEFINITELY needed a good hour.
I didn't find the garlic as softened as roasted garlic; nevertheless, I found this to be an easy, satisfying, homey lunch on a winter's day. I used a bay leaf. Very flavorful in a subtle way

comments.recipe_name: ground beef chili with chocolate and peanut butter
comments.comment: This came together very easily. I took the suggestion to use black beans, and it turned out fabulous--absolutrly delicious, and that was even without chipotles. I am heat-adverse, but the flavor of this chili is so mellow and complexly interesting that I think I could afford to throw in a chipotle next time because it would be balanced by the rest of the flavors without being straight heat. I used turkey, but the flavors are so interesting that it did not turn out bland.

comments.recipe_name: crispy parmesan roast chicken with lemon
comments.comment: The meat was moist, and I love rosemary, but I was not a fan of the Parmesan skin, and the chicken definitely did not need any salt along with the Parmesan, which I'm grateful I did not add. Will likely not make again.

This was terrific to make over a long, snowy weekend with leftover roast chicken (my own, not rotisserie). I used 4 tbsp fresh turmeric as I prefer the flavor and it keeps forever in the freezer. Yes, it does take a long time to make, but I hadn't made it for a few years. Although there are a lot of uses for leftover roast chicken, this seemed a propos fot a long snowy weekend. Very flavorful. I served over brown basmati.

I thought these were a welcome break from pastry bakery muffins, just as Martha said, and they taste terrific! The buttermilk makes all the difference. I love Martha Rose Shulman's recipes and miss her. These muffins had a great texture and crunch.

comments.recipe_name: turkey and rice casserole with yogurt topping
comments.comment: This really waa very tasty indeed and easy to pull together. The yogurt was an interesting alternative to a mash, and the lemon brightened it up. Used leftover roast chicken. Delicious and comforting indeed! I love Martha's recipes and miss seeing new ones from her; I always used to look forward to her column and have learned many things from her.

comments.recipe_name: neck bones pork neck and noodles
comments.comment: I snatch up neck bones every time they're on clearance: "a surprisingly rich, comforting and delicious dish coaxed from only a few ingredients," indeed. In the past, I have made Jacques Pepin's neck-bone stew, but this was a simple, satisfying change-up on a cold winter's evening. Egg noodles sound like a great alternative, too.

Well, I stupidly thought I might be able to halve this recipe, but it was a disaster, way too much to fit in a smaller pan, so I ended up making it in a 9 x 13 pan anyway, but thinner. I'd like to make this again using the full recipe, but my attempt to halve it was a disaster.

comments.recipe_name: braised chicken with tomatoes cumin and feta
comments.comment: This really turned out very well and was very easy to pull together. Chicken very moist and succulent and cumin flavor wonderful. Served over rice with cilantro.

comments.recipe_name: crisp chicken schnitzel with lemony herb salad
comments.comment: My mother reports that her father, who was Austrian, and her German mother both made schnitzel as described in the article, so I am eager to read the article myself and to follow the same method.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker lentil soup with sausage and greens
comments.comment: I think the next time I make this, I'll soak the lentils, because at 8 hours, they still weren't done. Usually make this with homemade vegetable stock but made this time with homemade chicken stock, which was very tasty indeed. Added blanched broccoli rabe as the green and loved it. I'll look forward to this for lunches during a sub-zero week.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker chickpea red pepper and tomato stew
comments.comment: This recipe turned out all right to pretty good, and I generally am enthusiastic about Spanish food and anything with pimenton. I used sherry vinegar, and smoked almonds would have been great, but I did not have them. Roasted my wn peppers and used crushed tomatoes. A fair amount of prep work so do make allowance for that.

I love Colu Henry's recipes, I love eggplant, and I love Pasta alla Norma, and I thought this recipe was terrific and came together rather easily. I also love that the eggplant is roasted, which I always do anyway. Very flavorful without being gloppy and heavy. I used mezze rigatoni.

I was skeptical, but the result was indeed a very creamy and smooth hummus; I ended up using the full 2/3 cup of water, and it took my chickpeas only an hour to break down. Used my Vitamix, and it worked beautifully.

I gave made this dish many times, and it really is an easy weeknight dish ans very flexible and adaptable to what's on hand. It makes a terrific base for leftover roast chicken or roasted vegetables or raw vegetables in the summer.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with spicy sausage broccoli rabe and chickpeas
comments.comment: I love broccoli rabe, I love chickpeas, and I love this dish. I grab broccoli rabe every chance I get. The lemon adda a wonderful flavor to this dish. Fairly easy to pull together, and delicious; I have made it several times.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with spicy sausage broccoli rabe and chickpeas
comments.comment: I meant to add, however, that I use only one pound of broccoli rabe: I mean, really? 1-1/2 pounds? What would I do with the remaining half-pound? And it turns out just fine.

I so appreciate Sam Sifton's suggestion to make a big vat of this soup this weekend, because that's exactly what I did, and I've been wanting to make this soup for a long time, and the substitution for the chipotle was perfect. This really is the best black bean soup, and the chipotle substitute made it flavorful without being heat-intensive. I wish I had seen the suggestion to soak the beans in dark lager, because I bet that would be good, too.

comments.recipe_name: sheet pan kielbasa with cabbage and beans
comments.comment: This was a wonderfully simple dinner after a long Monday and surprisingly flavorful. I took advantage of cabbage being on sale for St. Patrick's Day. Very easy to pull together after a long day and very satiafying.

comments.recipe_name: sheet pan kielbasa with cabbage and beans
comments.comment: This was so good that I decided to make it again with cabbage on sale after St. Patrick's Day. The cabbage sweetens as it roasts, and I already love roasted vegetables as it is. The vinaigrette is really simple but really good and flavorful. This was a simple and satisfying dish to make on a Friday evening. I always look forward to Ali Slagle's recipes.

This was really very simple to pull together and very tasty! I love Florence Fabricant's recipes. Wonderful discovery! Sausage is always on sale at my store, and this recipe made a very inexpensive, simple, and satisfying dinner, yet with a touch of elegance.

comments.recipe_name: cheesy baked pasta with sausage and ricotta
comments.comment: This was really relatively easy to pull together on a cool, damp, blustery March evening and very warming and satisfying. I do recommend using a small pasta such as the small shells and making sure it is submerged, because I used a medium shaped pasta and had to bake the dish for the full 22 minutes. But the end result was very flavorful and very satisfying. As I don't have a 12-inch ovenproof skillet, I used my Dutch oven, and the dish turned out fine.

I couldn't agree more with Steve's observation about the supposed 10-minute cook time: 10 minutes to COOK, certainly, but to cut those carrots into matchsticks? I don't believe my food processor does that; it's got to take an hour alone to do that, at least a half-hour.

comments.recipe_name: escarole salad with smoky halloumi croutons
comments.comment: Important note from the accompanying article not mentioned in the recipe notes: "other firm cheeses suited for frying and grilling — kefalotyri, queso blanco, bread cheese, paneer — can be used instead. You can even substitute feta."

comments.recipe_name: baked spanakopita pasta with greens and feta
comments.comment: I love greens, as well as Ali Slagle's recipes, and this recipe was very warming, satisfying, and comforting on a cool, rainy spring evening, and it really came together very easily and wasn't at all fussy. I used Swiss chard, arugula, parsley, and fusilli, though I think dill would have been lovely--I just didn't have any. I would definitely make this again, because as I say, I love greens.

Made this soup a second time again at Sam Sifton's suggestion on a cool, rainy, April weekend and was glad that I did. Added a jalapeno this time, and even though I am heat-adverse and used the chipotle substitute, I could have withstood more heat, but the soup was still very good and relatively easy to pull together. My beans did soften despite following the recipe, and what Julia writes about wine makes sense. I have grown to love this soup and plan on making it more.

This really came together rather easily on a cool, damp evening and was warming and satisfying. Did the reader who suggested the yogurt actually try it, because I believe this recipe needs the liquid for the pasta to absorb, except that mine may have come out a bit too liquidy, but it still turned out well. Would love to try this with kale and maybe even the yogurt. Mustard greens sound good, too, and my store often has turnip greens. Broccoli rabe might be good as well.

comments.recipe_name: slow cooker pork tacos with hoisin and ginger
comments.comment: This recipe was easy enough to make, but I"m unsure that I'd make it again, as the meat was so fatty, and the sauce wasn't so stupendous as to make it again: both hoisin and fish sauce are so salty, and I dislike using processed ingredients.

comments.recipe_name: one pan feta pasta with cherry tomatoes
comments.comment: An easy and delicious recipe, but has anyone seriously been able to submerge 12 oz of pasta in am 11 x 7 pan? I found it a bit crowded. Smashed the garlic to release more flavor and used farfalle. I personally love the flavor and aroma of thyme and rosemary, particularly rosemary, and I believe both herbs enhanced the dish. Did not add any salt as the feta has plenty. I'd rather read than cook, so I found this recipe beneficial on a cool, rainy weekend.

"Here's the recipe." That was my response to my husband's question, "What would you like me to make for dinner on your birthday, Sweetie." Pretty foolproof to make. Pretty fantabulous flavors to eat. We didn't find it dry as others suggest but my Italian hubby is Italian and it is the mode NOT to heavily sauce pastas in his heritage. Paired with a nice glass of Arneis, I was a happy woman...in spite of having another year on my age.

comments.recipe_name: scallops with pomegranate syrup and foriana sauce
comments.comment: Unusual combination of ingredients beckoned. Made per recipe with exception of substituting pistachios for pine nuts (allergy for latter). Outcome was delicious. Note ingredients list does not name pomegranate seeds are garnish although photo shows them on dish. So seeded a fresh pomegranate and used them per photo. A very nice addition for eye appeal, tartness and texture. This made it to our "keeper" file.

Easy to make, flavor rich, nutritious and makes the house smell wonderful while it simmers. A keeper! Made the vegetarian version, no pancetta but with parmesan rind. Didn't have farro in the pantry and too lazy to run to the CoOp to fetch some, I used 1/2 cup fregola instead and added that in for the last 20 minutes or so of cooking time. Splendid outcome. But next time with farro! PS - Even better the second day.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with fried lemons and chile flakes
comments.comment: Winter citrus season is THE time to make this dish! We've made it three times, last season and this one included, using thin skinned Meyer lemons and each time. Splendid outcomes. Be sure to watch the video for how to prep the lemons before caramelizing them. Also while the recipe lists says he celery leaves and parsley are optional, they really do make a difference in the eye appeal and flavor of the dish.

Brilliantly simple way to achieve outcome of tender lamb to reuse in other braises (like Bittman's Chinese flavored lamb dish) and ragus. Don't try to short cut / skimp on the browning step. Makes all the difference. I used about half water and verjus for the braise liquid and braise time of 2 hours. Worked swell.

Absent the tuna, I made the frisee /radicchio salad portion as a starter course for a seared scallop main dish. Delicious and good enough to be a 'keeper' in the salad file. Didn't have limes so used fresh Meyer lemon juice instead. Cooled the dressing before tossing with the greens. The Italian parsley in local organic market was so good, fresh and tender that I plucked the leaves and tossed them in whole with the greens. Salad plates were practically licked clean. :-)

Made as written. Outcome was luscious. Next time I'll make a couple of adjustments to find the 'just right' for us for amount of tahini and salt for our tastebuds. Maybe 25% less of both of those. Added extra lemon, as the recipe suggests. As close as I've come to making at home the fantastic hummus of take out Lebanese food place. The Lebanon raised owner told me the secret was using baking soda in soak and cook stages for the beans. Just as this recipe does.

Who knew? Two of my favorits, sour cherries and bourbon, are tasty partners in this easy cocktail. Canned or frozen (and thawed) sour cherries (available in groceries here on the west coast) work as well as fresh ones. I find a teaspoon (or so) of simple syrup works better than trying to dissolve sugar. If you go that route, try superfine sugar to dissolve easier than regular sugar. A little squeeze of fresh juice from the orange from which the peel came is a nice add too sometimes.

I'm a fan of Bittman. I love Cacio de Pepe. How could I lose? Big time as it turns out had I followed his recipe for this dish. See Peter and Michael's notes from two months ago. Then follow their suggestions. It will be a good outcome. Following this recipe will not get to the dish you the silky dish want to eat. Or look for the Bon Appetit / Epicurious published version from 2011.

comments.recipe_name: colorful chicory salad with anchovy dressing
comments.comment: If you can find salt packed anchovies, all the better! (They'll need to be soak, rinsed and drained first.) A simple and simply splendid salad. I cut back on the anchovies by half, personal preference since I prefer the little guys to offer a soft kiss and not a big smooch in dressings. I imagine you could eliminate them all together and this would still be great. Maybe sub oil cured black olives finely chopped in their place. Hmm...I might try round two with that edit tomorrow.

comments.recipe_name: cardamom scented oatmeal pancakes with apricots and almonds
comments.comment: My husband has made these several times since first seeing the recipe in 2011 and each time delicious. California apricots are best here, not Turkish. Hubby has even frozen leftovers then popped in toaster to reheat.

...agree with others who recommend cutting back salt in the apple cider / water cooking stock. I used the full amount the first time. I thought it was better using half the salt the second time. I also used extra lemon. And cut the amount of dressing back by almost half. A good dish.

If using pearled farro you can cook as per this recipe. But in my experience it still takes 45 min, not 30, to tender stage. If using non pearled farro, you'll need to soak overnight and then it will still take 45 min. They cook differently and have a slightly different texture. The outcome of the dish will be slightly different with each one. (See Francine's earlier comment.) Also...

More Latin inspired than pure Cuban w/ jalapeno and pork included? Great sofrito absent bacon. Outcome very good flavors. Beware salt lick if you follow recipe! Soaked beans (always do) and they still took 1:45 to cook. Skeptical beans can cook in 1 hr per recipe when not soaked. Prep notes: 1) soaked beans overnight, 2) salted beans after cooking, 3) reduced salt by 2/3 overall, 4) no bacon. Next time: I'll quarter green bell used in simmering stage so I can remove it - gets mushy.

Made per recipe. Easy. Delicious outcome. Instructions don't specify how thick to slice potatoes. Chose 1/4" "ish" and seemed right based on result. All liquid was absorbed nicely as the dish cooked. 1 hr + 15 minutes was perfect baking time. Good news if serving as part of 'buffet table' meal - it is as good barely warm as hot. Next time thought: I'll reduce gentle boil time from 5 to 4 min.

Been making Canlis Salad from original recipe from WAY back in late 70's. This is a little different and a lot better! Omitting bacon still makes a great salad - use some olive oil to toast crutons. Noting some don't want to use coddled egg yolk (but it is safe to do), try Lidia Bastianich's trick of using a hard boiled yolk in blender with other dressing ingredients. You can get by with @ 1.5 tsp dry oregano instead of fresh; whisk into dressing. Fresh mint is essential.

comments.recipe_name: roasted salmon glazed with brown sugar and mustard
comments.comment: Brilliantly simple and simply delicious. Foolproof. I tried this with Dijon and mustard per recipe the first time. Then a second time used whole grain mustard spread over salmon and then used a Microplane grater to grate over some hard as a rock brown sugar that I was going to toss out. That worked very well...in fact I may prefer that adaptation. So an idea for the brown sugar you think is too hard to use.

comments.recipe_name: baked cheesy pasta with wild mushrooms
