comments.comment: Per recipe but for 10 oz pasta instead of 8, couple extra ounces of mushrooms and subbing fresh thyme for rosemary. Watch video for the amount of time mushrooms should roast; that is key for texture. This is so rich with the cheeses and cream, I think it would be AOK to use only creminis next time and save the spendy wild mushrooms for something where their flavors are more naked. Needed @1/4C of pasta water to get to right consistency before baking. Served 5 as main course. Keeper!

comments.recipe_name: slow roasted cherry tomatoes with basil oil
comments.comment: Made per recipe but without the addition of the basil oil because I was using this as a side for Melissa Clark's Baked Cheesy Wild Mushroom Casserole. Easy technique and good outcome even if you don't want the basil oil included. I think the little bit of sugar with the salt in this recipe intensifies tomato flavor. Drizzled the juices from roasted tomatoes over them after arranging them on serving platter. Made these a couple of hours ahead and they were delicious room temperature.

Fast food at it's finest! So quick and easy to prepare. Delicious to eat. Even better that the ingredients are good for you. First pass at the recipe made it as written and used crushed red pepper flakes. In our household this was a main dish and enjoyed by all. Next time we'll play with another crushed pepper, like Aleppo which is staple for us. Not sure I'd add the olive oil to the yogurt next time.

comments.recipe_name: roasted sweet potatoes with yogurt and sesame seeds
comments.comment: I like fish sauce in many things but for my household's taste buds this recipe was better leaving that out and subbing salt instead. Otherwise as written. Used the pickled chiles option and not dried flakes. Would not use sesame seeds next time. Would increase 'salsa' next time. Japanese sweet potatoes do make a difference if you can find them. We're fans of sweet potatoes and yams of all kinds so this recipe is added to the keeper file.

comments.recipe_name: pizza with sweet and hot peppers
comments.comment: Used fresh pizza dough from local CoOp - Whole Foods or Pizzeria would have it too - to save time. Tossed / stretched to fit jelly roll pan. Cut the topping recipe in half (although I used the whole jalapeno anyway and it was just right heat level). It was a perfect fit of topping to crust. Serves four that way. Recommend mixing fresh marjoram in with the onion mixture if you're using it so that fresh herb doesn't burn.

comments.recipe_name: pasta salad with roasted eggplant chile and mint
comments.comment: 1) Watch video...helpful to see cook color for eggplant...took mine 40 min to get there not 25. 2) Says serves 2, I think 3 as main, even 4 as side. 3) Made twice; first per recipe, second minus anchovies given one guest can't eat them. Add a little more salt to compensate. Delicious both ways. 4) Prefer to chiffonade rather than tear herbs. 5) If skin on eggplant is a bit tough out of oven, no worries; softens to 'just right' when tossed in tomatoes. 6) Great room temp.

Thnx to others' notes, made couple of changes. Result just right dry sweet - salty - spicy treats. 1) Toasted raw nuts a little in saucepan first. 2) Removed pan from flame b/4 adding water, sugar etc to avoid splatter. 3) @1 tsp kosher salt cinnamon + @ 1/8 tsp New Mexico chili powder added to cinnmon. 4) Silicon spatula to stir gets sugar out pan edges so no burn. 5) @12 min for nice dry coating. 6) Lower flame as nuts cook to dry without burning sugar. 8) Sprinkle w/ Maldon salt.

Lyric of the Billy Joel classic comes to mind. "I love you just the way you are..." Well. Almost. I did bake the meatballs rather than fry them and sized them to get 28 total not 36. Otherwise made per recipe, used ground lamb for protein, with result of splendidly seasoned, tender meatballs. The sauce is brothy and perfect here. Made the couscous but used fregola. Golden raisins great counterpoint to spices in meat and broth. Excellent day 2 too. A Keeper!

Delicious as written. I decided to play with it too. Honey mixture makes splendid glaze for oven roasted salmon filet and then finished with the lime juice and sprinkled with jalapenos and scallions. Ditto with cubed and baked sweet potatoes. (Seeing purists in the mix for whether 'cleaned' shrimp means deveined, peel the sweet potatoes first.) Toss with honey mixture at last 5 or 10 minutes of baking to avoid honey burn then finish per recipe by tossing with the rest.

Made per recipe except I toasted pasillas in dry skillet over medium flame rather than charring them using tongs over open flame. Simple reason is I'm a klutz with the tong technique. Starting with pan toasted instead of flame charred chiles, remainder per recipe. Fantabulous flavor in chili salsa with the avocado tacos. I can think of all kinds of things to use with remaining salsa. Note: Tomatillos only took 7 min to cook, not 15 of recipe, for me.

My household loves slaw of all kinds and in general prefer all salads lightly dressed. Yet for this slaw we found ourselves wanting to increase the dressing by roughly half. Also for balance of ingredients we wanted to reduce cabbage by about half. In the "what kind of pickle juice" debate, we split the difference and used an artisan bread and butter pickle juice. We liked the apples included here. Good enough to have made once. Not so good as to be a keeper for us.

Keeper. Made per recipe. Weighed all ingredients where gram weights indicated. 10" cake pan perfect size. Topping not as expected for texture but very good. Re filling: Blue, black and raspberries combo/ used 3 TBSP sugar from 2-4 range and @ 3/4 TBSP tapioca. Seemed just right. Re topping: Took @65 min in my oven to get crispness / color(vs recipe 55 min.). Texture was coarse-sandy not clumps as expected per recipe notes. Crispy & stayed crisp but in fine not coarse crumb.

Once read comments by Chef Ottolenghi that went like this: hummus could be the glue that holds the Middle East together. Clever given there are so many variations to enjoy across countries and faiths. A Jordanian acquaintance taught me using baking soda, lots of tahini and lemon juice are secrets to great hummus. But this recipe was not my fav. Too much tahini and not enough lemon. Much prefer the Zahav’s Hummus ‘Tehina’ published by Melissa Clark. It remains my 'go to'.

Made per recipe. Started with two eggplant, 1# each. Using option to roast in oven at hottest possible heat, I chose 500 degrees. Took just 25 min "minus" for it to be done. Liked the flavors and pungency very much. Added just a little more lime juice. Looked just like photo at outcome. I'm a big fan of eggplant but not sure I liked the texture of the mashed (with fork) eggplant here. No fault of the recipe. Just different strokes for different palates.

Fantastic flavors. Easy. Made twice. Per recipe & w/ ‘quick cook’ changes. 1) This is hot with specified cayenne. Reduce by half if ‘heat sensitive’. 2) Sub parsley for those who don’t like cilantro flavor. We added 50% more cilantro. 3) Quick cook; sub good diced canned tomatoes for fresh & use can (15 oz) of drained & rinsed garbanzos for dried and soaked. Starting w/ dry is better but if you forget to soak the beans, I did 2nd time, this works.

comments.recipe_name: cold sesame noodles with sweet peppers
comments.comment: Made per recipe. So many recipes so little time; likely not a repeat. "It's not you, it's me." My palate wanted something more in dressing. Maybe a clove of garlic Microplane grated into it. Maybe a little honey. Maybe lime juice instead of rice wine vinegar. Different things please different palates. Notes: 1) Microplane the ginger for similar intensity as juice w/ less work. 2) Brewed black tea can be subbed for broth / water. 3) Don't skip black sesame seeds!

Fantastic flavors and very easy to do. The tamarind mixture elevates even lowly inexpensive rockfish, which I used, to be special. Made per recipe. Notes: 1) If using dried cranberries, find good ones, not 'craisin' quality, and coarse chop them...barberries are smaller. 2) Grating the garlic is best. 2) Used cilantro, chives, parsley and mint together. Great combo with the tamarind. 3) The squeeze of fresh lime and additional minced herbs as garnish are just right.

Using a Microplane to grate garlic into a tahini sauce as this recipe calls for makes all the difference to soften the edges of raw garlic flavor. No need to do anything more complicated in steps than just grate. Fan of proportion of lemon juice to tahini here. Have also omitted cumin at times and used ground Aleppo instead. We use this sauce in so many ways. Try it drizzled over oven roasted cauliflower "steaks" then sprinkle with sumac, sliced scallions and cilantro.
Delectable. I prefer salt reduced to 2 teaspoons. Otherwise as written.

Having made 3 times now, I'd offer 2 tips in addition to my first to reduce the salt to 2 tsp (or maybe 1 1/2). Those tips are: 1) Watch the time closely and pay attention to color and aroma as this cooks. In one pass at this, using someone else's kitchen, 25 minutes was plenty of time to get this done. 2) Stir in the coconut chips after the first 10 or 25 minutes of baking time. They will brown up faster than the other ingredients.

For people posting who are put off by the garlic powder, you might try grating a small garlic clove with a Microplane instead. Thanks to a Melissa Clark recipe some time back, I learned this tip and it seems to make all the difference when using raw garlic to soften it. It works 'magic' in a way pressing or mincing doesn't. But the point of this particular recipe is to mimic Ranch'ish style so garlic powder would fit in with that.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with roast chicken currants and pine nuts
comments.comment: Technique is easy and the outcome was delicious. Currants are great texture and flavor point. As much as I try to make 'per recipe' the first time through, I changed this to sub toasted chopped walnuts for pine nuts (guest preference) and I did not use the chicken skin. "It's not you it's me"; not a skin fan unless crispy. Glad we tried it and provides ideas for using leftover roast chicken and other herbs in similar dish keeping nuts and currants.

Simple to make & simply delicious; reliable every time recipe delivers dish as welcome at breakfast or teatime or dessert after dinner. We enjoy the texture of the almond plus wheat flours used together. Neither too custardy nor cakey. And just sweet enough but not too much. Also tried with today with frozen boysenberries. Didn't thaw berries first, reduced cook temp to 350 degrees and upped cook time to 60 min. "ish" for just right brown top & firm middle.

My fav of all curried Waldorfs I've made; it is cumin that makes this especially delicious. Notes: *For any Waldorf I like to mix all BUT nuts and parsley together to "get to know each other" as they chill in fridge a bit. Did that here too. It also helps hydrate raisins. *Adding nuts / parsley just before serving keeps both fresh *For dressing base used 2T mayo + 1/4 C yogurt + enough buttermilk (maybe 3T) to thin as desired *Broke up walnut halves after toasting

comments.recipe_name: rice pilaf with pistachios and almonds
comments.comment: Having tried rosewater in several other dishes to learn it isn't a flavor my household enjoys, I omitted it here. With that exception, made per recipe using dried apricots. Used California apricots, which seem a little more difficult to find these days, and not Turkish ones as we like the more tart flavor provided by the California variety. The aroma from the spices blooming in the ghee is luscious. So is the flavor of this as side dish. Great paired with lamb.

comments.recipe_name: white peaches pistachios honey and ricotta
comments.comment: Simply delicious. The kind of ricotta used will make a big difference here. If you can find Bel Gioioso's Ricotta Con Latte it is a delectable option in this recipe. I prefer yellow stone fruits over white ones so used yellow nectarines. Omitted the olive oil and it was still a winner. Although I'm considering trying this with sectioned (supremed) blood oranges come citrus season and I may add the olive oil then.

An experienced cook but the world's worst cooker of rice, I managed have even this brilliantly fool proof recipe be not quite right. To it I say, "It's not you. It's me." Used white long grain basmati and followed recipe. Even good advice of some to use foil between the pot and lid for tight fit before putting in oven. At 17 min baking /10 min rest it was perfectly fluffy but over cooked by just an eensie. Next time, I'll do 15 min in oven.

comments.recipe_name: sauteed brussels sprouts and apple with prosciutto
comments.comment: As with all Melissa Clark recipes I've tried, this is reliable and flexible for the cook. Used the Pecorino instead of the prosciutto for the vegetarian version only because I had the former in house and not the latter. Subbed local orchard Honeycrisps that were gifts from friends for the specified Golden Delicious. Outcome was delightful. Although added less salt than recipe suggested as I knew cheese would add a fair amount at end. A keeper.

comments.recipe_name: savory clafoutis with corn and swiss chard
comments.comment: With nod to Fall equinox, I small diced Sugar Pie pumpkin and hot oven roasted the tiny cubes to be tender to sub for corn kernels (2C). Omitted dill. Otherwise, made per recipe. In 10” straight sided skillet. Husband’s review as he ate; “This is EXCELLENT.” Pretty too. As usual for Melissa Clark recipes, reliable and versatile for home cooks. A keeper I’ll have fun "playing with" every season. Next time may sub sour cream or full fat Greek Yogurt for crème fraiche.

Very good, easy. NOTES: 1) Some say too bitter. Can mitigate in two ways. a) choose lower IBU (International Bitterness Unit) beer. Belgian style dark abbey as called for is pretty low; maybe 25 IBU. Get above 40 IBU & bitterness will come out. b) Use 1 bottle + 8 oz water instead of 2 bottles. Worked for me. 2) Remember cheddar will add lots of salt at end. 3) Cornstarch does NOT make this gummy. It helps cheese melt evenly & creates smooth mouth feel.
4) Omitted optional bacon. 5) Used stone ground brown mustard.

comments.recipe_name: crispy lamb with cumin scallions and red chiles
comments.comment: Made this per recipe. Weighing in on the cumin debate as part of this thread, thought the amount of cumin called for was just right for the dish. I'd use half the amount of salt called for in the lamb marinade if making again.

Can't think of anything simpler for a week day meal than the perfectly baked potatoes this recipe delivers. Love the warm oven on a cool evening and the aroma of the potatoes baking. The time they take is just enough time to change clothes and sip a glass of wine while chopping or prepping whatever it is you'll use for topping. Only change for me from recipe is I make a little "X" in middle then push from both ends to open it up for maximum fluffy goodness.

comments.recipe_name: cauliflower and tomato frittata with feta
comments.comment: Made per recipe for ingredients & with 10" skillet. A splendid blend of flavors. Great texture & color too. Used thyme option to start then parsley & mint as fresh herb combo. Take time needed to get tomato sauce to thick pasty texture. Makes all the difference in outcome. After step 5 I finished this in oven & not under broiler. Approx 35 min or so at 325 degrees worked in my oven for being done all the way through and puffy. Definitely a keeper!

comments.recipe_name: roasted butternut squash and red onions
comments.comment: Subbed Sugar Pie pumpkin (peeled) for squash and pepitas for pine nuts. Otherwise per recipe. Definitely a keeper. Used this as main dish although it could be a side. NOTES: 1) Tahini sauce is great with this. Don't leave it out. 2) Same for mint, cilantro, parsley mixed herbs. 3) ) I'd cut salt by half next time. Salt was over the top for our palates. 4) Pumpkin was done in about 30 min., 35 for onions. 4) Next time I'd reduce heat to 425 degrees.

Surprised at great flavor to be had from such a simple and short simmer broth! Very versatile in any recipe calling for mushroom or veggie broth. Inexpensive too given it takes only 4 grams dry porcini. NOTES: 1) I've subbed a good size leek for the onions & scallion with good outcome. 2) A few whole black peppercorns added in is good too. 3) Partially covering during simmer helps maintain volume but in general plan on 3 'plus' Cups yield. 4) Freezes well.

comments.recipe_name: roasted butternut squash and red onions
comments.comment: 5) A few fresh dates chopped and sprinkled on top are also a good finishing add.

Really delicious, versatile as sauce with roasted veggies or fish, dip with crudite, pasta salad dressing, tossed with rice or potatoes & as crostini spread. Holds well in fridge. NOTES: 1) California apricots are best here, not Turkish. 2) 2 cloves garlic is plenty. 3) BIG pinch for cayenne. 4) Olive oil works in place of walnut oil & reduce oil to 4T. 5) Prefer a bit more cilantro & bit less parsley. 6) Tarragon only in place of mixed herbs works & reduce to 3T.

comments.recipe_name: coconut pork stew with garam masala
comments.comment: Fan of Melissa Clark recipes as I find them to be both foolproof and delicious. This one was the latter but although I made it per recipe the yellow split peas never did soften up as expected. Even after 3 hrs of cook time - extended it to try to get them tender - & even when reheated on day 2. Very good reheated. Equally as good w/out the garlic oil. Used lots of cilantro as garnish. So many recipes & so little time not sure it will make it to repeat file. But glad I tried it.

Wonderful flavors. Some prep work but easy execution. Made per recipe for soup. Simplified serving. NOTES; A. For SOUP: 1. Started wiw/David Tanis' Porcini Broth 2. Did not remove 1/3 cup mushrooms. (See service). 3. Next time will reduce clove and ginger to 1/4 tsp each. B. For SERVICE: 1. Didn't broil per recipe. Instead made simple crostini in oven with thin gruyere melted on. Floated on soup. 2. A very little finely chopped parsley on top. Chives would work too.

comments.recipe_name: black bean chorizo casserole with pickled onions
comments.comment: Made per recipe minus chorizo. Fantastiic flavors. Lots of work (clean up too) but worth it. Soaked & cooked my own beans. Next time canned would be fine. Cilantro stems in bean simmer is 'it'. Used homemade chicken broth & think good broth is key to flavor as this uses a quart total. So, "Say no to the box. :-) " Pasilla chile sauce is excellent; can use that recipe for other things. For short cut; make elements day ahead and layer and bake day of.

I'm a Manhattan kinda girrrr......woman. Although I've been old enough to imbibe for several decades now, I've just never had an Old Fashioned. But saw this recipe and had all the ingredients so whipped up a couple for my hubby and me last night. Delightful. Easy enough that even a no mixology skills person like me can make them. I think I'll make us another round tonight. It was a long election season. Maybe it will all make more sense if we drink enough of these.

In household of cranberry sauce fan(atics) this may be one of all time favs. Too lazy to grind pequin chiles, I subbed approx. 1/4 tsp cayenne. Not as complex flavor as pequin or as many Scovilles for heat but a good option. Also used a big pinch of ground cloves rather than grinding own for that. We prefer cranberry sauce more tart so skimped just a bit on the sugar. All perfect for us. Next time may simmer 2 whole pequins in at cook stage then remove them at cool down. An easy keeper!

NOTES: 1) Made full size, half size & 1/3 of recipe. Delicious every time. 2) Set aside 1C or more hot potato cooking water b/4 draining. Add it in at mash step as needed to get to desired consistency. 3) Adjust final salt in dish as needed given cooking water is salted. 4) 1/3 recipe (2# potatoes) feeds 6 as side. 5) If making ahead remove from fridge at least 90 min before baking. then add crumbs. 6) Bake 350 degrees not 400 - for 40 min. to make crumbs golden / no risk of burning.

As Dana Carvey's immortal character'"The Church Lady' might ask "Well, isn't that special?" The turkey outcome using this recipe. Because while ours wasn't as woeful as Burt Spielman's funny review of his experience (laughing WITH you, Burt, not at you), it was not what we expected. And here I should ground what will be the start of a two screen review by saying my husband and I are experienced home cooks and fans of Melissa Clark. Next...

Used 14# fresh turkey. Gollowed recipe to a "T", using Orange-Bacon-Chile option minus bacon, dry brined 36 hours. Removed from fridge for 90 min then seared in roaster, placed on lowest oven rack at temp spec'd for 30 min then lowered temp for another 75. Two reads in each turkey thighs at 165 and breast 160. Let rest 30 min, went to carve and breast still way pink at bone. Back in oven to finish breast alone. Flavors good but skin too pliable and dark meat tough. Alas.

Three's a charm...definitely the last note. "Followed" not "Gollowed" in should have read my previous tome. Also to say the pan jus from this was luscious. Using the Orange-Chilie rub version returns a disquieting orange color to drippings but when strained into a fat separator, the resulting de-fatted jus is a lovely amber color and the orange stuff is tossed. Really great flavored pan jus for gravy or on its' own or in turkey tossed with noodle leftovers.

comments.recipe_name: mushroom spinach soup with cinnamon coriander and cumin
comments.comment: A couple of minor changes, otherwise made as written. We really enjoyed this for the earthiness of the mushrooms combined with the Middle Eastern spices. Getting the mushrooms well browned /caramelized really is a key step in this dish. Don't rush it. Leftovers delicious reheated next day. Changes: 1) Used half butter and have EVOO in browning step. 2) Reduced salt by a lot. 3) Did not use spinach.

Fantabulous flavors. So simple to make it works for"Tuesday dinner" or to serve guests. Like many braises we think this is best made a day ahead & reheated. (Also provides opportunity to remove fat layer.) Made as written but for these: 1) Olive oil sub for butter. 2) Water sub for chix stock. 3) Reduce water / liquid to 1 C. "plus". 4) After bringing to simmer on stovetop, transfer (covered) to 325 degree oven for 30 min then 275 degree for 1 1/2 hr.

Be sure to use some of the tender celery leaves from those inside stalks tossed into this salad. In fact, I think it looks like there are some celery leaves in the photo although not called out specifically in the ingredients list along with the celery. Some folks made good recommendations to "soften" the red onion flavor. My hubby is Siclian and we actually like it in "rough" form here when thinly sliced. This is a particularly great salad with escarole in the mix.

comments.recipe_name: greek chicken stew with cauliflower and olives
comments.comment: Used chicken legs & made per recipe for ingredients. Good enough one dish meal to be glad I tried it. Won't repeat. Best for those eaters who like subtle flavors. NOTES: 1) Instead of stove top, I finished in oven like this: at step 4 also added cauliflower & after bringing all to simmer covered & braised in 325 degree oven for 20 min. then lowered to 275 for 1hr. 2) After removing from oven stirred in olives & parsley. 3) Good made day ahead. Leftovers keep well.

comments.recipe_name: tortilla soup with roasted cauliflower rice
comments.comment: Very tasty. Made per recipe for ingredients. Only change in technique from as written was to toast tortilla strips in oven. Really liked the "riced"' cauliflower in this. Will use the technique for other things too. Chipotle powder in the cauliflower was a great flavor profile add. But noting we are big fans of spicy food, I'd say for many audiences you may want to cut the chipotle in half, to 1/2 tsp. Also, plan on this taking more like 2 hrs start to finish, not 1.

comments.recipe_name: sweet potatoes with yogurt and cilantro chile sauce
comments.comment: Easy and excellent when made as written. Used serrano chiles for the green chiles. If serving it as part of a family style or buffet table service it is as delicious when it starts to cool as it is hot. Don't know that it needs the drizzle of oil in step 4. Love yogurt with it but if serving lactose intolerant diners it is just as good without yogurt as with. Made it to our "keeper" file. PS - If you don't have blanched almonds in the house, sliced ones work well too.

comments.recipe_name: baked romanesco broccoli with mozzarella and olives
comments.comment: Made per recipe. Outcome was delicious. Agree you COULD make with plain cauliflower but the Romanesco is really THE thing for this. Next time I'll reduce both cheeses by almost half. But first time with a recipe I always like to do as written for a true 'test drive'. If serving anchovy averse diners, I think it would be fine to leave them out since this has so much going on for pungent flavor. But if so, I'd up the capers...maybe double. A keeper.

comments.recipe_name: braised lamb with red wine and prunes
comments.comment: Shoulder chops work well in place of lamb shoulder cut up. Increase from 2 lbs by a bit to allow for weight of bone in chops if using them. Doubled cinnamon as several suggested. Otherwise 'as written' for the 1st 'test drive'. Excellent flavors. Even better made day ahead. Leftovers are great. Next time I'd add a pinch or two of Aleppo or Cayenne pepper. Otherwise a keeper for sure as written!

comments.recipe_name: mini almond cakes with chocolate or cherry
comments.comment: Someone asked about Kosher confectioner's sugar that does not contain cornstarch in an earlier string so that these might work for Passover. I made these using Whole Foods 360 brand confectioners sugar & noticed it uses tapioca starch, not cornstarch, for sugar stabilizer. Cassava, source of tapioca, is a root like potato so should be Passover friendly. The sugar was marked U on bag so should fit for Passover use. But note the almond cake recipe also contains cornstarch.

Made this without the cream (add a little more water to make up for volume) and marshmallows. Also used about half the butter. Used an immersion blender to puree so texture not as smooth. Not exactly per original recipe but it was the inspiration for using all the other ingredients on hand for a easy, nutritious, delicious, "school night" meal for four. Belgian style beer or ale is really good in this given the low bitterness level. Made it to our "keeper" file.

comments.recipe_name: roasted cauliflower with ras el hanout
comments.comment: Good outcome for creamy texture of wedges at service. 1) Cauliflower head was small'ish side of medium so used 2 qt saucepan & approx 1 qt milk covered nicely. But head wanted to float to top so turned it upside down for braising step. Took 40 min to tender. 3) Given smallish head, used less butter and spice but made per recipe for technique. 4) Let come to room temp before roasting & 35 min was just right time. 5) Leftover milk delicious for cheese sauce next day meal.

comments.recipe_name: mini almond cakes with chocolate or cherry
comments.comment: Delectable flavors & texture. Keeper! 1) Made per recipe for cakes but modified to use neither ganache nor cherry jam for center. Instead used an Amerena cherry. 2) Experience similar to what others shared. At 10 min bake time too firm to push down gently. Would reduce this first bake time to 5 min. next time. 3) Divided perfectly between 12 muffin cups - well buttered first. Cakes released AOK with a little coaxing when still slightly warm. 4) Best day 1 but good day 2.

Oh me of little faith...soaked the beans the night before. I buy black beans at my CoOp where they always seem fresh. I cook them often. I was not believing they'd cook in an hour or two without pre-soak. Good thing. Because they took two hours and "a bit" to tender stage for this soup. Other than pre-soak step, made per recipe. Good, layered in flavors, just spicy enough. Start slowly with the salt at end. 1 1/2 tsp was enough for us.

My Brooklyn born and raised husband is a longtime Manhattan cocktail fan. Sometime during the 2016 Prez primary cycle when Lindsay Graham suggested if one started drinking during happy hour and had enough alcohol that Trump might start to make sense, we decided to put this cocktail recipe in our daily repertoire. Great with rye (hubby) or bourbon (me). We use just a tick less vermouth. And Amerena cherries. I like mine on rocks. Great drink. But did not work for that making sense thing.

Easy, delicious, healthy, fast. Made 2X; 1X per recipe & 1X w/slight modifications. Thanks to person who suggested a parm rind added in for the simmer. Worked well. Second pass at this I subbed thin sliced onion (@ 1 med.) for leeks. Also used 12 oz bottle hard cider (apple) instead of 2C stock & made up additional 4 oz. w/ addt'l. water. Added cider after cabbage caramelized & let simmer @ 5 min before step 2. Only took cabbage @ 15 min to caramelize both times.

comments.recipe_name: ande ki kari eggs in spicy tomato sauce
comments.comment: This sauce would make shoe leather taste good. It is a simply splendid sauce for flavor. If you don't eat eggs, try it on veggies or rice or flatbreads of any kind or steamed cauliflower or...the list is endless for possibilities. In my opinion, grating the fresh tomatoes as described in the recipe makes a good amount of difference. I wouldn't modify that step. The aaaahhhh...roma of this as it cooks is an added bonus.

comments.recipe_name: hot italian sausage and broccoli rabe frittata
comments.comment: A new "go to" for one of my fav veg, rapini (broccoli rabe) to introduce others to it. Made 3 times, 2 per recipe and 1 w/out mozzarella. Prefer w/out. Also recommend Viola Buitoni's method of cooking the rapini. Perfect outcome every time to balance natural bitterness of the veg. Cook rapini like this then proceed with the recipe. Delicious. http://zesterdaily.com/cooking/dont-bitter-just-cook-rapini-better/ Also found leftovers for this frittata travel and reheat well.

My 'go to'. Made at least 8 times now and also tried original Zav recipe (from the restaurant) twice. I prefer the tahini amount of the original (that is 2/3 C tahini to 1 C here) but often use more than 1/3 C lemon juice. I prefer this recipe for microplane grating in garlic cloves (I use 2) as opposed to original recipe of whirling the unpeeled garlic & lemon juice together then straining juice. I sometimes add Aleppo pepper in mixture & always sumac (not paprika) on top.

comments.recipe_name: malted milk fudge ripple ice cream
comments.comment: Ingredient notes: 1) King Arthur makes good malted milk powder. http://www.kingarthurflour.com/shop/items/malted-milk-powder-16-oz Our CoOp carries it in the baking section. Available at online retailers if you can't find malted milk powder at your local stores. 2) Barley Malt Syrup makes a difference here. Worth the hunt! Aunt Patty's brand (from Glory Bee /Eugene OR)is a good one. https://glorybee.com/barley-malt-syrup Eden makes one too.

Our finished dish looked precisely like recipe photo. And we made it per recipe. Luscious lamb ribs. Great glaze. Splendid sprinkle. (DON'T skip it.) Yummy yogurt. (Although, next time I'll use 2% yogurt and no crème fraiche. Pretty rich as written for lamb partner.) Still the whole lotta work was not quite what we expected for final; the sum was less than the whole of the parts. Note: Lamb ribs are messier than pork ribs by far. Know well your guests when serving! :-)

Very good. Italian in-laws would agree with author that guanciale is key for authenticity. Also a milder percorino than Romano. When we lived in NYC and SF areas these were easier to come by. In the 'wilds' of Boise where we are now...hahahaha. No. Not here. So pancetta & percorino romano it is. But no garlic. (FIL allergic so may be why.) Bucatini needs to be cooked "justa" to stay on the fork. Too firm & it will slap you with sauce as you try to twirl it.

comments.recipe_name: a big pot of simmered pintos
comments.comment: After soaking the beans overnight I drain them and start with fresh water. About 10 cups of water seems to be the right ratio for a pound of beans every time.

One of the lovely things about Bittman's recipes is they allow the user to make it as written or experiment with their own version and always have a delicious dish because of good foundation. I made this maybe half a dozen ways last season and served it at all times of the day (even breakfast). Rhubarb alone as written. Rhubarb with berries. Apples and berries with no rhubarb. Pecans. Walnuts. Thick sliced almonds. So good each time. Less sugar each time seemed best.

comments.recipe_name: tostadas with smashed black beans or vaqueros
comments.comment: Crisped the tortillas in oven instead of microwave (see Sara's tip on this thread.) These were delicious with recipe as written! And in poster LeslieMac being a person after my own tastebuds and appetite, I also found that one recipe served my husband and me with just a little leftover for a snack. I'd say stretching this to 4 - 8 servings per recipe works for more restrained appetites, but this was just SO good and healthy too so we didn't feel guilty.

comments.recipe_name: baked beans with pomegranate molasses walnuts and chard
comments.comment: Extraordinarily delicious made per recipe and what makes this even yummier is that it is so darned easy to do. Aside from the need to plan ahead by soaking the beans (overnight for me), this goes together in a snap. There are several white bean varieties and I prefer the tiny ones over the larger ones, so tiny it was in this recipe too. It may be even better warm than hot, but either way all good! Leftovers, if you have any, are wonderful too.

comments.recipe_name: pizza with caramelized onions ricotta and chard
comments.comment: Our CoOp having "Chard-a-palooza" with abundance of chard options. Fun to have all these great chard recipes with which to experiment; this one a 'keeper' for sure. Made as written with the exception of eliminating egg from ricotta...held without it...and add of fresh grated nutmeg and black pepper to same. Dusting of additional grated Parmesan over the top of the pizza and some crushed red pepper too is a nice touch out of oven. Onions in this are particularly good.

comments.recipe_name: cumin lamb meatballs with tahini yogurt dipping sauce
comments.comment: Delicious combination of flavors. And just because I have never really learned to use a broiler well, in spite of "40plus" years of home cooking, I started the meatballs by browning in a hot skillet on gas stove top and finished them baking in the oven. Loved the tahini-yogurt sauce with this. But if you're not a fan of tahini, which is likely true for folks who didn't care for it, just use the yogurt. (The photo with recipe looks like yogurt only for color.)

Not usually a fan of oatmeal cookies but any recipe with Chef Ottolenghi's name + tahni & halvah gets my attention. Good thing because they are splendidly delicious! Made twice, 1st time per recipe. Second time subbing thick cut sliced almonds for hazelnuts. Delicious both ways. Chopped pistachios would be good too. Cookies come together easily and just as recipe says they will. Made 20 both times. Left off chocolate drizzle 2nd time. Like flavor best w/out chocolate. Keeper!

1) Love tamarind & garam masala; addictively good flavor combo. 2) BUT way more fat from butter than expected. Nut mixture laying in little butter puddle as it cooled. Tricky to serve for guests' greasy fingers. 3) May try again - cutting butter to 3T and so on from there to see how that impacts outcome. 4) Fresh mint chiffonade great accent but makes nuts more difficult to serve / eat. Try mint drink instead like Julep, Mojito, Israeli Mint Lemonade, Mint iced tea...

comments.recipe_name: pork chops with tamarind and ginger
comments.comment: Made per recipe with great outcome. Cooking time will vary, of course, depending on thickness of chops and your preference for doneness. Don't skip the scallions and lime wedges for garnish and serving as they add that just right finishing touch.

Made per recipe. Got 8 just right sized servings. Great flavors in all elements. But let's talk about the biscuit shortcakes rising because, like others who posted here, mine didn't. Flavor was wonderful (used lime for citrus zest), they were nicely short and good crumb. But did not rise much. Baking powder was in date. I've been making biscuits since I was a kid so not a novice at it. But... Still would make these again for flavor. Great by themselves with coffee.
Should have said in previously posted comment, had 12 biscuits but I served 8 with the remainder of the recipe. Hence extras of the wonderfully flavored biscuits for tea or coffee companions.

I have always been a crisp-kinda-person who didn't get the allure of buckles. Until I tried this one. So good. Maybe it is the higher ratio of fruit in this than most recipes. Or the combination of lemon zest and nutmeg in the mix. Delicious any time of day. Serve for breakfast, lunch, tea time, dinner. Take it to a cookout or picnic or party. So easy to do. Made as written it is just right. PS - if yours sticks, see tips from some posters on how to mitigate that.

comments.recipe_name: roasted halibut with lemons olives and rosemary
comments.comment: Made as written but for eliminating rosemary. Very simple to make and simply delicious to eat. While you might substitute another chili powder, the fruity, moderate heat of Aleppo really is great here. Worth the search for it. We buy ours at an Afghani market but places like Savory Spice Shop and Penzey's sell it too as do several on Amazon. It's a staple in our kitchen these days. Took about 20 minutes for the halibut to reach level of doneness we prefer.

comments.recipe_name: creamy polenta with parmesan and sausage
comments.comment: This is unique for technique. Bittman starts polenta in a cup of cold water then adds more water as it cooks & stirs little. Most recipes bring all water to a boil & whisk in polenta & stir often. This recipe works splendidly! Took @ 21min for med. grind polenta to be perfect. (Watch the video) Can be topped with whatever you like...sausage, sautéed mushrooms, roasted veg., ragu... Leave out parm & sub a bit of ricotta & use with honey or maple syrup for breakfast.

Delicious to eat, simple to make, pretty color to serve and, like all Bittman recipes, versatile. Prefer putting all peas in food processor / blender for texture. Eliminated pine nuts. (Can't use them.) Great with mint but if out can also sub a couple tsp. fresh thyme and crushed red pepper (Calabrian or Aleppo are nice) for a flavor twist. Like to serve on crostini. Added bonus - peas are nutrition powerhouse for antioxidants & protein (8g/C.). What's not to love?

comments.recipe_name: monkfish roasted with herbs and olives
comments.comment: What a great tip to tie the monkfish, as one would a pork tenderloin, to tuck in the short end and help create a more uniform shape for even cooking. At 15 minutes cook time and after a 5 minute out of oven rest this was perfect for doneness and texture. Easy to make. Great flavors. Handsome to serve. For folks new to monkfish, the recipe assumes your fishmonger has cleaned it of the membrane surrounding the tail when it is fileted. If not done, you'll need to do that first.

Minus the mushrooms, nearly doubled the crab, used fresh Dungeness, lemon juice filled in for ponzu...all good! The little bit of butter at the end really helps accent the crab flavor along with the scatter of tarragon. Lovely.

Chris Kimball's new magazine Milk Street did feature on Israeli Hummus two issues ago, The recipe here is still my 'go to' (with tahini redux to 2/3 C) BUT Milk Street cook method for garbanzos is great to use with this. 1. 1C beans soaked in 8C water + 2 TBSP salt not baking soda. 2. Drain & cook in 10C water + 1/2 tsp soda. Beans get to overcooked stage needed in about 40 minutes. 3. Drain to use in this recipe. Tried 3 times & creamy, smooth outcome in this.

comments.recipe_name: muhammara red pepper and walnut spread
comments.comment: Muhammara is a favorite dish in our home. We've tried lots of versions. This recipe is our favorite. First time we've added scallions. Excellent marriage of flavors. Some reviewers say they used jarred peppers or honey or red wine vinegar in place of ingredients specified in recipe and were happy with the results. Maybe so. But those ingredients do not make this recipe. For what it's worth, we made this as written, the ingredients are staples for us, and it is delectable.

comments.recipe_name: lasagna with tomato sauce and roasted eggplant
comments.comment: Made as written with one exception. Didn't use no boil pasta but parboiled (for 4 min) De Cecco #1 lasagna noodles, then proceeded per recipe using them. Due to parboiled noodles, didn't need to cover when baking. But all ingredients and all proportions per recipe otherwise. Simply excellent for outcome. Easy too since I did the eggplant and marinara (made my own) one day and assembled and baked the next. Rectangular pan 11.5 x 7.5. Don't omit the tiny cinnamon pinch!

Seeing the numbers of folks who tried this and found it tasteless, I'll weigh in with the opposite. Made this per recipe the first time per recipe. Then per recipe minus chard the second just because I didn't have any in the kitchen so made it as a lentil "stoup".. We found it quite flavor filled both times from the bouquet garni, veg and the cheese the recipe calls for to finish it. One doesn't need to have tastebuds whalloped for things to be tasty.

Made as written, minus the optional eggs, this is terrific for a simple meal. My Italian heritage husband grew up in a Brooklyn household with his Italian speaking grandmother in charge of the kitchen and for the hundreds of times, it seems at least, that we've made sausage and peppers in his family's tradition, we never thought to try red wine vinegar for finishing the peppers. That tasty touch coupled with the technique for cooking the sausages makes this a "keeper"!

comments.recipe_name: butter steamed broccoli with peppery bread crumbs
comments.comment: Splendid in simplicity and flavor. Made as written making one half a recipe and it served 4 nicely as veggie side. For those who used panko crumbs and then commented there were too many bread crumbs, that would be so if you used panko. Using fresh crumbs made per recipe it was just right. I think there may be a typo in recipe, though. Note 1 lg. or 2 med. heads of broccoli are called out as 1/2 to 2 lb. I think that should probably be 1 1/2 - 2 lb. Keeper.

Pretty to look at and pretty perfect to eat. Made per recipe. Used fresh Dungeness. The tarragon and chives are particularly lovely 'partners' to support crab here and the red bell pepper too. Noting some objected to this being called a quiche since it lacks cheese, call it a custard if you like. By any name it will be taste-bud-tempting.
At our house we love bourbon. And Aperol. And now this drink with the recipe just as written. Yum. We wouldn't change a thing.

We were served the ricotta with a tomato marmellata (marmalade) alongside instead of the roasted red pepper atop. Grilled pieces of bruschetta sized bread accompanied for spreading on both. Delicious. To share that the ricotta part of the recipe is great to use that way as well. (The preserved lemon rind option was included.) I'm a roasted pepper fan so plan to try this as written for that presentation. But ricotta piece comes in handy for other things too.

comments.recipe_name: hot italian sausage and broccoli rabe frittata
comments.comment: Adding to my earlier comments, this is now my "take to a desert island" recipe. Made more than half a dozen times, each time with excellent outcome. (I do cook the rapini to start in step 1 per Viola Buitoni's technique: http://zesterdaily.com/cooking/dont-bitter-just-cook-rapini-better ) Great for B L or D served hot, warm or even room temp. Travels well. Reheats well. Can be 1 dish meal. Plays well w/ sparkling wine which I'll take to that desert island too. Perfect.

comments.recipe_name: spiced lamb and rice with walnuts mint and pomegranate
comments.comment: Made as written using optional pomegranate molasses (one of my fav ingredients) & cooking dried chickpeas rather than using canned. Good enough to be glad we tried it. But with so many recipes & so little time, this didn't make 'keeper' file. Don't skip the mint & pomegranate seeds at end. They add that "little something" to finish. Was less sold on the butter toasted walnuts. Really good on their own but seemed to get lost in this.

David Tanis' rapini recipes are genius! See also his Sausage & Broccoli Rabe Fritatta. But for this recipe as for the other, I recommend cooking the rapini in the blanch step (3) per Viola Buitoni's method, http://zesterdaily.com/cooking/dont-bitter-just-cook-rapini-better/ , then proceed with the remaining steps here. Broccoli Rabe is a winter veg so this is the perfect time of year to find it in markets. If you sub another veg it is not anywhere the same.

Since November 2016 I have become a fan of Mr. Simonson's libation recipes and tried several. Always with this question in mind when I choose one: "Will this make the turkey more palatable?" This is not to say the turkey we eat at the Thanksgiving table. But the turkey we find in the Oval Office. Being a gin fan, made Tuxedos for hubby and me for news watching one night. Good enough to have tried once but one and done. So many more to try. If my liver holds up before 2020.

comments.recipe_name: herbed white bean and sausage stew
comments.comment: The culprit for uncooked beans could be hard water as I learned moving to hard water town. From Chris Kimball: "...soaking dried beans in mineral-rich; hard tap water can toughen skins. Some recommend using distilled water to avoid this issue, but we’ve discovered a simpler solution: adding salt to the tap water...prevents the magnesium and calcium in the water from binding to the cell walls...three tablespoons of salt per gallon of soaking water is enough to guarantee soft skins."

Made just as written for ingredients. Great flavors and textures. We like spice and heat and this had both. Finished it in Step 3 with oven braise not on cooktop - prefer oven for ease and control of braise temp. (Using Emile Henri, heavy ceramic, Dutch Oven at 275 for about 1 hour.) We enjoyed a squeeze of fresh lime along with the cilantro when serving.

Good dish. But I see why some had problems with recipe. As written, seem to be some "huh?" in instructions: Step 2) If sauce was bitter, likely chiles were burned. Use pan toast method. Doesn't take long. Dried chiles burn easily. Step 4) Use ample liquid in blender (1C+) then strain mix to remove chile particles. Step 5) Days should be @4C sauce. Add pepper simmer broth as needed. Too little sauce this step may be why some had not enough liquid in completing Step 6.

As much as I cringe to see comments were the recipe was not followed, here goes my own in that vein. I used this filling recipe but subbed dried cherries for the dried cranberries. Luscious filling flavors. Loved the combination of tastes. But I was intimated by the instructions on the crust. So used a standard galette / crostada crust recipe. (You could use one from Melissa Clark's galettes.) For baking, started 375 for 30 minutes, then lowered to 350 for 30 more. Perfect.

comments.recipe_name: baked rigatoni with tomatoes olives and pepper
comments.comment: Made per recipe with one exception: started with can of crushed Italian tomatoes. Then as written from top to bottom of ingredients list and steps. Really good served hot as recommended but also darned delicious at room temp or even out of fridge for breakfast the next morning...assuming you have any leftover. My husband is Italian and we prefer our pastas lightly sauced, as this is. If you prefer the more Americanized standard of more sauce, you'll need to add to this.

This was a fantabulous beverage! Step 1) Needs to be lots of ice in that shaker. Use fresh squeezed juice, of course. I added a splash more juice because it is citrus season and grapefruit is particularly yummy. Step 2) This is cold to hang on to, so I used a stemmed Riedel Bordeaux glass with lots of ice (a la Venetian Spritz) instead of highball. Festive and practical. Ingredients are staples at our house so this will be on rotation for holidays and beyond.

As written, very easy and delicious. Easy "school night" recipe but also special enough for guests. Some suggest grating lemon instead of making zest matchsticks. I think matchsticks shape make a good difference, as written. This will accommodate a lot of black pepper. Easily scalable to cut recipe in half. A nice Prosecco is a good partner here.

Made as written & used Galliano for liquer. Was, well, a 'piece of cake' to make & delicious. Basically a sponge cake made with pistachio 'flour', the texture as expected. Lightly sweet, subtly flavored & nice green-ish color. Used Iranian pistachios (Iran & US vie for 1 - 2 spots in world production) at only $8/lb (shelled) at Middle Eastern market. Good color & flavor & closer in texture to Sicilian ones at fraction of cost. Serve BLD. Keeper.

Days of effort. Worth it for special meal. Made at Easter. Pasta dough is trickiest part to get right for texture & size & get into & out of baking bowl. Italian hubby had fun with that part. I was very glad to delegate. Make meatballs & ragu a day or two ahead. Can hard boil eggs day ahead. Helps to have at least two cooks on board for assembly. Recommend letting it sit more than 30 min - maybe 1 hr - cut too soon & will lose shape. Glad we tried it once.

comments.recipe_name: hot fudge and salted chocolate bits sundae
comments.comment: Re Amy's comment "...there is absolutely no reason to add corn syrup to a ganache." Actually, there is. Not that I'd argue with Dorie Greenspan on anything, including her addition of corn syrup here, but there IS a science to support this as well. Corn syrup, an invert sugar, prevents sugar crystals from forming. So in a liquid ganache, like this, it helps keep the fudge sauce smooth. It also adds a gloss quality.

Tongue in cheek, given I will never engage in the work (and fun perhaps but work for sure) of making this cake nor having a couple of dozen friends in the same place necessary to consume it. But my recipe for this and other similar Russian umpteen-layer, gravity defying cakes is a simple one step. Go to our local Russian immigrant run market where she flies in cakes like this from NYC, frozen, then thaws the to sell by the slice, buy a slice and, well, yum! All the flavor. None of the work!

Worth searching out the dried barberries. They make a distinct difference. Middle East markets have them. If subbing dried cherries, be sure to choose tart NOT Bing cherries and unsweetened. Dried cranberries usually won't be right here as most are "craisin" style with sugar processing and too sweet. But if you can find real, unsweetened dried cranberries those work well and are probably a closer flavor and texture to dried barberries. Honestly Cranberry is one brand.

In previous post I said this was a good dish. I was wrong. It is a great dish. Made 3 times now. Versatile for cooking ahead and serving later for holiday visitors. Shredding turkey meat and returning it to the chili sauce makes a great taco filling. If you get the chiles toasted correctly and simmered to the right softness, the sauce in step 4 will be silky smooth. (Pan toast chiles gently, dry chiles burn easily and then are bitter.) This would probably be great in tamales too.

comments.recipe_name: hot fudge and salted chocolate bits sundae
comments.comment: Splendiferous enough to be a special occasion dessert, we served this as finale for Christmas Eve dinner. Making the hot fudge sauce and chocolate bits is a snap. Both can be done a day head for easy service day of. The salted chocolate bits are scrumptious - used fleur de sel. We had quite a bit of bits leftover - which is a good thing as an encore performance of the sundaes has been requested. Any day can be a special occasion if these are on the menu.

comments.recipe_name: sea scallop salad with meyer lemon and pomegranate
comments.comment: Opening dish for a Christmas Eve dinner...my Italian husband's family tradition is Seven Fishes although we don't make it past three or four these days. This was as delicious to see for the eyes as it was to eat. The Meyer lemon vinaigrette is genius in simplicity and flavor using the tiny (and that is key) diced Meyer. It will be on our "use again soon" list with some Dungeness crab while it is still in season this year.

comments.recipe_name: lamb meatballs with spiced tomato sauce
comments.comment: Splendid make-ahead recipe with even better flavors day 2. 1) Agree with recommendations to double sauce. 2) As written for ingredients but used homemade bread crumbs, not store bought & soaked in cream before adding in. 3) Dislike broiling meats, so baked meatballs in hot (415'ish') oven to brown then finished them simmering in sauce. 4) Feta & Mint at end great touch! 5) Try David Tanis' delicous North African Meatballs (lamb) recipe too if you like this one.

comments.recipe_name: turkey soup with lime and chile
comments.comment: Remembering the old tune, "It's cryin' time again", as the New Year approaches "It's diet time again". And this recipe is a perfect fit for making dieting enjoyable. I make as written but for simmering the jalapeno in broth. If you don't have leftover turkey on hand, a half turkey breast (@ 2.75 lb) will yield what you need here. Most butchers have them post holidays, fresh or frozen. Braise breast stove top & you'll have broth to use too.

comments.recipe_name: lamb meatballs with spiced tomato sauce
comments.comment: One more note to add to my previous and that is this: This recipe was our fav in 2017 of the many we cooked from the NYT Cooking site. (In 2016 the "best of year for us" went to Julia Moskin's Spoon Lamb. Another splendid recipe to recommend if you like this one.) We cook several times a week - stress reliever for us and as much it has been annus horribilis in many ways since last January...well...we've been grateful for easy access to great recipes to try. Thanks!

comments.recipe_name: braised country style pork ribs with chipotle
comments.comment: Made per recipe. Easy, delicious "keeper". With these notes: 1) Big fans of heat-spice but seeing recommendations to reduce cayenne, we did to 1/4 tsp. Still on cusp of 'too hot'. Might reduce chipotle by a bit next time. Advice is start with less of both, taste the rub first, adjust up accordingly. 2) Beer choice matters I think. A hoppy IPA may be too bitter. Look for lower IBU choices like Belgian Brown Ale. We used Fat Tire Abbey (Dubbel). Took about 3/4C.

Made per recipe but for subbing thyme for dill. As usual for Martha Rose Shulman's recipes, this was foolproof. Used four 1 C ramekins and the mixture filled them 'just so'. Done in exactly recommended bake time. Subtle good flavors to taste each ingredient in the dish. Key is getting onion finely chopped and cabbage finely shredded. Great texture from those and hard boiled egg. Highly recommend Bel Gioioso's Ricotta Con Latte here. Whole Milk or Part Skim works.

comments.recipe_name: winter citrus and red chicories salad
comments.comment: So simple to make and so good. Be sure to add the kumquats if you're fortunate enough to live in a city where grocers carry them. And if you're a fan of this recipe by David Tanis, you may also want to try his Sicilian Citrus Salad recipe. It is simply splendid. See link here. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1018551-sicilian-style-citrus-salad

This dish is all about finesse in preparation to result in splendid combination of flavors. Note the recipe calls for tomato puree, not paste, and only 2 TB. It calls for Madiera and only 2T. It calls for wild mushrooms and only 4. Folks who say they used tomato paste, and a can no less, or a large amount of red wine instead of a little madeira or truffle oil (which is chemicals) in place of mushrooms are not making THIS recipe. Which is luscious as written. Serve as primi or as main.

Chicken n' dumplings was a much loved dish made by my much loved - great cook grandfather in the day of my childhood in my family's Anglo household. My Sicilian husband who grew up with his Italian speaking grandmother heading the kitchen has never appreciated dumplings. Until now. These dumplings are so good even he likes them. And for those who may be dumpling virgins, you need to have and keep the broth at simmer when adding and cooking the dumpling dough.

Turned out great & relying on notes of others to make that so, I'll summarize what worked. 1)Although I always cook my own chickpeas for anything from soups to hummus, for this I used a can, rinsed & drained. 2)Dried them on kitchen towel, then on dry paper towel on counter overnight. Getting super dry is key. 3)No parchment on baking pan, roasted 400 for 20 min & then enough crisped that I turned down to 200 for last 10 min. Perfect crunch. 4)Needed only @ 2 tsp oil.

Oven baked polenta technique referenced in this recipe is genius. Have always made polenta on stove top. Used oven technique embedded in article associated with this recipe and outcome was top notch. See article link at top of page or here. http://www.nytimes.com/2009/06/08/health/nutrition/08recipehealth.html Used on Superbowl. As meatballs and red sauce warmed on stove top, polenta was in the oven and all ready to eat at halftime without me having to "monitor". Touchdown!

Simple and splendid. Tried twice. First with light brown sugar as I didn't have moscovado . Second time with moscovado as written. The fresh mint sprinkle at end is genius for fresh flavor. Neither time looked quite like the photo but no matter, it was a hit with everyone. Once for dessert. Once for breakfast. For all who share here you learned to do this by broiling in Home Ec class, I did too. But broiling is not this technique. This technique is better. IMHO.

The proportion (1/1/1) can be used for luscious result for just a couple of cocktails too. You'll want to shake all WELL in ice filled shaker, omit the extra water - melting ice adds some to the drinks, and strain into salt rimmed martini glasses (up) or into salt rimmed, ice filled glasses (over). 2 drinks = 2 shots tequila + 2 shots Cointreau + 2 shots lime juice. Perfect. For those who say liquor quality doesn't matter here, I disagree. Good quality booze is essential.

Hubby is Italian, makes carbonara often. BTW, any carbonara is good for breakfast including wee morning hours after too much celebrating. Then again, it is great with Prosecco too if celebration isn't over. Big fan of Alton so tried this. A. Four eggs is two too many for 8 oz pasta for us. B. Step 6, turn off the flame / heat but leave pan on burner. C. Toss, toss, toss, toss...key to getting eggs cooked right. D. Use FRESH crumbs not packaged. E. Serves 2-4 not 4-6. :-)

Made this as written and it was superb! I would normally braise in oven, after starting on stovetop, but also finished on stove top per recipe. I found it took a something a little more than the 30 minutes specified for the braise step, maybe 40 for the thigh elements, but white meat done in 30 and I removed them to wait on the other pieces. A keeper indeed!

Made per recipe w/ delightfully delicious, tender, not-to-sweet cookies as outcome. Maybe bit more cardamom next time. Vanilla sugar essential finishing touch. Some say refrigerate dough before and/or after shaping. I wouldn't. Dough is just right but you need to handle gently. Use a 2 tsp 'ish' ice cream scoop to portion dough. Gently roll each portion between fingers into a little "log". Then bend each log to crescent shape. Easy. 18 min bake time. Made 25.

Made this twice, per recipe, in whole recipe and half. Delicious outcome each time. Half sized was not by plan but by necessity when I discovered I had only 1C of basmati in the pantry. Cut all other ingredients in half, used a smaller Dutch oven and worked well. Takes me about 10 min. to achieve correct brownness aroma of rice in step 3 (not the 5 - 8 min of recipe). Used Emile Henry Dutch ovens in both cases. Timing for finish was as recipe and good crust layer both times.

comments.recipe_name: red bean stew with fried onions and cilantro
comments.comment: Fantastic flavors. Made per recipe ingredients omitting optional blue fenugreek & using pomegranate molasses as Tkemali substitute. Needed @ 7C water for cook - not 6C - with just enough liquid remaining at end of 1.5 hour when beans very tender. Didn't have to drain to mash - used potato masher in bean pot. At step 5 added a bit more water to make 'soupy' enough. Feta & pomegranate seeds at end are pretty and round this out, but also great without them.

Comments here suggest some folks are using fresh sausage thinking it is substitute for Andouille. The Louisiana style Andouille the recipe author recommends is a coarse-grained smoked or cured sausage. It is not a raw, fresh, sausage as you might buy a finer grained, fresh sausage in casing at a grocery butcher case. It makes all the difference in this recipe to use that cured sausage.

Made per recipe. Easy enough for weeknight. Good enough for 'company's comin'. Cooked my own navy (small white) beans. (You can cook beans ahead of time & hold in fridge - so much better than from can. 1C rounded dry = 3.5C cooked.) Had only about 12 oz ground lamb on hand - was plenty of meat to bean ratio for us. Drained most fat from pan between step 1 & 2. Big fan of cilantro stem flavor, ingredient seldom seen in recipes & enjoyed it in this one.

comments.recipe_name: peruvian pork stew with chiles lime and apples
comments.comment: Made per recipe for ingredients. Counterpoint to those commenting this was bland, we thought it was very tasty. We love bold flavors too but not every dish needs to be aggressive. Sometimes subtle works as this. Easy to do. Noting salt is omitted from recipe, perhaps because Bittman assumes we all know to do that? It will need salt. We put in oven for covered braise, after step 2. About 90 min+ @ 325. This also works with pork sirloin. Not loin, too lean, but pork sirloin.

I put everything on a cutting board and run a chef's knife through it umpteen (how's that for specific) times to be sure all is finely blended together. Technique used for lots of seasoning salts. If 1/4C each sugar and salt (using same zest) this holds for me for weeks. Lemon zest works too. Can make it spice by adding some crushed Aleppo or red pepper flakes as you chop it up. This citrus salt blend has a zillion uses.

These. Are. SO. Good! For prep clarification, perhaps, in Step 1 the way to line the pan is to put the parchment (or foil) in as a "+". You'll use 'tails' on the liner material to lift the brownies out of the pan to cut them in Step 7. If you're not using crème de menthe but want a green color, you can put a 'eensie' drop of green food color in the buttercream as it is mixed in Step 4.

comments.recipe_name: mashed potato salad with scallions and herbs
comments.comment: Made 2X. 1st X as written. 2nd X omitting rosemary, doubling sour cream and reducing EVOO to 2 TBSP. Preferred 2nd X modifications. Original was too oil for my preference. Surprisingly good for texture and with lemon zing.

Made this. Exactly as written. Optioning in the splash of fortified wine. Choose Marsala for that. Outcome was a delicious, simple, dinner for two. Looking just like the recipe photo image. With all respect to those who want to complicate this with technique and ingredient modifications, sometimes simple is just the thing. This is a keeper.

Made as written with one minor ingredient adjustment. Used Bel Giosioso's Ricotta Con Latte (16 oz) and given that brand's whole milk version is so lovely and lusciously rich, I eliminated cream ingredient here. Delicioso! Next time I may reduce mozzarella a bit. Or not. And add oregano to 'the gravy'. Or not. In theory this should feed a small army. In reality, it is so good, I would be happy to eat it all! :-) Works really well to make ahead. Leftovers great.

comments.recipe_name: linguine with clams roasted tomatoes and caramelized garlic
comments.comment: Made just as written and outcome was just right delicous. For cooks who said they subbed Pernod for white wine, Pernod is anise flavor spirit that @ 40% alcohol. If using a full 1/2 cup to replace the white wine, as some said they did, it will overpower the subtle flavors of this dish and be quite a lot more alcohol "hot" than the 13%'ish' white wine. If you want to use Pernod, just a splash at end is the way to use it. But this is so good as written, I wouldn't.

Good thing about Bittman's great recipes is they are template to adjust for your taste buds. Some say this had too little sauce for 16 oz noodles. It isn't intended to be super-saucy (see photo example) and recipe specifies 12-16 oz. We prefer 12 but use even less if you want more sauce ratio. (EZ to find Thai Kitchen brand linguine-sized 'thin' noodle is 14.4 oz box.) We prefer no shrimp, less nam pla and generous mix of all herbs. Point is to cook at home and enjoy it.

Does the 2 1/2 cup fresh cilantro used in this include stems or only the leaves? Preparing to make it for first time. Assuming since it doesn't call out just leaves that this is whole stem? Thanks to anyone who might know the answer.

comments.recipe_name: avocado salad with herbs and capers
comments.comment: With 50-iwh notes so far about how to buy and ripen avocados and keep them from browning after cutting, thought I'd weigh in with a comment on the recipe itself. Made it this afternoon. As written. Simple to make, simply delicious to eat. No need to worry about how to keep the avocados from discoloring, as discussion here, since they are sliced, plated and eaten in pretty quick order. One clove of garlic was ample. This dish, good bread, white wine. Sunday "L-inner"!

This can be modified to reduce sweetness as you choose. Coconut oil can also be reduced for those concerned with fat content. (Mild EVOO can be subbed too.) NOTE RE SALT - this calls for FLAKY salt. Those finding it too salty may be using something other than flake. Good but Madison Park granola per Sam Sifton is my fav: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1014304-eleven-madison-park-granola?...

This recipe is why God invented buttermilk-ranch-style dressing and iceberg lettuce. Will acknowledge wedge salads to be a throwback, guilty pleasure for hubby and me and for that 'sin' we've been introduced to several not so nice ones along with the goodies. But this made at home version ranks up there with the best of them all. Scallions make a fine substitute for chives if you need to do that. Keeper! This is main dish for us with good bread and vino...or martini.

Made 2X. 1) Both times sautéd garlic and onion in quantities per recipe & added to jar of Rao's Marinara simply for convenience - had all other ingredients but fresh tomatoes. Turned out swell using Rao's & just "eensie" sauce leftover to dollop each serving. 2) Second time added 3 TBSP 'ish' panko crumbs to parmesan. Result a bit firmer texture. 3) Maybe even better warm to room temp than hot. 4) Leftovers really good. Keeper!

Used Esperanza's tip to pulse oats a bit first (good tip - worked swell) but otherwise per recipe. Used chili powder option in the smoked paprika or choice. Delicious outcome. Made 6 patties, about 4"-ish wide each, so easy to turn them in skillet and just right for bun size. Cooked in about 3 1/2 min. each side. If 5 min per recipe they would have been over crisp. Used olive oil in scanpan. Have a couple left that I'm going to freeze to see how that works for make ahead.

Plum season ALREADY? So it is as the weeks fly by and time for this annual homage to seeing fall on the horizon. I like a little lemon zest added to batter. 3/4 sugar is plenty. Having butter SOFT is key to start. 9" springform seems to work best for me. Quartering the plums is my preference over halves. Note that plums throw off a LOT of liquid when they cook. Don't under bake this. Clean toothpick test applies. Maybe more than hour depending on oven. Freezes so well.

Noting several comments of folks arguing - in the debate sense of the verb - the "only right way" to make a tomato toast is their Catalonian recipe made their way, I thought I'd simply weigh in on this recipe made this way. It is a splendidly simple to make and utterly luscious to eat celebration of fresh tomatoes in season. A glass of wine, this and it is "ahhh...some" meal or starter. There may be tomato toasts as good but I'd wager there are none better.

Subbed ground lamb for beef only because we don't eat the latter and love the former. Luscious flavors. A tip for keeping 'fresh' pomegranate seeds at hand: Woodstock Organics brand has them frozen in resealable bags. Available in natural food stores; Woodstock is broadly distributed. Fan of flavor of tamarind in ketchup but agree with Mark not really needed here. Yogurt sauce is "the thing". Next time I'll sub cilantro for the iceberg lettuce.

comments.recipe_name: black bean chorizo casserole with pickled onions
comments.comment: 1st time made per recipe minus chorizo & cooked my black beans from scratch. SO delicious. 2nd time used cured chorizo (but only half pound) & used organic, canned black beans, well rinsed. Again SO good. It was "can you bring a hot beans type dish" contribution to summer Spanish theme dinner & a big hit. Not as much work as you think as lots can be done ahead. Plus reheats well so can even be baked in advance. As good warm as hot so works well on buffet table.

May be my fav of all zucchini loaves baked over umpteen decades of finding a use for generous neighbors' garden bounty. Just sweet enough but not too much. Goes together in a snap. Made per recipe but for reducing cinnamon to 1 tsp. Took 50 min. to perfect in 8" loaf pan. Bonus is how good house smelled when baking. Tip: Also good zucch recipes on NYT site 1) Molly O'Neill's Zucchini Cake with Ginger & Hazelnuts and 2) Zucchini Parmesan by Martha Rose Shulman.

comments.recipe_name: chicken curry with sweet potatoes and coconut milk
comments.comment: De-luscious! Simple to do. Pretty on plate. Made per recipe but for subbing sliced shallots for chopped scallions simply because I didn't have them on hand. Noting some found chicken skin ugly-flabby at end, my outcome was not that. Don't rush the browning step in 2. For me took about 2C water to cover chicken half way up. But chicken throws a lot of liquid as it braises. Next time I'll use about 1C 'plus' water. Take the time to redux broth to sauce in step 5.

Made as written but for subbing walnuts for pine nuts. Stirring parm into pesto at end I was certain it would be too much cheese. I was wrong. Perfect for proportion. Have made umpteen batches pesto over 30 years (hubby Italian). This is good one. For reasons of environmental concerns and health (I had 'pine nut mouth' once), I only use pine nuts harvested and processed in Italy or US, not the Asian variety processed in China. Hence subbing walnuts or pistachios in pesto.

Great use for a couple of tomatoes from neighbor's garden that were just shy of overripe. We backed down garlic and kosher salt by half to suit our palates. Don't skip the good Maldon flakes at the end. Bread quality matters here too. So delicious an anchor for a 'too hot to cook' dinner along with jamon,cheese, olives...you choose.. And yes, that bottle of Cava is perfect pairing.

Genius to combine pan con tomate into a tomato sandwich. One of those recipes where one wonders "why didn't I think of that?" But all the years of making con tomate and tomato sandwiches both and I didn't until now. My preference is to leave the mayo off. Sacrilege perhaps but I really don't like mayo. It is a great sandwich without mayo or bacon. But...oh...those onions...nice, sweet summer onions are essential! Another great Melissa Clark recipe.

Good way to roast garden grown in-season tomatoes that are just on the cusp of being too ripe to save them as delicious somethings as well as the medium ripe winter tomatoes from market to transform them into something delicious. Textures at end of cook time will be different for one v. the other. Either a good choice for a buffet service meal any time of year since they are as good room temperature as warm. "Travel" well too since can be made in advance and reheated.

Simply excellent. Simple to make. Excellent to eat. Made it exactly per recipe. Outcome just as expected. Very easy. Delicous. Keeper! We could easily have served 3 or maybe even 4 people for smaller portions. But so good that two of us finished it off to be full to the brim served alongside garden fresh tomatoes and some of Bittman's McBitty bean patties served sans bun.

comments.recipe_name: beans and garlic toast in broth
comments.comment: Made as written but for subbing thyme for marjoram. Delicious dish we enjoy for B, L, or D. Well, if for B holding garlic rub on the toast if going to work or school. Otherwise, full rub ahead for that! I often make parmesan broth to have on hand in freezer and always cook beans from scratch but had not thought to cook the beans with the parm rinds in this way before. Used Great Northerns and they were done in about 1 1/2 hr. Also, next time I'd simmer with lid off the pot. Keeper.

comments.recipe_name: nectarine raspberry cobbler with ginger biscuits
comments.comment: Biscuits delightful baked on their own & perfect with coffee or tea. Take @ 16 min to done on parchment lined sheet @ 375F. Made per recipe for ingredients using gram weights where provided & 1/2&1/2 for liquid & demerara sugar for final sprinkle. Next time will double pistachios. Be sure dice to ginger very small. Given eggs in mix & relatively small amount of butter to flour these are 'cake-ier' than most biscuits. See second note for technique comments.

comments.recipe_name: nectarine raspberry cobbler with ginger biscuits
comments.comment: See previous note for ingredient comments. Delicous biscuits in cobbler or baked on own. Re technique, you definitely want to have these 3/4" at start, less and they won't rise tall enuf. They expand both ways baking. Using 3" biscuit cutter, best I could get was 9 not 12. If need 12, use 2.75 or 2.5" cutter. Do not make them flatter to get there. Don't overhandle dough - knead only a few turns, not 1 min. Patting out not rolling out is best in my experience.

If you're the invitee to "please bring..." events with rep for contributing 'gourmet' each time, bring this & play with them. After they stop "1995 wants it's food back" & start eating they will say "ahhh…" some. :-) Good hot thru lukewarm so fits in buffet spread. Fine chop spinach MEANS fine. FRESH mozzarella not the plastic stuff. Try Kim Severson's Bits & Pieces cheese ball for similar 'audience response'. :-)

Have made a lot of panzanella over the years seldom using a recipe. Used the recipe for this one and it was a winner with late summer tomatoes. Only changes were for our palates just a splash more vinegar in step 3 with a skoosh less EVOO and doubling capers. We started with day-old baguette cubes then oven toasted per step 1 for 10'ish' minutes. Bread cubes pretty crispy firm by the time the dish went together and we found an hour was the just right 'sit time' in step 4.

comments.recipe_name: angel hair pasta with peppers and tomatoes
comments.comment: Lusciously seasonal. Just perfect made as written. The only modification was to grate garlic on microplane rather than smashing to paste. Used spaghettini as pasta option and ricotta salata for cheese choice. For those who suggested roasting peppers first - I wouldn't do that but note the finely diced instructions. It makes all the difference.

comments.recipe_name: rawia bisharas brussels sprouts with tahini sauce
comments.comment: Delicious. 1)Used oven roast option for cooking sprouts. 2) Opted not to use bread crumbs. 3) Prefer a little more lemon juice than 1/2 C and a bit more pomegranate molasses (just taste until tartness reaches your palate's preference). 4)I think grating garlic on microplane works better than chopping it. (Thanks to Melissa Clark for that grating of garlic tip years ago that I now use for nearly everything called for chopped.) 5)Good tahini (look in Middle East markets) essential.

comments.recipe_name: lebanese style bread salad with tomatoes and herbs
comments.comment: For folks who posted being critical that this recipe is not authentic Fattoush (be it Lebanese, Palestinian or Israeli), for the use of this ingredient or absence of that one, note the author didn't represent it as such. It is titled "Lebanese-style". Made as written, this is easy and delicious way to enjoy the bounty of late summer veg. I prefer to oven toast the pita and sometimes use Arabic flatbread from a local Iraqi market instead of pita. Don't skip the sumac.

Noting the lemon wedges in the photo for this dish, were they added by food stylist for color contrast interest in the photo, or was there lemon used in recipe but omitted from the text when printed? A curious cook who plans to make this next week would find it helpful to know. ;-)

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: Keeper! 1)The squeeze of lemon juice at end "is it" for finishing touch of acid on this simple and delicious dish. 2) We omitted rosemary. 3)Used pancetta but if you need to sub bacon use unsmoked variety. 4)Recipe serves 2. Hubby and I find 5.5 oz pasta is plenty for us to each have good sized serving with right ratio of pasta to 8 oz Brussels for our preferences. 5)Save a little of pasta cookng water in case you want to add at end.

comments.recipe_name: creamy stovetop corn with poblano chiles
comments.comment: Great use for late season corn and peak season poblanos and splendid outcome for dish as recipe was written but for using only about half of crème fraiche specified since that's what I had on hand. Actually seemed just right for finished dish. Think full fat Greek yogurt or sour cream could be subbed too. 3 ears of corn produced a generous 3 cups of kernels for us. We used the optional jalapenos. The lime juice at end "is it" for kiss of acid. Keeper!

Made as written. Nice agrodolce and while my fully Sicilian hubby argues this may not have been considered caponata in his nonna's kitchen for limited tomato, it is a good eggplant dish. Olives are important - don't use pimento stuffed variety - we used. Castelvetrano. My "oops' in misreading recipe was to blanch celery stalks then chop them. Worked well in spite of my myopia. Brilliant idea to blanch celery - I'll use technique in other recipes.

comments.recipe_name: tomato salad on cumin spiced yogurt
comments.comment: Our market had lovely late season cherry tomatoes in a couple of colors so used those to make this plus newly harvested dill and both greens and whites of scallions.
