Delicious flavors in the yogurt and vinaigrette. (Note recipe was edited after first publication for clarification on how much yogurt goes into the vinaigrette.) Cool the cumin seeds before grinding. Used California Ranch "Everyday" Olive Oil. Jacobsen's Flake Salt used to finish "is it".

comments.recipe_name: chicken noodle salad with creamy sesame dressing
comments.comment: 1) Takes very little effort to make and delivers very tasty outcome. Even less effort if using leftover roasted chicken breast for 'school night meal' and not poaching 'from scratch'. 2) Tahini brand matters here - look for Alwadi or Soom or similar. 3) Thought dressing benefitted from a little sweetener - sugar or brown sugar or a little honey. 3) Prefer to grate ginger on microplane. 4) Prefer water over broth in the option for those ingredients.

As fun to make as it is tasty. Made per recipe for most part. 1) Loaf - a) used broccoli rabe & chose turkey (ground breast) for second meat. Next time I'd reduce turkey to 1/2# & use 1 1/2# pork. b) redux salt to 1/2 tsp - cheese is salty. c) omitted bay leaves from top. d) some say omit olives but I used Castelvetranos & we liked them here. 2) Sauce - per recipe. But didn't pour all of it over. 3) Topping - subbed parm for pecorino. 4) Bake - to 160 degrees internal.

Re this in my previous notes: "2) Sauce - per recipe. But didn't pour all of it over.". The reason was I was pretty sure the baking loaf would throw off more fat & protein "ooze" than I'd want to use as 'spoon over'. That turned out to be so. The sauce is essential in baking this, but for us the decision to leave some of it to heat and serve separately was the right one. Discarded most of sauce in pan.

comments.recipe_name: penne with brussels sprouts chile and pancetta
comments.comment: Simple and delicious. Keeper. Made two times. First per recipe. Second per recipe but nearly doubled the brussels sprouts - keeping other ingredients per recipe - and didn't use penne. Both times squeezed juice from half lemon over and tossed well. If veg doubled then it works well as side dish. Without penne the brussels mixture is good as bruschetta or crostini topping. (I like to spread bread with soft goat cheese or ewe's cheese first.)

Made per recipe but for using 1# oyster mushrooms for pale-wild & 1# cremini for brown.. Relatively little effort needed - especially given make ahead portion - for delicious dish. 1)Served with pappardelle and found 12 oz pasta good amount as ragu partner. 2) Used homemade veg broth. 3) Used oyster mushrooms for pale-wild option. Thought they got a tad lost in this. Next time I'll mix shitake with cremini for browns and use chanterelles for wilds as they might hold up better.

comments.recipe_name: pearl couscous with shrimp and clams
comments.comment: Pernod is delightful playmate for shellfish. But be cautious of using "or other similar anise liquer". Because Anisette & so on will be much too sweet. (Ex: Pernod has 1.8 T sugar/C & anise liquers up to 6T.) See link here to Pernod 101 by food scientist Harold Magee. Pernod really has no substitute. I use it in cooking & agree with Prof. Magee "Pernod is best treated gently." See his #5 point. https://www.starchefs.com/pernod/html/pernod_101.shtml

Curry, paprika and tarragon flavors combined were delightful surprise for me and equally wonderful - or maybe more - is the article on Thomas Keller and his father that accompanies the recipe. Use "What the last meal taught him" link to read it and appreciate even more the gift that is cooking good food for loved ones - or oneself. 1) Finding 6oz minus breasts a challenge. 2) Chicken may take longer than 1-1.5 min to brown. 3) Add mixed greens w/vinaigrette + crusty bread = dinner.

comments.recipe_name: sweet potatoes with bourbon and brown sugar
comments.comment: Being among "those" who wonder why people post about recipes they have not yet made, I become one of "those" to do that thing. To say it was ingredients that drew me in. What's not to like for in a household where we eat lots of sweets? Not to mention Thanksgiving on the horizon. But what leads me to post now is recommend "WATCH THE VIDEO!" With that emphasis. Ina and Jeffrey? I'll raise you Melissa and Daniel! And adorable Dahlia too!

WATCH THE VIDEO. :-) Melissa, husband Daniel, daughter Dahlia and mom Rita all are delightful. I send wishes for peace of spirit to this family who will spend their first Thanksgiving without Melissa's father, Rita's husband, this year as Melissa shares in another video. Rehearsal run of this recipe - which will debut on our TDay table in full form - cut in half (for 4) and using stale Boule I had on hand instead of Brioche was delicious. Bread torn 1" is key technique here.

comments.recipe_name: pork stew with pears and sweet potatoes
comments.comment: Delicious. Easy. As written except minor adjustments noted. 1) 2.5 lb boneless country ribs (a great cut) - well trimmed of fat to start = no extra fat at end 2) Reduced allspice and caraway by half 3) Used 1C wine + 2C water (instead of stock) 4) Used yams (orange) not sweet potatoes (white) 5) Added yams after 1 hr. Then 30 min more added pears for 15 min. Just right textures, in particular if planning to reheat. 6) Lemon juice at end + grainy mustard in bowls 'makes it'.

comments.recipe_name: roasted sweet potatoes with hot honey browned butter
comments.comment: My husband is a fiend for orange flesh sweet potatoes (aren't they yams?) and we eat a lot of them in our home, on their own and as part of other dishes. This stand alone dish for 'sweets' has become a favorite preparation for a simply easy and delicious meal. We most often use apple cider vinegar with this, but Rancho Gordo has a pineapple vinegar (no kidding) that is a lovely, low acid vinegar that we like in this recipe too. And good butter is key. We like Kerrygold.

comments.recipe_name: baked egg with prosciutto and tomato
comments.comment: Being among the odd-ducks who like egg yolks more firm, my hubby and I find 18-20 minutes is right cook time for us at 375 temp. And we were reminded last night what a versatile technique is Bittman's baked egg recipe as guideline as we made delightful dinner using leftovers - a little cream in bottom of ramekins, a sprinkle of diced ham, a couple of eggs (in each of the 5" ramekins), topped with bread crumbs and grated cheese. Make toast and sip wine as eggs bake. Ahh-some!

comments.recipe_name: maple roasted brussels sprouts with toasted hazelnuts
comments.comment: As written for ingredients and oven temp. Outcome just OK - not a keeper. 15 min cut side down & already tender. Then cut side up for maple syrup drizzle and done in another 15. Would have been far overcooked at recipe specified time. No caramelization so left in and turned up oven for another 10 min but still not. Good flavor. But not a stndout for us.

One of our fav preparations for cranberry sauce, a condiment we eat all season long with lots of things. We use a bit less OJ and cut the sugar by half only because we prefer cranberry sauce very tart and thick. Play with the sweetness level for what works for your palate. The flavor of orange and cranberry is such a good pairing. Cranberry harvests in 2018 were so robust that farmers dumped 25% of crops in some states. Eat an extra helping of this cranberry sauce and help boost sales!

Made as written for opening course at Thanksgiving. It was a hit and nary a mushroom left on plates. Were I making this again, I wouldn't change a thing. Some say reduce sugar, but we all found the agrodolce character very good. The recipe says it serves 4 but we could have arranged a 5th serving out of it if needed. I made it in a.m. for dinner service and the roughly 7 hours of marinade time worked. I used cremini, trumpet and shitake as mushrooms.

The woman across the dinner table was a psychologist. But for the 3 glasses of wine consumed she may not have asked, 'HOW do you stay SO well adjusted given your diagnosis?". This during course 3 as I mentioned living with progressive CNS disease for 40 years. "Cooking and food", I said. Specifically this kind of food and cooking. Making this kind of dish is Zen-time for me. And in this recipe it is as if I heard Samin's voice in my kitchen with me. So delicious!

Roasting turkey parts delivers rich flavor & great color broth. Used 4# turkey wings, parsley in place of thyme & 'some' whole black peppercorns instead of ground. Roasted turkey in roughly 12"w x 8"h stockpot (took 1 hr to get golden), just moving it from oven to stove top at step 2. Added onions, bay leaves & peppercorns in step 2, simmered 1 hr then added rest of the veg at step 3 & simmered 1 1/2 hr more. Yield after straining = @ 2qt +3C.

For those looking for mahaleb, there really is no substitute for this, and if your local Middle Eastern market is out of it (as mine), thought it might be useful to know this is sometimes spelled mahleb if you're doing a web search. World Spice is out of stock. Savory Spice no longer sells it. But SNUK has some available. Buy the smallest amount you can. A little goes a long way and too much can sometimes add bitterness.

For those asking about puffed grains availability and about puffed grains to sub for wheat to make gluten free dish, thought this might be helpful. You can find puffed wheat pretty readily all grocery stores that have a natural foods section. Arrowood is one brand broadly distributed and makes puffed grains sold in bags. Wheat. Brown Rice (GF). Kamut (ancient wheat derivative and not GF.) Also it changes the texture a bit but puffed Millet - grass not grain so GF might be worth a try.

Made this as written. It went together exactly as described. Looked just like the photos at outcome. And these are delicious in flavor and texture. Used the flour option (not matzoh meal). Where volume measures and gram weights were provided I used gram weights. Only tricky part was shaping logs in step 3. The recipe takes a little time to do - but some of that is unattended time - but isn't difficult to make. Wouldn't change a thing.
Does anyone know if I can substitute chuck roast or blade roast trimmed and cut 1/2" dice as per brisket instructions and get the tender outcome desired for this chile in the same simmering time?

comments.recipe_name: sabzi polo herbed rice with tahdig
comments.comment: Virgin voyage at making Persian rice for tahdig crust, which we love but had not tried at home, was a great success thanks to Samin's solid instructions (such a good teacher) and a 10" Scanpan for non stick and good browning of crisp tahdig layer. We used these instructions for rice amounts and technique and timing. But omitted the herbs to make a saffron rice layer at the bottom using the saffron 'tea' instructions from Samin's Salt-Fat-Acid-Heat book's rice recipe. Perfect result.

Perfect as written! CenterPoint of Christmas Eve dinner at which my husband's tradition is to eat fish. (Seven Fishes for many years. More realistically one these days. :-) ) But for thinking we'd cook the scallops 6 minutes on each side next time (just our preference for how done we like scallops at center), we wouldn't change a thing.

comments.recipe_name: bits and pieces party cheese ball
comments.comment: Good to have tried. David Lebovitz (Chez Panisse alum of L'Appart and other cookbook fame) starts with citing the same Vivian Howard recipe as referenced for this one. Roughly the same but without fish sauce (using 1/4 tsp salt and hot sauce instead) and adding 1/4 C chopped dates (and also suggests other dried fruits like apricots or cranberries would work) and a couple TBSP chopped scallions or chives.

Recipe uses 4 1/2C liquid (juice + water) & 3 packets powdered gelatin. Assuming Knox (most common brand) their recommendation is 2 C liquid / 1 packet. For those following recipe & reporting too thick outcome, consider removing 1/2 C water for 4C liquid total & use 2 packets gelatin. With that ratio, mine turned out of bowl mold just right & lightly held 'wiggly' shape with soft texture on tongue. Reducing water also makes more juice tart mitigating what some said was not as intense flavor.

*Pretty basic cooking techniques involved. Tasty. But so many recipes & so little time - won't do again. *Full flavors - for those who thought too bland, wonder if really fresh spices used? *Reduced water by 1.5 C given some comments - seemed good to have done. *Didn't use rose water or buds since we are not fond of them. *Definitely make this far enough ahead to well defat braise liquid. *Needed kiss of sweet at end to balance lime juice in braise liquid - added @1T honey w/ good result

First, the olive oil measure in this recipe should be 1/3 C. Looks like a typo in ingredients as published here. I have been making my own crouton for 30 years, but it didn't occur to me to tear the bread until seeing Samin's recipe. Genius. I've made these in both standard and "classic" - see Tip at bottom of recipe - and they're great to use in salad, with soup, to dip in hummus or other spreads or on their own with a libation. Airtight container and they've held well for a week for us.

comments.recipe_name: orecchiette with nduja shrimp and tomatoes
comments.comment: Seems author intends raw broccoli rabe (rapini) to be cooked about 3 min. as part of step 7. Broccoli rabe (turnip family) is one of few green veg that benefits from long cooking. Too crunchy and far too bitter as spec'd here. Cook it first. (Italian hubby so we use rapini regularly.) For good tutorial see Samin's Nosrat's Salt-Fat-Acid-Heat or any recipe here by her using rapini. Or, Viola Butoni's method to cook rapini to use in any dish. https://www.huffpost.com/entry/rapini_b_4072299

For the multiple folks who are objecting to the use of Coca Cola in this dish, I'm wondering if that could be much concern about nothing. First, it is but 2 oz cola per serving. Also, you aren't eating this three meals x seven a week. It is one dish for one meal in what I assume wouldn't be more on rotation than weekly. If that. Not to mention there is cane sugar sweetened Coke on the market these days. A couple of oz of cola consumed is not fatal. IMHO

1. Made per recipe with following exceptions. Delicious and just as expected for tagine for form and sauce. Keeper. 2. Exceptions to as written recipe: a) Used water instead of stock. b) Used Emile Henry Dutch Oven which has 'dimpled' lid so didn't need foil and oven at 300 degrees (not 325) for perfectly tender in 2'ish hours. 3. Toasted almonds and other garnish 'make it' for final presentation. 4. Used Calif. not Turkish apricots. CA cots a bit more tart so didn't need lemon at end.

comments.recipe_name: radish sandwiches with butter and salt
comments.comment: Very good as written. For my Italian hubby who never has learned to enjoy butter as bread spread, we also like this with an Israeli version twist. That is substitute labneh (spreadable consistency yogurt cheese) for the butter. Then sprinkle with a little sumac and flake salt, add the radishes. Flat leaf parsley instead of arugula and chives for herbs. Another riff on this very good starting recipe. Both are great for appetizers or even for breakfast on the run.

comments.recipe_name: cold sesame noodles with crunchy vegetables
comments.comment: As most Bittman recipes, this is very good as written & great to use as inspiration to modify as you choose. Comments: 1) Blending tahini with peanut butter (@ 6T tahini + 2T pb) will get flavor more like Chinese sesame paste. 2) Prefer eensie less sesame oil & a lot more fresh ginger.. 3) Prefer a little less soy then add (Diamond Crystal) kosher salt as needed to taste. 5) Rancho Gordo's pineapple vinegar (low acid) is great here but so is rice vinegar. 6) Like to finish w/cilantro.

Hubby grew up in Italian-American neighborhood in Brooklyn with grandmother who spoke only Italian so he not considers himself 'expert' (in jest) on good pizza crust & makes a lot of pizza at home. He said this was one of the easiest to work with doughs he has ever used. The chew & taste were great! A couple of notes: 1) We weigh everything but 8g salt (Diamond Crystal) was over 1.5t. Used 1t. 2) Watch the video in addition to reading recipe. 3) Did second rise at room temp for 4.5 hrs.

Easy recipe. Tasty outcome. Notes to share: 1) Taking time to caramelize onions is key - they don't need babysitting but do need attending. Took @ 1hr 15 min. 2) Some say 'too sweet' but caramelized onions are that. As expected in that regard & "skoosh" of lemon juice added in pan at end balances that before chopping. 3) Make ahead seemed a good idea BUT thickened from dip to spread consistency in fridge overnight. Day of use assembly might be best. 4) Didn't need EVOO drizzle at end.

comments.recipe_name: lentils with chorizo greens and yellow rice
comments.comment: Rustic. Comforting. Delicious. Easy. Keeper. 1. Cut in half - served 3 generously. Looked like pix. 2. Made as written for ingredients except subbed escarole, had on hand, for kale. 3. Great flavors in lentils & in rice. Tread lightly adding the extra salt to lentils in step 3 - Chorizo releases salt as it simmers. 4. Noting several reported problems with rice, I note step 7 doesn't say specifically to cover rice for the 15 min. of cook. I did. Then left covered for 25 min. steam. Perfect

comments.recipe_name: fettuccine with ricotta and a fistful of mint
comments.comment: In the same theme as Dorie Greenspan's Spoonable Ricotta (Everyday Dorie), this rendition is really tasty too. Not just for pasta but to top potatoes. (I'm thinking this will be particularly good with new potatoes and Spring peas in market in a few weeks.) It is also yummy spread on toast. (Ask me how I know that.) Or as dip for veggies. Or to spread on crackers. Or to eat with a spoon. A good quality ricotta, like Bel Gioioso Con Latte, works as well in reduced fat as whole milk.

Luscious. Keeper! Made to sip while cooking Valentine's Day dinner to celebrate 40 Valentine's Days shared in the kitchen together and acknowledge the bitter that comes with the sweet in love. Corny - sure - but it's what caught our eye to try this libation. This was a hit with both of us!. Notable since my hubby does not share my affection for French 75 which I enjoy as champagne cocktail. We used dry Prosecco and orange bitters and dialed back the bitters just a bit.

Paul and Lawrence say the parsley is more than a garnish. So agree with them. Essential - not much is needed but it is needed and makes a difference. KCH says use more clams, like the 48, use good ingredients and get out of the way. Amen! I sometimes like to add a splash or two of whatever white wine I might be drinking with this into the clam liquid in Step 4. Sometimes but not always. This is as good as it gets to perfect.

comments.recipe_name: mussakhan roast chicken with sumac and red onions
comments.comment: Made recipe as written. Good enough to be glad I tried it. Not so good as to make it to 'keeper' . I have had other versions of Mussakhan in which the chicken is pulled off the bone to top the naan and I prefer that presentation. Also, this seemed just a tad salty for our palates using the 1 1/2 tsp salt. (Diamond Crystal Kosher.) I'd cut that back a bit. 375 oven took about 55 min to done.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with roasted cauliflower and blue cheese
comments.comment: Delicious and easy. Worthy of "company's coming" or weeknight supper. 1. Made per recipe for ingredients cutting all in half and using 5 oz pasta to make 2 generous servings. 2. Skipped chives - I was out. But see #5 for other topping. 3. Fresh lemon juice at end is essential - don't skip it. 4. Also good with peas added along with the other veg - maybe 1C for full recipe. 5. Toasted bread crumbs to top each serving is a nice touch.

I made this a couple of times, both as whole recipe and as half, omitting the bacon but all other ingredients and it is delightful in that vegetarian mode. Don't be tempted to use a vegetable oil, like olive oil, in place of butter. Something about the butter "makes it" in combination with the rest. We choose a low IBU (International Bitterness Units) brew and particularly like Belgian Ale used as the beer. Comes together fast enough to be weeknight meal.

How fun this was to make! We used salted butter (Kerrygold) so skipped the pinch of kosher salt. Otherwise made as written and opted for 3 TBSP sugar which was plenty sweet. I bet this would be yummy with a tiny scoop of vanilla ice cream but alas none in the house. This is a perfect little recipe to introduce kids to cooking or to bring out he kid in we...old...umm...well seasoned cooks who love to play in the kitchen.

In my previous comment about trying out tahini, " I thought about the question someone asked about using oil instead of butter. I didn't want to try oil (I love butter) but it made me wonder if tahini - a great chocolate partner - would work instead. Used 1TBSP tahini plus a few drops of water in place of butter. Worked very well.", I should have added that it was a good Lebanese pourable consistency tahini. I like AlWadi. But any pourable, like SOOM, should work.

Made this 2 times following the recipe but for using salted butter & so eliminating pinch of kosher salt. Chose 3T for the sugar & was plenty sweet. Fun & delicous & looked just like photo. The 3rd time I thought about the question someone asked about using oil instead of butter. I didn't want to try oil (I love butter) but it made me wonder if tahini - a great chocolate partner - would work instead. Used 1TBSP tahini plus a few drops of water in place of butter. Worked very well.

Addictively delicious. Notes based on making twice: 1) Used gram weights for all where it was noted. 2) Second time reduced honey & brown sugar to 100g each - prefer that. 3) Mixed spices in with sesame seeds before mixing into nuts & kamut - worked well to distribute spices throughout. 4) Bake time seems best at 7 min for 1st bake & 5 min for 2nd. 5) We make for snacking not as breakfast cereal so we sprinkle a little flake salt over when trays come out of oven

Nice outcome which may not be "wow" flavors outcome that some are looking for based on others' comments but a good "yum" bowl. Good day one, better day two, freezes and reheats well. Made per recipe except for the following changes: 1) Drained soaking water and added all fresh water to beans in the pot. 2) Used Rancho Gordo's pineapple vinegar (a favorite in bean dishes) instead of red wine vinegar. 3) Used only fresh dill at end and about 1/4 C since I was out of parsley.
I'll echo Nan's comment. Don't be fooled that such a simple recipe can deliver such simply delicious results. Keeper!

A favorite at our house. First time I made this I pureed all the peas, didn't leave any out to stir in whole. It was delicious. Each time after that I made it as written to leave out a cup of peas to stir in later. Even more delicious. We like a little more mint and a little less garlic than specified. What makes this even better is that peas are a nutrition powerhouse for protein and fiber both. Tastes great and is good for you.

comments.recipe_name: red curry mussels and roasted sweet potatoes
comments.comment: Makrut lime leaves are also called kaffir lime leaves and looking for them labeled as the latter may make it easier to find them. Even in some mainstream grocers - typically in the fresh herb produce section. Easier to buy if you have a SE Asian or Indian market nearby. Kaffir lime leaves add a distinct quality to a dish and I use a number of recipes that call for them. The good news is if you do find them, they freeze well to use as you need them.

comments.recipe_name: lemony whipped feta with charred scallions
comments.comment: 1. Keeper. Made as written in morning of event day & refrigerated until @ 1 hr B4 service. Texture just right. 2. I sliced lemons very thin for 2 benefits. a) They'll char at same time as scallions. b) Tucked in across the spread they are easy to spoon out with the spread & delicious to eat. 3. Labneh, Fromage Blanc or Greek Yogurt can sub for cream cheese - will be more 'tang' & lighter texture if so. 4. See NYT Cooking demo video here. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=galpmZHzwIs

Top notch for flavors & ease of prep. Keeper. Notes: 1) Used boneless country style ribs (great cut - 'introduced' by NYT article last year & now a fav). 2) Ingredients as recipe - didn't use optional chiles. 3) Procedure - a) Salted pork 2 hr ahead. b) After adding water brought to simmer on cooktop then covered into 300 oven @ 1hr + uncovered 1 1/4 hr 4) Throws LOTs of fat. Recommend separating meat from broth at end & chill overnight to remove big fat cap then put meat in to reheat.

comments.recipe_name: chopped salad with apples walnuts and bitter lettuces
comments.comment: Delicious keeper we made multiple times in Fall and Winter when heirloom apples were plentiful. Notes: 1) Some pears work here too but the outcome isn't as tart as choosing the right apples. 2) Just for personal preference for the amount of acid we prefer in salad dressings, we cut back on the oil a bit to increase the acids to fats balance. 3) We prefer to grate the garlic with a microplane or mash it up on the cutting board with a little salt to make it into a paste before adding

comments.recipe_name: roasted garlic and white bean dip with rosemary
comments.comment: Intro'd to warm white bean dip w/rosemary, like this, at Denver Italian eatery that served w/ grissini. I serve this the same way. Notes: *Best served warm. *Great w / glass of any kind of vino. *Much prefer to start with beans I cook myself rather than canned. (Home cooked beans freeze well or hold in fridge for days to use when needed.) *Also fine w/out fried rosemary to finish. Use olive oil. *Can replace cayenne w / paprika (for color) to garnish if serving heat averse.

Made per recipe choosing water for water / stock option and using very well rinsed rice (swishing in a couple - three of bowls of water and draining until water is clear-ish) it turned out just right for flavor and the 17 min cook / 10 min stand for time was perfect for being done. Keeper.

comments.recipe_name: sheet pan pizza with asparagus and arugula
comments.comment: Made per recipe with 2 small tweaks: 1) cut asparagus in 1-inch pieces not 5-inch pieces as recipe specified and 2) used prosciutto instead of pancetta and given the nature of prosciutto added it not in step 2 but at the same time as olives in Step 3. Otherwise, per recipe for ingredients and instructions. Just right for amount of topping. It was done just right at the temperature and in the time called for. Cut in squares to serve. Good warm and also tasty at room temperature

comments.recipe_name: fettuccine with merguez and mint pesto
comments.comment: Excellent outcome. We prepared it mostly as written. All ingredients we love but in this dish the mint pesto 'makes it'. Only deviations: * We cut the Kalamata's in half * Used regular, not spinach, fettucine * Instead of sun dried tomatoes we used large cherry tomatoes, cut in quarters and tossed in at end right before serving.

comments.recipe_name: eggs with gigante beans and harissa
comments.comment: Made recipe as written choosing prosciutto instead of mojama and one egg, not two, per serving. All were surprised how good a pairing was egg with this as some were skeptical at start. Delicous all around. One diner with egg allergy thought the workaround of another dollop of crème fraiche on top to fill in for fat of egg yolk was delicious that way too. PS - I always start with fresh cooked beans, not canned. Cooking ahead and refrigerating or freezing keeps them handy.

1. Really versatile meatballs that can be added to marinara or in broths or for sandwiches. That they freeze well makes them easy to have on hand. 2. Made recipe as written except for cutting back on salt - used maybe 1/2 tsp - due to parmesan. Cooked a bit to taste for before forming meatballs and less salt was just right for our taste buds. 3. Used homemade bread crumbs - if using commercial product be sure they are unseasoned/salted. 4. Took a little longer in oven than 15 min.

Out of room in previous comments to add that the pork mixture is easier to handle to form the meatballs if you have the time to cover it and put in in the fridge for 30 - 60 minutes first. Just that little bit of time to let the mixture 'rest' makes a difference.

Yum! What if? Typical responses from my husband and me when reading a Bittman recipe. We cook 5 1/2 of 7 days a week. We have been cooking - as kids - since Johnson was President. In all those years we have never braised potatoes like this. Until now. How is it we were missing out on what now may be our fav technique? In 'what if' mode, we had a little schmaltz to use up so subbed that for butter, water with a splash or two of Belgian style ale for broth with fresh thyme. Yumm.

Cut this in half, it served four nicely, and made the recipe as written with delicous outcome. Just right on every level for subtle flavors and perfect texture. Only note I would add is I typically find basmati is done at 17 or 18 minutes and so that is the time I used, not the 20 specified in the recipe.

Sablefish, aka butterfish, is a rich fish 'in the nude' and we found the addition of butter and hazelnuts to be just too much to enjoy this dish, which I made per the recipe. I did like the concept for the wine and miso on the fish before adding the butter toasted nuts to bake and the wine and honey sauce finish. Those miso and wine - next time using white in each - and honey elements I'll try again with some other fish. But in mode of different strokes, this was too rich for us.

comments.recipe_name: herb and radish salad with feta and walnuts
comments.comment: Made as written and cut in half it easily fed three people. Refreshingly delicious. IMHO toasting walnuts is key (I note some say they used them raw). The sieving step was a new tip for me in using walnuts toasted, which I often do. I'll apply that to other recipes now too. Top quality sheep's feta is also key here as is olive oil quality, using Persian, not regular garden variety, cukes and really fresh herbs. Each ingredient supports the others.

comments.recipe_name: sous vide rib steaks with spicy salsa verde
comments.comment: In a riff on the old movie line - "Leave the gun. Take the cannoli." - even if you don't plan to make the sous vide steak try this recipe for the delicious salsa verde. It can be drizzled on roasted or grilled veg, on almost any protein, spooned on bread or over fresh tomatoes or maybe just eaten with a spoon! I prefer a little more lime than specified. A little flake salt - I like Jacobsen's - added over top of whatever you're putting this on is the "just right" end touch.

comments.recipe_name: dandelion salad with garlic confit dressing
comments.comment: Looking for NYT Cooking instructions to confit garlic & came upon this recipe. (And Marian Burros confit instructions too.) Delicous and made mostly as written but: *used big bunch of arugula not dandelion, *upped lemon juice by a good bit for personal preference, *reduced 1/4 tsp salt to big pinch which worked given capers add salt. Flake salt to finish is key. If extra garlic and oil left put them in a sealable glass container in the fridge. Should hold 2 weeks'ish for lots of uses.

Made per recipe using @ 1/4C EVOO & 1/3C "plus" parmesan. Made all in food processor. Great flavor used as spread on ciabbata sandwiches, tossed in pasta w/ fresh tomatoes & swirled in ricotta as dip for veggies & torn croutons (Samin's). Arugula is peppery brassicale (like mustard) containing glucosinolate that some people's taste buds process as more bitter. Noting some say this recipe is bitter, if you find arugula to be so this likely isn’t the pesto for you.

Made mostly per recipe with delicious outcome. I've never used cornmeal to thicken corn soup but Bittman seldom steers me wrong so I used it. Brilliant idea. Albeit I used half of what was specified (2 T not 1/4 C). Bob's Red Mill medium grind was what I had on hand and it was just right for this.

Local farm corn is abundant and I've been on a corn soup makin' kick. This recipe was one of our recent favs for outcome made as written. With respect to those who used chicken broth or thought it needed same, I think the corn stock here is deliciously perfect on its' own. We prefer fresh thyme over basil as garnishing herb and use olive not canola oil. An easy recipe to cut in half if you want to do that but leftovers really good re-heated. I'm going to try freezing some next time.

comments.recipe_name: blistered green beans and tomatoes with honey harissa and mint
comments.comment: Perhaps the fav at my home for new-to-us green bean recipe this year. Made as written but for adding an 'eensie' more lime juice. Used avocado oil - my preference for high smoke point vegetable oil - in place of grapeseed. Also "know thy harissa" may be a good thought to keep in mind given they can vary so much in heat level. I used the spiciest of my 'stash' and it worked well. Pretty - looked exactly like photo. Good warm and also good room temp if you want to make ahead.

*Great recipe in particular for people like my husband who enjoy bisque textures but not the dairy usually in them. *Made this several times all with good outcome. As deviation from instructions, we prefer using immersion blender to puree soup BEFORE adding in chopped shrimp then proceed to add in shrimp & finish. *Sub Dungeness crab for shrimp & you'll have a dish similar to Scottish Partan Bree - delicious variation. Use crab shell for stock, crab meat in the bisque.

Made 2X per recipe for ingredients, proportions & technique. Splendid. If reduced by 1/3 all around will serve 3 ample or 2 uber-generous portions. Used ripe Heirloom tomatoes both times with no need to peel or seed when cut in small dice. Used carnaroli rice - my fav for risottos. Needed "skoosh" more broth (used parm broth) both times & "2 min. minus" more cook time to just right. Second time finished with soft butter not EVOO in step 4. Delicious either way - liked butter best.

Made dish per recipe. Delicious keeper. Notes: *Feta adds salt here & some za'atar mixes have salt in them. If yours does, be mindful when 'generously' salting onions - step 3. *Note step 7 instruction - don't over bake. Pliability when done is key. You don't want these to be crisp. * The fresh herbs to garnish are "it". * Our favorite temperature to eat these is just slightly warm.

comments.recipe_name: marinated feta with herbs and peppercorns
comments.comment: 1) Delicious made per recipe choosing preserved lemon option in the "or orange..." choice, including optional serrano & electing only black / green peppercorns in the "any combination of". 2) Used my preferred firm, brine packed Feta which is French in origin & sheep's milk based. 3) If oil is leftover when cheese is all eaten, it is good for crostini or with pasta or roasted veg or on salads. 4) Presented in a nice glass container, this makes an "ahh"some host(ess) gift.

comments.recipe_name: grilled corn and avocado salad with feta dressing
comments.comment: Made per recipe. Delicious outcome. Also as idea from this recipe, the feta, buttermilk, lemon zest and juice and garlic can be mashed and whisked at the bottom of a salad bowl as dressing. Then sturdy greens - like romaine or frisee - on top along with other fridge friendly salad items (sliced radish, onions, corn...) and put in fridge ahead of meal. When ready for salad course the dressing is ready to go in the bottom of the bowl to toss in. If using tomatoes toss them in last.

comments.recipe_name: pasta with zucchini feta and fried lemon
comments.comment: First try of this will be dinner this week where I need something to make in advance & eat room temp. Reading comments in advance & noting some found the lemon too bitter, see recommendation on 'how to avoid' via Melissa Clark's fantabulous recipe for "Pasta With Fried Lemons and Chile Flakes" on this site. Watch her VIDEO for sectioning lemons, slicing and blanching before frying them to avoid bitterness. I will try this recipe as written first time but offer 'heads up' for M. Clark's tips.

I would follow Samin anywhere and made this several times. My 'go to'. Making chicken stock for years but as usual, Samin makes me wiser - better cook and I now add vinegar. BUT... *I think this is better if you add only water and chicken to the stockpot, bring to a boil, lower to simmer and let it go about 20 min then skim 'gunk" and THEN add veg (and vinegar). *I find 4 hrs is plenty of time. Note: Saved fat that rises to top is great for potatoes or root veg cooking.

comments.recipe_name: rice pilaf with pumpkin currants and pine nuts
comments.comment: Entire dinner in which this recipe appeared will be our Thanksgiving this year. A note in advance for others regarding a very good source for excellent Turkish variety pine nuts at the on-line shop of Maureen Abood. I used these in multiple dishes and found them the best. https://maureenaboodmarket.com/collections/our-best-sellers/products/extra-large-pine-nuts I had pine-nut-mouth several years using the "bin brand" ones, mostly from China, and I'm now very choosy about pine nuts.

comments.recipe_name: spicy caramelized squash with lemon and hazelnuts
comments.comment: Simple and delicious. Made recipe as written but for swapping out the hot smoked paprika with regular (aka sweet) paprika and a little cayenne. Used butternut squash. Noting others mention their dish burned at the recommended temp and time for cooking, ours was just right in 45 minutes @ 425. We did turn it twice. The lemon zest, juice, nuts and flake salt are 'it' for finishing the dish. If hazelnuts aren't your thing, try almonds or pistachios or pepitas. Skin was edible texture.

comments.recipe_name: buttered stuffing with celery and leeks
comments.comment: Made recipe as written except I tore bread into 1" pieces not 1.5 - 2". Knowing bread pieces would 'grow' as they absorbed the liquid it seemed a better at end "eatin' size" Was a good choice for us. This is a delicious dish for more than Thanksgiving table to go with turkey. Lots of roasted meats or veg would partner with this. Or as we used it in our dry run...all by itself along with cranberry sauce and escarole salad it was a "just right" meal.

Made as written and cut in half using a 6.5" springform pan for 6 servings with great outcome for form and flavor. Delicious - just sweet enough but not too much. Much prefer this texture over traditional sweet potato pie. Made it day 1 through step 5, finished it day 2 and easy peasy. Serving issues were: a) removing from spring form pan bottom to serving platter a bit difficult, 2) needed to use sharp narrow knife (chose boning knife) to cleanly cut through marshmallow top for slicing.

comments.recipe_name: rice pilaf with pumpkin currants and pine nuts
comments.comment: Fan of Tanis' recipes and in particular rice, but think there may be an error in this that results in what some report as 'crunchy' rice, etc. That is if using Bomba or Arborio (or Caranoli which is Aborio-type) since rice to liquid ratio for those is 1 part to 3 parts (because they absorb more than a long grain like basmati) you need to use 1C rice here for best outcome. We used 1 C Carnaroli, the rest of recipe as written except reduced butter by half. 20 min cook + 15 rest = Perfect

Having leeks and Sugar Pie variety pumpkin leftover from Thanksgiving produce to use up, this recipe was inspiration for a "what will we have for dinner?" easy to pull together meal. Subbed the pumpkin for winter squash and since no fresh dill on hand used Italian parsley. Otherwise per recipe. Tasty outcome. Equally good not and just barely warm.

comments.recipe_name: one pot pasta with ricotta and lemon
comments.comment: For whatever reason I'm less inclined to use basil as winter pasta add in and it was early December when I made this lovely dish. So I used a few torn arugula leaves. But I think shredded radicchio or torn endive or any other sort of winter chicory would be an equally good choice. So would Italian parsley. Otherwise I made this per recipe (using cavatappi as pasta choice) and it was a really delicious dish using winter lemons as 'tis the season for citrus.

comments.recipe_name: thumbprints with dulce de leche nutella or jam
comments.comment: "Cookies will keep in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 1 week." Wait! What did we do wrong? The pecan with dulce filling disappeared in 24 hours. ;-) Excellent cookies!

comments.recipe_name: pork chops in cherry pepper sauce
comments.comment: Taking a page from Dan G's book when he comments that this dish is delicious and akin to Scarparello which he recommends everyone try, I agree if you enjoy this dish you will likely enjoy that one. And a recipe published on this site is one of the very best for Scarpariello I have ever had. See link here. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/11990-raos-chicken-scarpariello-shoemakers-chicken

Just right for us made per recipe with but one little tweak - used just one big pinch of cayenne and then added a few shakes of Tabasco. My single edit would be that I'm not sure you'll need the clam liquor if you're using the canned chopped clams. We found this came together for nicely dip-able texture without it. And as PS - if using canned clams invest in a really good onshore brand. This is easy to scale down by half if you're serving a smaller group.

comments.recipe_name: marinated giant white beans and beets
comments.comment: *Made bean portion of this looking for home cooked analog to the marinated large white beans found at antipasti bars at grocery chains (Whole Foods etc), most of which come from same mass distributor and use soybean oil (ugh). *Made twice - once with Royal Corona beans and then Judion beans, both soaked overnight before cooking. *Roasted the red bell pepper & used more of it. Subbed thyme for dill. Added splash wine vinegar. *Holds well for 3/4 days in fridge. Served room temp.

We loved Brussels sprouts since before doing so was cool and all the ingredients here are things we like so well as to almost always have them on hand. This combination sounded dee-luscious but fell short for us of being a "keeper". We made it per recipe but for our taste buds it just didn't click. If I zero in, it might have been that as much as we love coriander - seeds and ground - the flavor didn't work for us in this.

comments.recipe_name: passover inspired braised lamb with dried fruit
comments.comment: Cooking in the time of covid19 to find lamb in freezer and all other ingredients on hand so dinner could be made without a need for non-essential trip to market. Too early for Passover or Easter by a little but so delicious and comforting for a meal. The "ahhh...roma" as it cooked alone was enough to raise spirits. Made as written a day ahead to reheat. Chose fresh ginger. Apricots broke down a lot to add to 'saucey'. Would increase prunes next time - yummy. Used cilantro garnish.

Made per recipe. Very easy. Very delicious. Very much improves Ina Garten original - which I also used once. Notes: 1) The only rub is not specifying cauliflower head size. So "medium-ish". You'll know when stirring in the cauliflower to the ricotta mixture when to call 'enough'. (Roasted cauliflower not used here is great all other ways.) 2) Yes. You DO want to put the nutmeg in this. Absolutely. 3) Prefer ricotta in this and specifically Bel Gioso brand Ricotta Con Latte.

Our Farmers Market opened even in covid19 times thanks to creative and determined manager and nimble innovative crew of volunteers to pivot to order ahead and drive through to pick up local farmer and producer treasures. (Covid19 has broken some big supply chains but local ones continue to work.) Which is how I came have green garlic to make this excellent compound butter. To spread on artisan toasted local baker bread from the market . And eat with local hot house tomatoes. Ahhhh...some.

Green garlic season is fleeting. Perhaps that it arrives this year in the time of covid19 makes one all the more mindful of how uncertain things can be and wanting to cook while ingredients exist. So using this spring's green garlic, I made this soup twice. Once per recipe - used pastured chicken from farmers market. Once in vegan mode using Royal Corona beans (no joke intended - from Rancho Gordo) as the base so one has bean pot liquor not chicken broth. Both times very good. Keeper.

Printed page of this recipe was filed in my 'to try' folder for a year until I did that last night. Recipe now in 'keeper' binder. Super easy. Very tasty. Made nearly as written but for subbing spaghetti for udon. instead of cucumber used small dice of orange bells and celery. Covid19 shopping times meant using what was on hand and the cool, crispy veg were good partners here. Benefited from 90 min 'ish' sit at room temp before serving.

comments.recipe_name: macaroni salad with lemon and herbs
comments.comment: At its' delicious best when chilled for a couple to a few hours. And delicious it is. I was tempted to eat the entire dish all by myself. The 'peel the celery' instruction is a good one. I always do this for use in cold salads of any kind because it makes a difference - here too. I'll use grainy mustard next time and no caper brine. If you're absent buttermilk, I think sour cream could work fine to substitute. This is now in keep and often repeat file.

comments.recipe_name: the most adaptable one bowl cornmeal poundcake
comments.comment: Delightful recipe! Notes: 1) Per recommendations to reduce sugar, I did that to a scant 3/4C - perfect for us. 2) Otherwise, made per recipe - choosing orange zest & vanilla options - but with an addition of 1C diced tender rhubarb. Mixed it wtih 2T of the total sugar to let sit while preparing the rest of cake batter then gently stirred rhubarb into batter. Very good outcome for it staying suspended in batter. Delicious too. 3) Used 9" pan but 8" would also work. 4) 50'ish' min. bake.

comments.recipe_name: quick ragu with ricotta and lemon
comments.comment: *Ground lamb in place of beef + harissa in place of sambal olek. *Consider adding Merguez spices - fennel, cumin, coriander - when sauteing lamb. Or not. *Sub a more lamb and hot spice friendly wine - lower tannin, more fruit forward vino red or white - for Cab. Doesn't need to be spendy but something you would choose to drink with this. *Consider preserved lemon (peel only of course) to replace fresh lemon zest. *Otherwise per recipe. Delicous!

Every item in this recipe (absent curry leaves) is a staple in my kitchen but for the toor dal. I have red, black, green and brown lentils, green split peas, and umpteen kinds of beans but wouldn't ya know...no pigeon peas / yellow lentil aka toor dal. Rats. Rather than run out for 1 tsp I'm going to try red lentil as substitute since I thinks a little thickening is what the toor dal provides. They will cook faster than the toor dal, I know, but seem the best option of what I have on hand.

comments.recipe_name: neck bones pork neck and noodles
comments.comment: Our Farmers Market has 4 farmers who have pasture raised pork and it is processed by a small local FDA approved slaughterhouse. Every part of a pig is available to purchase. Including necks. If you have access to pasture raised pork I think you'll find there is a substantial flavor difference for all of it, including necks in a dish like this, to the big factory farmed and production slaughter house animals. I buy humanely husbanded animals for my conscience. But it benefits taste buds too.
Equally as delicious without the cheese if cooking for folks who can't eat dairy. Just as perfect for dinner as breakfast or snack. Thanks for this.

Came together in a snap. Outcome = tender, perfect texture scones. Notes: *Technique per recipe. Used gram weights not measures. *Put cold butter in freezer for @1hr prior to grating. *Used sour cream & whole milk per recipe. *Noticing sweetness comments of others, cut back sugar by @1/3rd. Glad I did. *Used fresh diced rhubarb for fruit. Macerated @ 20min w/2T of total sugar. *Cut 8 triangles not squares. *Omitted egg to brush top. Used milk + sprinkle demerara. sugar. * 19 min bake

Delicious meatballs & gravy. (Made w/o chickpeas.) Some say problems w/rice being fully cooked or dry meatballs. Our outcome great. What we did: *Lamb as meat. *White basmati rice rinsed several times 'til water is clear (how I always handle basmati b4 cooking). *Meatball ingredients & technique per recipe to combine. *But browned meatballs in braising pan on cooktop then defatted pan and added 'gravy', covered & low simmer for @30 min. *Didn't use saffron in gravy - felt it would be los

comments.recipe_name: spicy and saucy cherry tomato pasta
comments.comment: *A good use for cherry tomatoes at the market or on your kitchen counter which may be an "eensie" towards the downhill side of wanting to munch on them fresh or toss them in a salad. I usually oven roast those little gems at that point but this recipe offers a perfect alternative to enjoy them. *The little fried capers were an "it's it!" addition. *Depending on how saucy you like pasta you may want to consider 12 oz not 1 lb. *Any shaped pasta works but good with spaghetti too.

comments.recipe_name: sweet and sour cauliflower with golden raisins
comments.comment: *Made per recipe first time & this was just right agrodolce balance of sweet & tart for us. (Chose turmeric in the '...or saffron' option.) *Took a couple of extra min. to cook cauliflower step 2 per notes of others. But also note this is supposed to be just tender not soft. *Tasty warm or room temp. Appreciate it can be made ahead. *Second time subbed Farmers' Market 'new to me' veg Caraflex cabbage (cut @ 1.5" pieces) for cauliflower & also very good.

*Tender and delicious lamb made per recipe using both Maras pepper & pomegranate molasses shown as optional. Fuyu are a fav for us but out of season so omitted from garnish, otherwise as specified. *Made day ahead to Step 4 then separated meat from juices to cover & refrigerate both so fat could be easily lifted off. Day of service, rough sliced lamb, reheated in defatted juices (didn't need reducing). *Easy prep, needs little tending, aroma "ahh..." some, on 'keeper' list.

Roasted the tomatoes and finished them with parsley / cheese mixture per recipe. Very good. Did not make the from-scratch pasta. And while we have dried strozzapreti among the pasta "library" in our pantry it isn't one of our fav shapes. So we used dried spaghetti noodles cooked al dente, drained and tossed tomatoes in with them, marrying with a little pasta water too. Tasty. The tomato mixture would likely also be very good on crostini or bruschetta.

Brilliant idea to marinate the tomatoes a bit in the vinegar and olive oil. Color me embarrassed to not have thought of that before in decades of BLT making. This is excellent. (Without weighing in on the debate taking place here on order of assembly of ingredients between the uber crisp (definite good thing) toast because my hubby puts his together in a different order than do I.) But we also found half the ingredients were plenty to fill two sandwiches.

As my pantry is my witness, I keep juice-packed pineapple tidbits stored there just so I can make this kind of carrot salad. (Well...on occasion when making pizza I add them to peppers and onions on "a pie". But I will deny that in public.) I choose brown raisins here and yogurt over mayo. Seems plenty sweet to me without optional honey but your taste may vary. I prefer lemon zest to orange. And you'd be amazed what a difference fresh Farmer's Market carrots make grated for this.

*Delicious outcome. Made per recipe but for omitting 1/4 C fresh basil in step 4. (We prefer basil in alla Norma dishes added at end and not simmered in with mixture.) *Fresh tomatoes best but Petite Dice fire roasted (Muir Glen - Petite cut is key) @ 15 oz can drained works in pinch. *Ricotta salata best but Grana Padano or Parmesan work too. *Many eggplant dishes benefit from making ahead (ex: caponata) and this does too. *If you want agrodolce (sweet/sour) flavors look for caponata.

This recipe calls for a 'modified' or partially spatchcocked chicken given the backbone is cut only on one side and left on the bird. A fully spatchcocked chicken is even better to use. You can see Melissa Clark's great 'how to' video at the following link. I always do it this "full" way - easy to do and to cook - and save backbones in freezer for stock. https://www.nytimes.com/video/dining/100000001076482/spatchcocking-a-chicken.html

comments.recipe_name: herby feta and yogurt dip with sumac
comments.comment: *Made as written. Delicious flavors, good texture. *Full fat Greek yogurt (like Fage 5%) and quality feta (used my fav brine packed sheep's milk French brand) - not the pre crumbled stuff - are key. *Love sumac but next time I'll try it just on top as it does 'color up' the dip when stirred in. *Leftovers good on toast, to top oven roasted potatoes or sweet potatoes...

comments.recipe_name: summer shrimp scampi with tomatoes and corn
comments.comment: Made this absent the shrimp and it was a really delicious summer veg dish.

comments.recipe_name: seared scallops with jammy cherry tomatoes
comments.comment: Made absent the scallops. The jammy cherry tomatoes are a delicious partner with burrata or on bruschetta or as part of cheese / charcuterie / olives and crostini plate for a simple meal.

comments.recipe_name: roasted zucchini with garlicky bread crumbs and mozzarella
comments.comment: "You'll be dining on your own, sweetie." My comment to my hubby as he prepared this dish for dinner was because I love all veg except zucchini which it is not too big a stretch to say I loathe. But this preparation was great! I even had a second serving. He made it per the recipe but for omitting anchovies. And for step 1 he salted the zucch maybe 45 min. ahead and patted dry before putting under broiler (set on high). Tender veg, crispy crumb topping above melted cheese. Keeper!

Nice sized bunch of broccoli rabe for greens component was great partner for garlic, olive oil and German Butterball potatoes (from local farmer as 'tis the season here) in this mash and just harvested onions were allium choice. This recipe was originally created as a way to cook tasty meals from our pantries during the midst of pandemic lockdowns. It creates a good dish now too when things are a tad more opened up. Cooking keeps me sane. Thanks to Melissa Clark for her recipes for all times

comments.recipe_name: oven braised guinness beef stew with horseradish cream
comments.comment: Delicious outcome for this dish made largely per recipe but for the following: *didn't have Guinness or other stout so used Belgian Ale *since not using stout, omitted espresso powder *used water in place of beef broth & about 1/2C less than specified Added the veg about 45 minutes into cooking time so they wouldn't overcook. At 2 hr 15 min total cook time meat and veg were just right done. Sauce / gravy / broth is yummy - country bread served to sop up every drop a good idea.

comments.recipe_name: neck bones pork neck and noodles
comments.comment: Noting Ryan U's comment re this turning into a "pot of grease", it is true pork neck releases a LOT of fat when simmered. What works best for me in making neck stock is a ratio of about 7C water to 3lb "ish" bones. After 3 "ish" hours remove the bones. Strain, cool & refrigerate stock to let the big fat cap come to top. Lift it off, reheat and use stock for all kinds of things. (Ex: I use @ 2 parts neck stock + 1 part water for Mexican stewed beans & fat from cap to saute sofrito.)

Made this just as written - except for reducing 1/4C oil to 2T which works well - maybe as many as a dozen times since discovering the recipe here a couple of years ago and outcome has been just right every time. In the "either / or" of stock or water I always choose water. Enjoyed serving it with a pot of Mexican style stewed cranberry beans (see Milk Street recipe) tonight but the rice is delicious with so many things.

Made twice for tender and delicious meatballs. Mix is so tender that some tweaking on 2nd pass helped make them easier to handle and even more yummy (imho) in outcome. Notes: *Used all pork (hubby can't eat beef) *1 egg worked better than 2 *1/3C crumbs worked better than 1/4C *let ricotta-crumb panade mix rest 15 min in step 1 *once meat mix is together return to fridge for 30-60 min for easiest handling to form meatballs - makes all the difference *even better 2nd day *freeze well in sauce
Forgot to add to previous note: *One whole egg works as well as two yolks in our experience.

*Shells can be made & stuffed then cooled & put on a baking sheet or other flat tray in freezer & when frozen stored in zip loc bag until ready to use them - then put in dish with sauce to bake per recipe. *Made as written & also with blanched chopped chard in filling. *Sauce is simple & tasty - we increase by 50%. *Spare par-cooked & unstuffed shells can be cooled & frozen to use - we discovered they were easy to handle just barely thawed to fill then & bake.

comments.recipe_name: no bake lemon custards with strawberries
comments.comment: Neighbors who are really good cooks invited us to dinner for a splendid meal at every step. They ended with this indescribably perfect dessert. Made exactly per the recipe said the talented cook. I was tempted to lick the dish. So I offer up these comments not as the one doing the cooking but as one who had the delightful experience of eating this lemony, just sweet enough but not to much, just rich enough but not overly so delight. I plan to lick the dish when I make it at home!

comments.recipe_name: baked polenta with ricotta and parmesan
comments.comment: Excellent dish turned out exactly as expected based on recipe & photos. Paired with "sort of" version of Alison Roman's pork meatballs for easy to make ahead comfort meal to eat while watching 2020 election returns. Notes: *cut recipe in half and still served 4 for our purposes, *deviation from instructions - soaked polenta in water we cooked it in & given we weren't going to drain first, added a little extra (3Tish?) water to soak. *only took 10 min for last cook in step 3

comments.recipe_name: sourdough stuffing with kale and dates
comments.comment: I use chili de arbol frequently in cooking and have a bag of them in my pantry. In my experience they are small dried chiles and cannot be sliced as this recipe specifies. Does anyone know if there are fresh chili de arbol (bird's beak chili)?

comments.recipe_name: one pan one pot thanksgiving dinner
comments.comment: Brava to Melissa Clark for the culinary choreography work that went into creating this menu and testing out the recipes and timing for the meal. If you haven't seen the 11 minute-ish video that goes with this, I so recommend it. Melissa Clark is always so fun to watch cook. And her contributions through the Times also are rewarding to cook. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SNx5wYGnh0k&feature=emb_logo I'll try this sometime this winter. Minus bacon on the turkey I think. But fun concept!

Very good - made it twice - and a little change of pace for the umpteen batches of cranberry sauce I made during cranberry season. We prefer more tart cranberry sauce so only used about 10 Tbsp sugar not 1C. Omitted cayenne.

comments.recipe_name: turkey breast roulade with garlic and rosemary
comments.comment: Have made various turkey roulades several times & whether this recipe or others, I SO agree with cooks who have posted here about the importance of NOT rolling the skin in with the roulade but making sure the skin is kept on the outside. Otherwise you'll end up with flabby skin roasted in the final product. One of the best 'how to' videos for roulade turkey breast for technique is by J. Kenji Lopez-Alt. See steps 3 and 4. Can apply technique here. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mEofA0SvS1k

comments.recipe_name: buttered stuffing with celery and leeks
comments.comment: *Made for Thanksgiving last year, 2019 and it was delicous. In this crazy 2020 year I made it for two of us to enjoy as a pre-Thanksgiving meal (since that will be pasta this year...why not) and it was just as yummy and easy to cut in half. *To cut it in half - used 7 oz total of torn sourdough (weight after drying) and 1 lg. egg whisked in with 1 1/4 C stock. Proportions worked, next time I might use 2 sm eggs.

comments.recipe_name: buttered stuffing with celery and leeks
comments.comment: *In previous note about cutting this in half, should also have said I used a well buttered 8" square baking dish and fill was just right in that.

comments.recipe_name: herby bread and butter stuffing for two
comments.comment: A very good recipe for reliable and tasty outcome made as written. Also a very good base recipe for jumping off point if you want to experiment with different elements added or substituted. This would be a good one for 'first timers' to making dressing / stuffing to rely on for proportions and then easily doubled if serving four.

comments.recipe_name: baked polenta with ricotta and parmesan
comments.comment: Likely impossible to screw up this tasty dish. IMHO after making 5 times & once trying w/o trying to screw it up myself - from memory when to stir in ricotta & getting way wrong & once when relative misread instruction. Still delicious outcomes. As long as you soak the polenta first -see previous note as like others I find no need to drain it - this seems foolproof. Also found it works fine with half as much ricotta or a little less parmesan. Half recipe serves 4 for us.

comments.recipe_name: pumpkin soup with ancho and apple
comments.comment: *Keeper made per recipe! Next time will add another ancho. Used bit more sugar than specified. *Easy to scale up or down depending on pumpkin on hand. *Noting some used stick blender; don't think you'll texture right that way. Texture is key element of this soup. Use blender or food processor. *Sytrian (aka Austrian) pumpkin seeds (typically US grown) are better in a dish like this than bulk mass market seeds (typically China origin). See Nuts.com, Amazon or WF to source. I like OR growers.

Responded tongue in cheek to DT who said this makes a TON and recommended cutting it in half. He/She is right in that this DOES make a lot and because this is my first pass at it, I scaled it down to about 1/4 of the recipe. Easy to do. Now in lidded glass jar I'll keep it in the fridge, turn a couple of times a day per instructions, and open it to use for the associated lamb recipe on Christmas day with lots left for the associated cocktail. Aaahh-some aroma already.

Claire Saffitz is an excellent instructor and with that in mind I highly recommend investing the roughly 9 minutes needed to watch and listen to her instruction as she demos making these cookies. Great technique tips. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4nshlz1FL-E For those who mention the almond extract flavor competed too much with the pistachio, note Claire suggests using vanilla or lemon zest in the dough as alternative for those who don't like almond extract.

comments.recipe_name: brussels sprouts with pickled shallots and labneh
comments.comment: Loved it made as recipe was written. Wouldn't change a thing. Two of us polished this off as dinner because it was so tasty.
For bakers who have never made rugelach before, might be helpful to see Melissa Clark demo this delicious recipe by watching this youtube video. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=05XZfvGTufk

*Cut in half, used a 1.5 qt. (@12"L x 2"H) gratin dish & perfect fit. *As contrast to folks who say they'd add more tuna, we found proportions of tuna to noodles to veg to sauce just right. *Omitted mushrooms (not a fan of 'rooms & tuna textures together). Added 'some' small dice red bell pepper; tasty & festive too for Christmas season. *Omitted garlic & capers. *Used panko rubbed with a little EVOO and a little kosher salt instead of chips. 2/3C just right for half recipe.

comments.recipe_name: braised lamb with squash and brandied fruit
comments.comment: *Christmas dinner was "winner" per hubby eating this dish made per recipe for ingredients but with minor 'tweaks' of instruction. *Braised lamb day in advance. Separated lamb from sauce to refrigerate to let fat rise overnight. *Lamb perfect tender, removed from bone to reheat in sauce before serving. *Used tomatoes as in "tip" option. *Started brandied fruit about 2 weeks in advance of using for this dish. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1021676-brandied-dried-fruit

comments.recipe_name: lentil soup with smoked sausage and apples
comments.comment: *Wonderful flavors, easy to make, a keeper for rotation. Delicious day made, even better the next. *Made per recipe for ingredients but starting with 8 oz lentils and easy to scale proportionally. *Only change in technique was to chop celery in 1/4" not 1/2" and add in with the apples in step 1 instead of with garlic and herbs in step 2 as I wanted more time for celery to cook / soften. It was a good choice for us.

Totally tasty, easy and quick to make, healthy dish that reheats well too. Keeper. Made per recipe for ingredients and technique but cut in half to accommodate the remaining brown lentils in my pantry. Very easy to scale down for all ingredients and used 1 small canned chipotle which was just enough smoky heat zing and not too much. Be certain to toast and grind cumin seeds per recipe, using pre ground will not deliver the same flavor.

comments.recipe_name: crispy stuffed mushrooms with harissa and apricots
comments.comment: *These were excellent made per recipe. Keeper! *Noting some others say they had leftover filling, I found it just right amount to compactly fill mushrooms w/no extra. For context, I used cremini and 8 oz was 12 mushrooms. I wonder if different takes on what "1/2 small onion" is might be at root of too much fill? *Made through step 5 about 90 min in advance and held at room temperature before step 6. Worked well.

Good quality tahini is essential. Choose brands like Soom (Ethiopian sourced sesame seeds) or Al Wadi (Lebanese and pretty easy to find in well stocked grocers) or similar. Some other brands, like Once Again as someone mentions in a post, can use South American sesame seeds and are more bitter. (I have used that brand too for personal experience.) Also avoid Joyva, the ubiquitous one in can, for bitterness. Good tahini should not be over roasted. Should also be pourable at room temp.

All on the ingredients list are in my pantry and fridge but for the Pixian doubanjiang. Reading the ingredients in it, and realizing it will be a little different using a substitution, does anyone have familiarity with it to know if gochujang or sambal olek would work as I have both of those.

comments.recipe_name: lemony white bean soup with turkey and greens
comments.comment: *Truly tasty outcome of nutritious soup requiring very little time and some steps can be done in advance making this even easier to do. *We're not vegetarians but play at eating like them 80% of the time so eliminated turkey and subbed veg broth for chicken and delicious outcome. *Tuscan kale (aka Lacinato) as our sturdy greens choice. Parsley, mint, chives for herbs. *Made several hours ahead through step 4. Then reheated to finish through step 6. Worked well.

comments.recipe_name: brown butter lentil and sweet potato salad
comments.comment: *Remarkably good flavors from remarkably easy to make dish and 'bonus points' it used all staples in my kitchen for easy 'school night' meal. *Brown butter vinaigrette is genius. Liking higher acid dressings added a splash more vinegar and drizzle more maple syrup for balance. Otherwise, as written and the dressing is 'it' in this dish. *We chose sweet potatoes (only needed 1T oil to cook) among 'or carrots or butternut' options. *This fed 3 for us. Dish is at its' best warm.

Have made this as written several times in the last year or so --- hot chocolate helps make sticking to home base as part of pandemic recommendations go down --- and this is a delightful libation. We like the combo of creamy almond butter and rice milk for the "either / or" alternatives and we do use the optional sugar. Sometimes also a pinch of ground chili powder or cinnamon too. We aren't vegan so make dairy based hot chocolate too. But this is a good one as well.

Excellent outcome making recipe as written. Has been a while since I worked with phyllo but fun to for creating the 'cigar' shape. Since phyllo is something we don't often use and since the lamb and cheese fillings were so delicious in this combo we're starting to experiment with using them on flatbreads, inside pita, as savory bun fillings and so on.

comments.recipe_name: hot italian sausage and broccoli rabe frittata
comments.comment: In previous 'high five' note for this remarkable dish I said it was my "take to a desert island" recipe. That was in 'before days' and now it has been 'pandemic days favorite' too. We haven't been out to eat since 3/16/20 and it is 2/02/21 as I post this and our cooking at home audience is now 2 people. Made this many times in easy to scale down to half size - so 4 portions means 2 night's dinners for 2 - by using 8" sq baking dish to assemble ingredients.

Samin leads in the article, in which this recipe is discussed, by saying it is her last column for the magazine. I will miss reading her columns as I hear her voice as I read. She has taught me much and I am an old...umm...well seasoned...passionate home cook. I also do not 'get' Instant Pots. Thanks for making me not the only person to not use one. And her technique here for refried beans leads to luscious outcome. I eat beans like this for breakfast at times. Truth.

A great recipe for birria, I made it with lamb (using this time osso bucco cross cut shanks from local rancher) which is always my favorite protein for birria. The grated fresh ginger in this was a new to me element in birria recipe and it was a good add. I like to make day ahead and refrigerate so I can remove some of the fat at top then reheat to use. In addition to tortillas you may want a spoon at the table to get every drop of spicy broth from the bowl.

comments.recipe_name: goat cheese and fig quick bread
comments.comment: Excellent! Made as written except increased figs from 4 to 7 based on comments of others who said they might double figs next time. That choice first time was right for our taste buds. Used dried Mission Figs (dark skinned) - we always have them. Baked up exactly as expected and using 9" loaf pan took 34 min to perfect then removed from pan to cool per recipe. Some commented this was dry; I wonder if they overbaked? Great w/tea or wine or a Manhattan cocktail before dinner...or as dinner.

Sue Li is adorable and she finds watching Niles and Frasier Crane relaxing. Two more reasons to enjoy her 'company' on this NYT Cooking video demo for her delicious galette. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RDi9sCR7Uxk Note at the end the garnish touch she does with the melted cheese off the parchment. Very nice. From my experience the onions benefit from longer time to caramelize. And letting rest for 30 min before cutting is best too. So good with Alsatian Pinot Blanc or Belgian Ale.

comments.recipe_name: roasted tomato and white bean stew
comments.comment: Where beans share starring role with fresh cherry tomatoes, it makes sense to cook beans from scratch if you can. It makes such a flavor difference, imho. This recipe is fun with giant butter beans too, like Royal Corona or Judion. Cooking a pot of beans then dividing and freezing them in portions equivalent to canned beans makes having them on hand for recipes easy. And if you tuck some parmesan rinds in with the beans as they cook then really good in this dish.

We enjoyed this simple, comforting, nutritious soup made very close to as written and reheated to eat the day after it was cooked. For tomatoes I used a 14 oz can of Mutti brand canned cherry tomatoes. Substituted thyme for rosemary. Lots of black pepper and grated parmesan to finish. Keeper for us.

comments.recipe_name: steamed artichokes with vinaigrette dipping sauce
comments.comment: Made just as recipe was written and outcome just perfectly delicious.
Made as written except for subbing blood orange vinegar for Champagne / white wine vinegar. The recipe was a hit for flavor and pretty presentation both.

comments.recipe_name: game hens with sumac pomegranate and cardamom rice
comments.comment: 1) Made with ingredients as written. So good for rice and poultry. 2) Re "Place seasoned birds in a roasting pan...drizzle all over with pomegranate molasses. Turn birds breast-side down and add water to pan to a depth of ½ inch." I placed poussin on rack just above water not in it. 3) Re "... baste birds with pan juices frequently, until well browned." took longer than recipe specified to brown. 4) The rice is a keeper for lots of dishes.

Made as written x 2 for 2 and we both enjoyed this to be very happy we tried it - festive looking too in the lowball glass - but not enough to add it to the 'keeper' cocktail file at home. To put our opinion in context I'll share that hubby and I both enjoy bitter flavors, are fans of gin, he is a fan of Amaro and related bitter liquors, me not so much, I am a fan of all sour style cocktails, him not as much, and we both love grapefruit.

comments.recipe_name: kukul mas maluwa sri lankan chicken curry
comments.comment: Kay, you ask where one gets pandan leaves in US. Like fresh curry leaves, also more difficult to source, you can find them in Indian foods markets if you have one near you. Also found in SE Asian foods markets as they are used in Vietnamese and Thai cooking. Also via Amazon in dry form. See sources like Spruce Eats for description of how to refresh dry leaves to use in recipes like this. Pandan also comes in powder (even Kroger's owned Fred Meyer has that) but powder can't be used in this.

Re 3:1 ratio of honey to water to make the honey syrup for this yummmm....y libation, Bill asks "Every recipe for honey syrup that I have seen calls for one to one ratio of honey to water...Why the difference in this recipe?" I guess my answer is "Because this is a different recipe." It is a good one. Honey a little more prominent at 3:1 than 2:1, flavor just a tad different using a darker honey v lighter one of course. Endless possibilities for honey and bourbon type.

comments.recipe_name: brothy cod with peas and mushrooms
comments.comment: Easy to make as recipe is written and delicious to eat. Also really good with fregola substituted for couscous - fregola and seafood are a classic partnership - and halibut in place of cod. Keeper.

We enjoy making this with merguez (spicy lamb sausage) and given the seasonings in merguez (more North African flavor profile) we find fontina not quite the right option for the meltable cheese here using merguez...although it is a great option with Italian sausage in this. For the meltable nature and similar texture consider mozzarella. Also a good feta crumbled over can replace the grated parmesan when using merguez.

Gochugaru and kimchi are staples in our kitchen. Not so for green plum syrup. Not certain I wanted to source it give recipe takes only 1 TBSP (although we have a Korean market in town) I read a couple of food blogs by Korean cooks to see what might be substituted. Both similar to the info in the one linked here: https://kimchimari.com/green-plum-syrup-%EB%A7%A4%EC%8B%A4%EC%B2%AD-maesil-chung/

comments.recipe_name: mezzelune pasta with peas and shiitake mushrooms
comments.comment: Making pasta dough from scratch is above my functional set (a disabled cook with balance problems due to MS) I opted to use top quality spinach - ricotta filled fresh ravioli made by an Italian couple and sold at Farmer's Market as starting point. Then continued recipe as written for ingredients and steps 5 - 7. I did add a little lemon zest to the pan with the veg / sauce since the ravioli didn't contain it. Delicious, even with purchased ravioli replacing the mezzelune.

comments.recipe_name: roasted sausages with grapes and onions
comments.comment: This is great as written using Italian pork sausages and really good using merguez, a spicy lamb sausage. We like fennel seed when using pork sausages and cumin and/or coriander as the crushed spice options when using merguez. We have used red grapes and black ones. Always a good sherry vinegar to finish.

comments.recipe_name: gungjung tteokbokki korean royal court rice cakes
comments.comment: We are fans of Korean food flavors in our house and have a local Korean market from which we can source rice cakes. But as much as we enjoy Korean dishes and as good as are the flavors in this dish, we've tried rice cakes a couple of times now, both cooked by us at home like this and as cooked by a local skilled chef, and we just can't appreciate the texture of them. Different strokes. You really can't make this dish absent rice cakes. They are the key ingredient.
Easy and satisfying. Just the right amount of sweetness. This is the first yeast bread I have made in years. And I'm glad I held out for this classic.
Very tasty. Easy to make and hold at room temperature. Totally outside my comfort zone but I had the bacon, for some reason, and I have to admit this was a good way to use it. It was devoured.
Easy, wonderful with all the fresh herbs in the garden, bursting with flavor. Wonderful side to a rack of lamb.

Delicious appetizer using day-old Sullivan Street bakery bread (if only I could get a loaf delivered every day with my NYT) and my own green garlic and chives. I made the compound butter a few hours in advance and kept refrigerated. I browned the bread in the broiler about 15 minutes before company arrived and slathered it with the cold butter. When the doorbell rang I put the toasts under the broiler for a minute. Delicious way to start a dinner.
A new way to make broccoli (always a good thing). Peeled the stalks (can't help it) from local product, put in a bit of low-salt soy and cheap balsamic, and then grilled. Ate like popcorn.

comments.recipe_name: marcella hazans roast chicken with lemons
comments.comment: Perfect as written.

I've made some variation of this many times since Costco carries black rice. I use use under a pound of mixed mushrooms with a cup of rice and no egg. Sometimes I add the tomato paste. It is a delicious, unusual dish, now in my repertoire.

Sour cherries appear in farmers' markets for a week or two every year. So I grabbed some yesterday and baked this pie, which I have wanted to make since the recipe appeared originally. As usual, my attempts at a food-processor crust failed so the bottom crust stuck to the pie pan. The filling, enriched with some ancient Cherry Heering, was delicious as was the biscuit-shaped crusts on top. Will attempt again next year.

Absolutely wonderful. Creamy and so flavorful. I only used 1 cup of rice and a very small fennel and a medium onion. I used water instead of vegetable broth but added a tsp or so of La Boite's Lula to it. When the fennel cooks this long you don't taste the fennel; it becomes something mysterious with a hint of sweetness and a complexity that I can't describe. Served with a roast chicken.
